author: @jeremycatches9766
text:
For people with a BKN amputation this type of leg is overkill and the comfort loss is not worth the benefits of this style.   These silicone socks are torturous and a sweaty mess when you wear them for just a couple hours.

I wear a leg with a foam insert and a sock.  I can take it on or off in 2 seconds and i can wear it all day without getting uncomfortable and stinky/sweaty.  

The problem is that you cant hardly find a prosthetist that has the skills anymore.  The ones nowadays just take measurements and send it off to be manufactured somewhere else so the try to set every client the same.  

So if you are new to being an amputee you wont even know the old style leg exists.

I should reiterate the silicone sock style is great for people with no knee left and it is difficult for the leg to stay on.  But if you have a knee the leg with lock on without weird silicone socks or belts.

author: @melopuss375
text:
as much sense as your point makes, there is a practical reason for those to be there.

the soviet union once did a funny little thing, as they do, where they stopped branding their products entirely. the result of that was wild quality differences between seemingly identical products, the manufacturers of which were hard to hold accountable. also made repair and part replacement a nightmare and obliterate consumer trust of everything.

put a sticker on it, cover it with clothes, do what you gotta do to feel dignified because you do have a point, but understand that this is as much marketing as it is an obligation.

My brother lost his leg same as yours ,he was 23 years old ,had a Wife ,3 Children and never went on Disability,this was in the 70s . No insurance paid for his prosthetic leg or socks. His wife worked to pay for everything and bills and food. 
He started working after 8 months as a mechanic with cars. He worked through the fathom pains and the blisters ,and never complained. Your lucky to have a fancy one. Good Luck.
My Brother has passed away now at 76 , he worked right up till he passed. 🕊️🌹🇺🇸

author: @lumity238

author: @lisamurrell1337
text: I agree with your response…. I work as an OA at a Hanger Clinic….. until your in that would you may not get it.  The number of calls that come in weekly for needing new sleeves.. hot spots from rubbing, warranties on some of the prosthetic components, weight loss, change in your physical activity and the list is ongoing. It is a very rewarding job. The level of you relationships with your patient is incredible . I can’t say enough about how happy it makes me to watch a patients progression. Thank you for sharing your experience.

author: @wildmntflower
text:
What kind of person thinks it would be appropriate to be judgemental towards her in that situation? I'm sure if we put most of those commenters in a similarly risky and stressful situation, they wouldn't handle whatever their equivalent situation is any better than she did. 

Her prosthetic is more expensive than many cars, is mechanically complex, and having it stuck was a health and safety concern. Of course she wanted to error on the side of caution, because she's not an overconfident moron. 

Blindly fiddling with things is not always the answer. Ask mechanics about some of the problems they've seen in cars due to pure overconfidence and stupidity. There's no shame in making sure you're not going to damage important equipment.

author: @SonsOfLorgar
text:
As a former maintenance tech with ADD, I can say that while I agree with your precautious choise of seeking manufacturers council before trying to tinker yourself.
I would most likely have tried to tinker anyways in the same situation, or even tried to examine the mechanisms of the prosthetic as soon as I got home with it after the final test fitting session 😅
That said, I know enough about machinery to say that if examining any kind of device without an open manual for it in arms reach, never apply preassure on anything that could possibly bend or break if I encounter resistance. Especially when pulling, pushing or twisting at something sticking out in dissassembly or pushing, pulling and/or twisting during reassembly, because most likely, the resistance is caused by misalignment or forgetting a step/part that goes inbetween what I tried to reassemble, and forcing it too far will break or bend something beyond my capability to fix.
Wiggling is superior to forcing, every single time.

author: @ninjabgwriter
text:
This doesn't even remotely compare since it's not... a *replacement body part* and necessary for daily functioning, but I was gifted a few really old sewing machines from someone who knew I wanted to learn but couldn't remotely afford the equipment on my own. Even though I could see several moving parts and intuit I could do something to them, I didn't know how to use the equipment and didn't feel comfortable fiddling with anything until I had read the manuals on what those parts did and how to use them properly. Not because I couldn't figure it out with common sense, but because it's so easy to break something if you don't know exactly what it's supposed to do. If that something is a thing you got for $10 off of Amazon and is easily replaceable, that risk isn't really a problem. But if it's something you can't remotely afford to replace or repair (especially if replacement parts might not even be made anymore), you darn well aren't going to try to force something and just hope it doesn't break.

Another example: I don't think it's quite the same level as a prosthetic limb (for me at least, since I have one ear that hears fairly well, so I could hear at all if this happened), but I do use a hearing aid. There's a certain amount of 'oh CRUD' that comes when something you rely on like one of your own body parts just breaks. Panic, trying to figure out if you can replace it if it's hosed (most insurance companies do not see things like hearing aids or CIs or prosthesis or motorized mobility aids or such as things necessary for functioning but on the level of priority to someone's health as a medically unnecessary plastic surgery, some don't cover them at all or only cover them partially and so you must pay sometimes prohibitively expensive amounts of money, and are very strict about when they will cover you getting replacement parts and what parts they will cover), thinking ahead and calculating how difficult things you have planned for both necessity like a job, or leisure and fun and just *living* will still be possible... It's very scary. But for me when stuff like that happens, the panic is mixed with this cold dread that just freezes me and forces me to slow down, because I know that in a crisis, sometimes nobody else is going to come and help me, so I have to collect myself and proceed with utmost caution and not lose my head - though admittedly that has happened more with serious injuries than my hearing aid for me personally. But for a family member I have who is deaf without hearing aids and has a CI, I've seen that same freaking out when something goes wrong, because their job and ability to socialize and pretty much everything they do relies on being able to hear. And a friend of mine could not afford even arm crutches when they found they would need them for basic mobility - it was only the kindness of someone from our friend group *in another country* ordering them crutches secretly as a surprise that made it possible for them to have that aid which allows them to function... at a fraction of the ease of someone who's well, and still with pain and difficulty just from existing.

I guess the point I'm getting at is trying to help others who might not understand how it feels, to have some empathy. It isn't just equipment, it's almost like a part of your body, and there is such a huge pay wall between the people who direly need them, and basic functions that the majority of people have simply by virtue of being alive. If something was stuck around your biological leg and you were genuinely worried you'd break your leg possibly irreparably if you got it off wrong, you'd absolutely proceed with caution and ask someone who's an expert on what's stuck on your leg for help. Especially if one wrong move could put you out of commission for months and lead to really expensive hospital bills just to get your leg to work again, or just have to go without walking.

I hope none of this came off as aggressive or bitter towards anyone. I don't hold ire towards *anyone* for being healthy, I think everyone deserves to be as healthy as they possibly can. But learning empathy and compassion for people with different needs can help possibly anyone you meet. Adding extra time between seeing someone struggling, and some kind of value judgment or judgment of their competency or intelligence or whatnot, opens more room for connection and compassion rather than division and kicking someone who's already down. Even if it's in small ways, like a comment that might not have been meant to cut as deep as it does simply because someone didn't think a few moments longer about what they were saying and how it would affect someone else. It's hard enough not to internalize stuff like feeling bad about yourself for struggling, even when you know it's not your fault.

author: @adaliawright6891

Can I ask something really stupid?


Why are they so expensive?

Like, I've seen some prosthetics that have lights, sensors, bells and whistles galore, but from what I've seen of yours (which admittedly is very little) it kind of just looks... leg shaped. 

Like, there's probably a hinge-joint thing at the ankle, maybe there's something flexible about the foot, but other then that... I mean, I could see it getting much more complicated if your amputation is above the knee, but I don't think yours is....?

Anyways, I guess I'm saying that it kind of seems like a fancy peg leg. I feel like (if I knew how to carve) I could take a log and carve it into something leg-shaped enough to work relatively well. Then just put some sort of thing in for the ankle, and boom! Prosthetic leg!

I'm sure it's much more complicated than that, I'm just not sure why ir would be.

author: @KilltheElephantintheroom
text:
I don't get to see every podcast, Jo.
But what I've seen is huge in your approach to disability. 
My brother suffered for years with Forrest Gump braces.
My brother suffered from Cerebral Palsy. He was harrassed, even in a special needs school, he flat out quit.
We worked with my dad. We were pool excavators.
At 13-14I was driving a bobtail dump in the streets of Los Angeles. When I left home. My BROTHER, a palsied person took over.
I came back for family stuff.
I had the great pleasure of seeing my brother load a dump truck, just like every other equipment operator. 
You're an inspiration Jo.
And just know...people like me get crazy happy when we see a physical victory.
You're needed in your issue.
But only to show what youve endured, and what you've dealt with in frustration, you're more that big bright spot in the morning.
What a Doll!!
Just acknowledgement Gurl. In multiples❤❤

author: @docwil2541
text:
You'll love a pin lock over a vacuum socket. You don't have to worry about leaks and losing suction. 

Go with a total contact socket. You get better weight distribution. 

The ability to lock and run is the best.

I'm going through fitting on my newest socket. RBKA 2000 So I've been through just a few legs.

Doc Wil
Former Team Össur
Amputee Firefighter EMT

author: @skylarjay3513
text:
I remember having a pin lock for about 12 years. While it was nice to easily take off and put on my leg, the weight thing at the time caused me huge issues (there's a bundle of sensitive nerves at the tip of my stump that was always the victim of the pin), not to mention the leg would twist whenever I slipped, and it dislocated my knee a lot (knee issues are greaaaat..).
Currently I'm using a standard suction, but because of how small my stump is it has a hard time staying on. But so far it's the type I love best, but I've been concidering trying something else because when the suction doesn't work (most of the time) it starts to really bother my hip and thigh, and it's probably not good for my scoliosis lol.
Anyway, I'm glad the pin lock is working well for you :) maybe I'll give it another shot myself now that some of my previous issues have been reduced
(Oh gosh sorry for info dumping lol)

author: @lincolnpascual
text:
GET A DERMA ROLLER (the wheel thing that has the really tiny needles? THAT). Yeah, I know we are told not to shave and what not because micro abrasions and possibility of infection, but if you use the roller at night before sleep, and wash the liner daily, you should be good to go. I've even actually used a liner straight through for 48 hours without problem (removing once ever 6 hours for 15 minutes to let the skin breathe).

The derma roller stimulates the healing response in your skin and tricks the skin to grow thicker. It'll make wearing the pin lock system A LOT MORE BEARABLE.

I've been using the pin lock system for about a year, and IMO it's the second best next to getting an osseointergration surgery (I'm working on that one... my buddy lost his arm overseas, got the surgery. He wears his arm all day every day). I use the pin lock system for my leg, and I can do muay thai and firearm training as well as I used to before losing the leg. 

The only downside is that the liner I have (Willowwood Alpha Classic) is simply not durable enough to deal with the abuse I put it through. I have 4 on standby at all times (not a cheap endeavor) that insurance didn't pay for, simply so that I can rest assured I'll be ready when one wears through.

author: @AnnoyingNewsletters
text:
When worked as a Ride Host at Cedar Point amusement park Summer of 2007, I was loading people onto Shot side (red)* of Power Tower, securing harnesses, asking them to remove visible loose items to set on the tray until they get back down, etc., and making banter.

Someone handed me their sunglasses, I spun around to put them on the shelf, and there was a leg on it. 🦿 I know I must've at least froze for a moment, if not outright jumped. Yes, a leg gave me a jump scare. 😅 

It was late October and *_finally_* the weather had turned guests attire from multi layer pastel tank tops and ugly shorts (pastel yellow with the other pastels in a pattern) to From Russia With Love. The only thing that didn't change were the oversized sunglasses. 😎

So everyone had gotten on the ride with jeans and the appearance of two legs, so it caught me off guard, especially since it took up the whole shelf.

Once I'd cleared and launched them, the control tower calls me on our station phone ☎️  _Is that a leg?_ Yup. 😅

*Shot side (red) shoots riders 240 feet in the air, and then slowly lowers them. 

Drop side (aqua) raises riders 240 feet in the air, drops them about 2/3rds of the way, yo-yo's them back up part way (and it's at this moment that the most phones, that they weren't supposed to bring on the ride in the first place, go flying out of pockets ((Summer 2007 it was Motorola Razr))), and then slowly lowers them.

P.s. Our rider numbers for that summer were lower than usual because on a similar kind of vertical ride at Kentucky (iirc) Six Flags, a cable snapped and made some girl/woman (I don't know her age) into a double amputee, taking at least both her feet. 😢 [I'm going to go look it up.]

P.p.s. I had a rider come up the exit ramp for special loading due to disability. He was a double amputee at the knees. He'd already ridden Raptor, and our harnesses were a similar over the shoulder, presses to the thighs, design, so good to go!

🤔💭  _Right after he left the ride afterwards, I thought, _I wonder if he bought any Raptor merch that said, I kicked the sky, for the irony factor._ 😅😂

author: @willowsverge
text:
My dad had one like that! I dont know if it will help or not… he was told to keep seated, put both hands on his knee and press down a couple times until it fully clicks in place. They told him it was to make certain it was totally engaged before standing and off balancing  because of “missing” a click. 
He used to take his leg off and use it for a pop can holder/side table. If you put your hat over it, the bees and flys keep out of it. His was a full leg look. With the hat over it, folks going up the hill past our house saw an old guy, a dog and a leg, sock and shoe with a hat on it. Double and triple takes were normal! 😂😂😂 Take care and keep going!😊

author: @jacobmitchell7402
text: I don’t know why, but I got a sudden image of you going to the mall or store or something like that, taking a seat and just taking your foot off and with several people’s mixed reactions. I’d probably laugh, not at misfortune of being an amputee, but that certain situation already reminds me of a good friend of mine, Jacob. Not me, an actual friend named Jacob. Anyway, Jacob has a glass eye, and a bit after the time it happened, he went to school and at one point he told his teacher, who had no idea of his situation, that his eye felt funny. Being the good teacher she was, she said the usual line of, “ let me see”. And so, my buddy, Jacob, plopped his glass eye into the teacher’s hand. I’m not sure what happened after that, but I can guess, and I know that was a phone call home

author: @DrJoshuaPerry
text: Please try the Ottobock negative vacuum system, it’s the only one that has been comfortable for me and not resulted in any skin irritation or pinch points with fantastic proprioception.  It has the same carbon fiber socket as the pin system, but uses an inner and outer liner that form a vacuum around the limb and socket with a small pump in the toe keeping everything nice and tight with no need to use any socks.  It has been life changing for me, no more skin irritation or nerve spikes (except when I get a viral infection like a flu, but that’s different, right?), and the liners last me a year without wearing out and slipping off.

author: @FireOak222
text: My dad had his pointer fingers cut off in a table saw accident before I was born. Us kids never thought anything of it until someone would ask about it, like, kinda in hushed speak.  Dad would show kids his stubs and let them touch them and explained patiently how it happened.  It actually opened the door for us to right out ask other amputees what happened and how. No one we ever talked to got upset about us asking.  And, it felt odd, to us, to hold hands with someone who had all of their fingers. Dad retired an EMT/Paramedic/Captain of fire department. You can do anything, if you want to.

author: @hannahsarthub
text: I actually asked for a solid white leg, and I basically zentangled all over it with oil based acrylic paint pens.  The prosthetist who was working with me at the time said he had been working for 30 years, and had never seen anyone do that to an artificial limb.  When I inevitably replace this one, I’ll have a blank canvas to start again.  Next time I’m going to find a better way to preserve the design, as I’ve had to repair the spots that get the most wear and tear.  The gray liner is pretty ugly though.  That’s a great trick.

author: @ampisbadatthis
text:
the fact that its branded and that its so easy to cover up the brand leaves a very important and incredibly dystopian/late stage capitalist question. 

if a prosthetic limb is copyrighted and you appear on a commercial or show or pretty much any public appearance for profit with it being visible, covered or not, could the company that owns the patent sue and would it be for covering the logo or for using the logo design? if they can sue, that's nightmarish that they can basically copyright people, but if they can't, what does that mean for people using other copyrighted designs for products or logos and not compensating the designer/company?

I'm hoping that its not possible to copyright these sorts of things and that the logo owner wouldn't sue over whats basically a free ad for them, but you never know with how much capitalism has become a parody of itself in the last century

author: @peterjohn64
text: As a prosthetic teck in the past and see the complications of a socket. The constant adjustments,  sores etc..I recomend a titanium rod implanted in the bone , negating any need for a socket. Air can get to it instead of a continuous germ factory. I've seen them and they are modular and snap I to place not unlike a socket. They want repeat buisiness. You dont..time, the one thing they don't make more of..Not to mention the wonder of 3D printing that can make a mirror reproduction of your existing limb..feel free to ask questions if you like..PTB sockets are pretty taxing on your knee joint..

author: @margodphd
text:
Ooh,hear me out - customisable length and width, print on demand prosthetic covers? You could make ones like so, or ones that reach the ankle. You could have coordinating pairs or contrasting pairs with the other limb, differing heights and prints. Ooor you could make a set of socks and easily removable, embellished covers for the carbon fibre part?
I kind of want to make an evening version. I just... don't know how to approach someone with such limb without sounding like a fetishistic creep, you know? "Hey,can I embellish your leg? And take pictures?"

Is there a place where you can buy or borrow broken or replica prosthetic, making sure you're not taking it from someone who could still use some parts of the prosthetic?  Or a sort of "model" of how usually the part that goes into the prosthetic (the pin on the "meaty" side) is shaped?

There should be more options! Us disabled folk want to look cute, too!

author: @tanya5322
text:
My first thought when I read the headline was that you were reacting to comments from people who thought you needed to do something to change your appearance to suit their weird needs about you… and I was expecting a big “ef off with that noise”
Which I would totally understand and support. 

Then I watched and realized it was *you*  who wanted make your prosthetic and peripherals cuter… *for yourself*
And again, I totally understand and support!
Bonus points for sharing your creativity with others who might be in similar circumstances ❤

Not to mention giving us ordinary people something to think about and expand our understanding 👍🏽

author: @ChronicHomo
text: I don’t know for sure but the logo looks like a direct to film transfer. It can be removed by heating the area to around 350 F with an iron and some parchment paper (make sure to test on something less important so you don’t accidentally ruin the fabric) and peeling it off while it’s still hot.

author: @Yelhsawo
text: I wish I’d have seen this or heard about it years ago. My mom had emphysema and started using oxygen in her early 40s, so she always had to carry a portable tank around with her. She would have been tickled to death if I knitted or crocheted her something cute to put the tank in. My mom passed almost three years ago so I can’t (even though I really wish I could) but I really hope this tip helps inspire someone else❤

author: @ToastontheEastCoast
text: The lacy sock looks so cute!  Being able to do something to personalize to your taste is way under rated but so needed.  My first pair of hearing aids were skin toned and not my choice, it was assumed that I'd want them to blend in and be unnoticed.  Nope, this time around I went with pewter gray, even though my hair is blond, and I would have gone with a vibrant purple if that had been an option.  I'm not ashamed of my hearing aids and I wear them everyday, just like my glasses, and it would be nice to have better options for personalization, but that seems to be reserved for kids.

author: @Near_the_Bear
text:
My mom had a mastectomy due to breast cancer and of course people knew she was having surgery and why because she was going to have a long recovery. When she finally got back to attending church a freaking DOCTOR in their congregation just full on had a conversation with her chest while asking how she was doing, stared the whole time. I don't think he was intending to be creepy; I think he was probably wondering what the extent of her surgery was (she had reconstruction at the same time so she didn't look any different), and his brain just shorted out. But still. 

Fortunately my mom has a good sense of humor and mostly thought it was funny.

author: @randyhohnstein8494
text:
Public transportation was ALWAYS  . . . Well there was always one that, in the morning got the story of either the shark attack or being torn from the back of an elephant by a tiger whilst on safari. 
But by the afternoon (I too deal with chronic pain issues) as soon as someone would start up with "Can I ask  . . ."  my immediate response was "If I can ask what happened to your teeth" or "You weren't born with that eyebrow, can I ASK" .
I've been an LBK amputee since 1985 and have been stared at, criticized, pitied and complimented as well. Sometimes, you are juuust full ! But I'm always kind to inquisitive children. The sneering, raised upper lip "Eeewww" kids, not so much. 
It's taken me a few decades to completely realize that this is MY baggage and will carry it whichever way I choose. At times, perhaps a little rougher. Depending on the circumstances.

author: @vaughnslavin9784
text: I wear, literally, a  BK peg leg. The bottom is small and round about the 4" across. It works well for working in the woods. When I first lost my leg 38 years ago, I had a list of responses depending on who was asking and how were they asking. They ranged from, I was in a bar fight and I won, to, I was wrestling an alligator, etc. Little kids are always curious, much to the dismay of their parents. I would show them my fake leg and then tell them that a big truck hit me and they should be really careful and watch out. My leg was crushed at a lumber mill when a piece of machinery shoved a unit of lumber into me and the wall behind me. I was young and anxious to get back to normal. I often wonder if I would have accomplished the things I have if God hadn't allowed the accident to happen. Well Jo, you got the spunk! Get er dun!

author: @lissakterra6467
text: Yikes.  When I was still reliant on my chair, I regularly had people staring and asking dumb/invasive questions and it was annoying.  Not quite as annoying as the people who decided that for some reason this meant the chair and my body were public property (literally had someone lean on the back of my chair and rest their arm and drink on my head once, like WTF?!), but kids were generally easier.  They might ask invasive questions but were usually more tolerant and understanding and would move on quicker with less awkward silences and averted eyes.  When my mom finally had the surgery to take her leg off (atk) she was still running her in home daycare and the kids were easier to handle than the parents.

text: Jo, the only thing I can think is that the guy couldn't wrap his mind around you dancing.  Prosthetics have gotten so much better and compared to the 80's where it appeared that they were mainly about appearing like the missing part today's prosthetics seem like they're much more about functionality over how they look.  In my career discussing prosthetics is treated like any other health issues, usually it's about the best places to call for help if your prosthetic leg goes out during a hike.  Now days I fight fire , a fire fighter I work with on occasion has a prosthetic leg and a prosthetic arm, just a little extra awareness towards him just the same as any other firefighter with a medical condition.  Great guy, very solid FF.  Always happy to have him on scene.

author: @oppaloopa3698
text:
I really don’t think it’s ok to ask if you’re an adult. It’s not your business and we’re all far too grown for this. If I wanted to tell you, I would. It’s completely unacceptable to insist that you have some right to a persons body just cause they’re different. Can I start going up to women as a man and asking them extremely personal questions regarding their body just cause they’re a woman? I mean they can totally say no, of course. So it’s fine, right?

And before anyone calls me the worst think since Hitler cause I don’t fall at ableds feet and give them my entire medical history whenever they demand it, I always tell them in a pretty monotone voice “This has no bearing on the situation or our relationship so I won’t be discussing this. Thank you for understanding”. It’s a very well used line given how many people in the 45+ crowd especially were never taught that disabled people are human.

author: @SomeRandomEcho
text:
I’m so so worried about making people feel uncomfortable by looking at them. I don’t want to be weird and stare, but a part of my brain is like “woah- they look different” and I wanna take in their appearance but I don’t wanna make them feel weird by staring. I feel really bad about it, but I don’t think my face ever looks disgusted, and I don’t ask invasive questions completely unprompted.

I also wonder if I make people feel uncomfortable by *not* looking at them the “normal amount” (I have no idea what that is). 

everyone’s physical differences are fascinating and interesting, and I am sorry if I make you feel uncomfortable, it is not intentional, I am so sorry, I mean no disrespect and you are all so cool and awesome humans!

author: @mitchell1091
text: I met a stranger at work the other day, prosthetic arm and leg (opposite sides). I hardly even noticed, and about halfway through his interaction, he asked me if I was okay, which caught me a little off guard. I told him I was fine, and asked why he thought I might not be. He told me that fir the last decade, his prosthetics are all anyone has ever been able to focus on when it came to interactions with him. I shook my head and assured him I hadn't really registered the prosthetics, and didn't give a flying fuck either way. I told him that I'd served, and that was why. Little things like that just didn't really matter to me. He looked like he was almost in tears, asked about my service and all in all, he told me he had an amazing appointment and would definitely be coming back. The worst part? I'm a GP, a doctor.

author: @catreader9733
text: In my early days as a TSA Officer, a person with a visible prosthetic leg approached the screening checkpoint.  I did allow my eyes to rest on the prosthetic, and perhaps they stayed there a bit too long for the passenger's comfort.  The passenger said, civilly, but with a bit of edginess, "Do you have a problem?"  I now looked direftly at the passenger's face and responded affably, "Only in that I am considering the options--yours and mine--for screening you comfortably and securely."  The passenger then relaxed and grinned.  We agreed on a protocol that suited and got on with it.

It was the WAY they asked, in a baby voice as if I was mentally disabled and then seeming incredulous that I was alone and didn't have a caretaker with me.

author: @iamme2072
text:
If it was a STEM person, they may have gone into analysis mode.  They see something new and technically intersting, mechanical, electrical, or otherwise, and they'll analyze how it works, break it into parts in their mind, analyze those, then reassemble it in their heads, possibly with new parts in their head to see if they optimize it to make it better.  Even prosthetics.

If it is, They're not doing it to be creepy.  Engineers, scientists, and technicians are always looking for a problem to solve, or the next item to optimize.  Yes, there is a bit of hubris that makes them think could potentially build it better than your prosthetist.  Won't stop them from doing it anyway.

She reported it to her school and as far as she knows, he called her a bitch once and they never had another interaction. People are f-ing crazy as soon as they meet someone different, ESPECIALLY (but not exclusively) if that person is a woman

author: @sisu411
text:
I'm more the type to completely miss the fact that someone has a prostetic leg...
I met a new co worker for the first time at a Office partd and we talked for like 2 hours. I started on a project with him the next week and we had meetings like twice a week for months! What I didn't realize is the fact, that the guys left leg was in fact a prostetic. It also never came up in conversations or anything (or I just missed it... seems like something I do a lot....😂)

After almost a year (!) he came early to a meeting and asked, if it would bother me, if he "popped is leg off real quick in my office, it's kinda loose today."
I was like "Pop off your what now?!" 😂😂 He thought it was hilarious, he thought I had realized it by now... well I didn't... sorry Marcus 😂

author: @RCMIToronto

In fact they've gone after her for spoiling it for the respectable amputees. Guys seem to get less disgust and more "Thank you for your service".

author: @Belgaer
text: My most uncomfortable conversations have always been the ones where someone tells me they want to have a leg just like mine. I’m really not sure if they even realize the implications of that. It seems like its well intended, maybe they just say it in an effort to make me feel welcomed in their presence, but also, they’re super awkward and uncomfortable because they’ve clearly never been in this situation. I started responding with “Really?? That’s crazy, because I’ve always wanted one just like yours!” And, based on their response, maybe I’ll throw in a “Wanna trade?” Maybe that’s mean. I’m not sure. Maybe it forces someone to reflect, and that gives them a chance to grow, so ….just maybe it’s fine. 🧐

author: @StillLeeSullivan
text:
Like this guy was so sheltered or something his parents hadn’t allowed him to see a badly injured person and since then he had avoided the experience somehow so it was as if you were carrying your head around under your arm.

Though I must confess I was just as stupid and insensitive toward a girl at my junior high school who had lost about half of her forearm and of course her hand in a highway accident that had killed her parents and sister, leaving her the only survivor. It may have been a combination of knowledge of this compound tragedy and fascination with the split-hook prosthetic she used in place of one hand that rendered me so dumb in every sense of the word upon meeting her. She reacted with weary poise, asking did I want to know about her hand? I don’t even remember what she said after that. I wish I had just been straightforward and not so embarrassing to both of us.

author: @richardhill2643
text:
Random Person, “Oh, how did you lose your leg?”
Me, “Well, I was on my way home on the bus, got to my stop and got off the bus. As the bus pulled away I realised that I had left my leg on the bus. I later rang the bus company’s “lost luggage” department, but it had not been handed in. Gone! That is how I lost it.” 
Random Person “!!??”…☹️
Me (internally) 😂🤣😂🤣

author: @ElliottRodgers
text:
I am a Left Below Knee amputee. 

I was in wheelchair in supermarket wearing shorts and in milk aisle when a sweet 5-6 year old asked "Hey why is your leg goned? "

Rather than ten minute explanation involving falling on ice, years of agony, multiple surgeries, messed up medical procedures until in desperate I told surgeon to take my leg off I told her a shark bit it off. I live 100s of miles from ocean... 

Saw mum few weeks later and she  confessed her daughter went into school and wrote two sides of paper about meeting a lovely man. He had only one leg as a ahark bit it off... 

Teacher spoke to mum after school about her daughter enornous fibs... 

🙄🤷‍♂️

She was a very strong lady... but even her hope for a miracle like mine was not possible for her cancer and nothing I could do to give her the miracle she wanted. Life is short and have to make the most of the advances available even not perfect. I wish the prosthetic was stronger for what I do but it isn't. Carbon fiber is not that strong to me... I broke a foot too... as well as the carbon fiber socket... interesting... thanks for your videos. author: @MarkusAldawn
text:
I'm also not sure how much I love the concept of 'unfair advantage' as applied to sports- people are fine with the fact that all the Olympic runners have a very specific body profile. All those runners also wear shoes, because it makes them faster and protects their feet so they can run longer without pain. So if one day we create prosthetics that can beat the speed of a fleshy leg, I'm not sure how unfair this advantage is!
It's possible that very few of the people watching this channel will have body types that could even theoretically compete at the highest level (somebody might be too tall or short, too thin or too fat, have muscles in the wrong places, etc). Why is it that "long legs, slighter upper body, powerful calves" should be an okay requirement to have as a body to be the fastest 'fair' athlete, whereas "below the knee amputations on both legs" should be an unfair body?

And, of course, like a lot of these complaints about unfair advantages recently, it often assumes people will intentionally put them in the position to receive this advantage. Which always seems unlikely to me, but especially here.

It is like when you see kids on two crutches literally fling themselves across the playground at high speeds.
Yes quicker on short straighr distances, then they need to turn a corner!

So, in full flow on that staight 100m, last 10/20m there may be a minor advantage, but getting to that speed takes longer, and so would never in competition see it as an advantage for all the reasons she said - the start, the build up of momentum, the sheer stress on the other muscles to actually move rhe articficial leg and keep it going. 

It is an illusion of advantage when you just see the full flow working.

author: @hibingboing2418
text:
Hey, I don’t want to make anyone angry, and I know having a prosthetic isn’t easy, but, unless I misunderstood you, you didn’t prove that it’s not a benefit on straightaways.

Many races don’t use starting blocks, especially long distance ones, so that stat isn’t useful for every race.

Taking longer to reach aerobic capacity is a good thing, assuming no other variables (I.e. acceleration) change. 

Yes, a prosthetic may be a direct hinderance around corners. However, if prosthetics are better for straightaways, then that marginal 1-3% slowing could be counteracted by even a small 1% or .5% increase in straightaway speed for certain tracks.

I’m not saying it is an advantage, you just need one or two more measurements (acceleration, top speed, endurance test, etc.) to find that out.

author: @gordon.sarratt
text: I would like to note that while these statistic figures are a serious disadvantage in any running scenario, they are WAY more important when running distances from a sprint up to probably something like a 3200 meter race. At least from how those numbers sound (which is obviously not the most important thing) the disadvantage lessens if the running distance is longer that 2-3 miles, which is pretty sweet since many people’s average fun/fitness runs end up being between 3 and 7 miles. It might be that most running blades are tested in this kind of environment, and designed for this kind of run, which is why it’s when they’re the most effective (but not quite as effective as a leg with muscles in it) however, take all that i just said with a HUGE grain of salt because Im just guessing.

author: @Zoey5374
text:
Out of curiosity, have you heard of Terry Fox? Here in Canada every year there's Terry Fox runs that happen across the country to raise awareness and money for cancer research. He lost his leg to cancer. To raise awareness about Cancer and to raise money (with a goal of $1 for every Canadian, at the time about $24 million). With his leg amputated, he ran across Canada hoping to go all the way from the east coast of Newfoundland all the way to BC. He only made it to Thunderbay, Ontario before the cancer had spread and he passed. However, the Marathon of Hope brought awareness about cancer and raised a lot of funds for cancer research! 
I've always been amazed by how much effort he put in, and how hard it would've been! I believe his brother and friend were following him in a van, but he was running for hours every single day! I never liked having to run for short distances like the Terry Fox runs we did in school, I can't imagine running that far, in that amount of time, and with an amputated leg! 
There's a reason why he's regarded as a real Canadian hero!

author: @TomTomLLong
text: I read a good explanation to this from a bi-lateral bk who was shut down in a sport due to that very question. His response was this; Yes, yes they are an advantage, because although I can't run or move faster, I train harder and longer to try and make myself stronger. I get up earlier, train harder, stay longer, focus more intently and just flat out want it more than most people that have two legs do. So, in answer to that decision that I have an unfair advantage, I will answer yes. Because I WANT it more than the other competitors. It was powerful to read his response and has helped me to respond to similar questions and concerns. We just try harder than most two leggers. Keep rockin it!!

That would not be cool.

The thing none of these people are getting is that maybe the real advantage an amputee has is that they've lost that limb, and know what life is like without it, after all it's not like you're walking again in 6 weeks, like with a broken leg.   Maybe these athletes have just found more motivation to work harder than the other athletes.

author: @basher20
text: A lot of this stems from the Pistorius case, which was poorly reported at the time.  IAF rules prohibit any use of spring devices intended to enhance energy return, and the running blades obviously fell afoul of this rule. (Vaulting poles are a special exception, as that is the essence of the event.) The South African federation applied for a waiver, which was granted, subject to complying with the rules governing the Paralympic event.  Here again there was an issue, since the particular model of blades he intended to use had not been on the market long enough to comply with those rules.  Thus, the contested second waiver, mostly opposed by those who feared that setting the wrong precedent could turn Track and Field into the kind of equipment battle that was occurring in swimming, with the custom performance swimsuits in that era.

author: @kitmakin289
text: Plus the more advanced and closer to human leg movement a prosthesis gets the less and less likely any amputee is actually going to be able to afford it.

author: @gemcorker3982
text: I remember people saying this back when Oscar Pistorius was wanting to run at the Olympics as well as the Paralympics, like he was suddenly gonna run like the Bionic Man or something. And then he placed 16 out of 52, when he'd been getting medals in the Paralympics, so that shut them up somewhat. I think a lot of people fail to understand the amount of work calves, ankles & feet do when running, and while a carbon fibre running blade has a good amount of spring to it, it can't give you the same bounce and control as an ankle, foot and a whole bunch of muscles and ligaments. And that's OK!

author: @Serenity_Dee
text: There's a hypothesis that humans evolved as endurance runners, and there's good evidence to support it. And building a machine that can even just walk like we do is _extremely_ difficult, even just replicating the mechanics, let alone all the autonomic stuff that goes into it.

author: @metawarp7446
text: But if we are evolved for endurance, to me it sounds plausible that prosthetic legs couls be better in 5 000 meters for an example (?)

author: @Serenity_Dee
text:
@metawarp7446 Prosthetics aren't integrated into the nervous system, so you don't get the fine autonomous control over your joints or the same range of motion, or any of the other things our meat limbs generally do (for example, I have all my meat limbs, but osteoarthritis means I don't have all the cartilage in my knees I should, and my extensor tenosynovitis in my right wrist means it's prone to painful inflammation, but they're still much more functional than any prosthetic). That's why you need different prosthetics for different purposes; a walking leg is better for, y'know, general walking, but it doesn't have the energy return of a running blade, let alone what our tendons do in our meat legs.

Most people in the modern world don't do regular endurance running, but one of the things we have over most other species is the potential for utterly _absurd_ stamina; there are a few populations that live in ways similar to what we evolved doing, and they can run down birds. As in, the birds can't fly as long and as far as humans can without rest. We have used horses for transportation for tens of thousands of years, but their advantage isn't stamina, it's weight capacity. A human can move at pace for distances and times that would kill any horse. And we're also far more resilient by comparison; we have evidence of people healing from pretty severe injuries (and being cared for by their group) even before we had agriculture.

author: @farkasmactavish
text: ​@samhowl1152No, absolutely not. Looking at how disc golf is done, it involves your core a LOT. If you can't engage your entire core (which includes your hips and upper thighs), then you can't put in as much power.

author: @kiingblue
text: ​@farkasmactavish This. I have chronic right hip pain (so not even total loss of the leg, but I lack full range of motion and it impacts my balance very heavily- I have to focus so hard just to walk straight, it's unreal) and used to throw shotput and discus in track. Even though I was more powerful/muscular than most of the kids my age, I had to put in a lot of extra work in order to get my technique right on account of the balance issues and how difficult it was for me to fully engage my core muscles, and got completely whipped in competitions lmao. Not dead last, but pretty close to it. I can't imagine wheelchair users have it easier compared to abled peers by any stretch of the imagination.

author: @Mattbird-2676

author: @lincolnpascual
text: It really comes down to being like John Wick- Sheer will and determination. I'm a BTK amputee, former military, and I'm actually capable of doing all the grunt stuff I was capable of BEFORE losing my leg. I run, climb, shoot and fight at nearly 100% of the capability I used to have before. About the only thing I can't do as well is swim. I sink like a rock without a pool noodle wrapped around the shaft of the foot. And my muay thai is ON POINT. I keep telling my buddies not to test it, but 3 guys and three broken toes later, they still haven't given up trying to break my prosthetic. Hey, they know what they're signing up for. I don't force them to do it, but muay thai fighters are a different breed. So are Rangers, cause the younger guys I train these days are just as mental... or maybe I'm just mellowing out in my old age. I'm pretty sure I was just as dumb in my 20s.

author: @douglaswolfen7820
text:
​@laurieb348 words mean what we use them to mean. I think it makes a lot of sense to call a prosthetic leg a leg. It's the thing that supports you, that you put your weight on, and that you move back and forth in counter-phase with your other leg when you're walking. Once a prosthetic leg is in place it looks like a leg, works like a leg, and it's used like a leg… because it's a leg

And no, it doesn't look, or work exactly like a biological leg, because it's not a biological leg. But it's still a leg if that's what Jo wants to call it

author: @annonymat
text:
Get a USB-C BMS. These chips are cheap A.F. 
Go to a vape store and ask for a bunch of used flat vape pens. 
Have the feel of your trust harvest the batteries and solder them to the BMS. 
Have your friend solder a switch to one lead of the BMS and attach all the LEDs to that switch and the other lead of the BMS. 
Switch: $1 BMS $2, cables €5, types of 3-5V LED: go wild girl. 
If he is a great geek, he’ll throw in a resistor for long life. 

If you wanna go full on crazy, get an arduino with the WLED app and connect it with the stuff I mentioned before. Now You can have any LED effect you can imagine. 
Sparkles, RGB, dripping lights, rings going around…

author: @freakern
text:
There are some cool things you could use for led‘s. My son wanted the glowing thing he has on the chest. I printed some parts and put some mini led‘s meant for mini Christmas trees in there. They run out of 2x cr2032 batteries and run continuously for a couple of days. It’s a pretty thin cable with some ultra small leds.

Another idea are some baloonlights. Each led has its own battery encapsulated. We used these to put some lights into legos with some double sided tape. Maybe you could use some superglue.

author: @jamesirwin7677
text:
I'm not sure this fits.
Ableism is a word for unfairly favouring non-disabled people. Ableism means prioritising the needs of non-disabled people. In an ableist society, it's assumed that the “normal” way to live is as a non-disabled person. It is ableist to believe that non-disabled people are more valuable to society than disabled people.

author: @JHabc
text: I haven’t lost a limb, but I have a bunch of disabling health conditions.  So many people want to tell me what I should do or how I should overcome.  I started grad school a few months after I suddenly got very ill.  I was not recovering as expected.  And a colleague said something about how having faced death would make my life so amazing.  It didn’t. I had no idea I was never going to recover the way the doctors told me I was.  That my expectation that this was one more thing I was going to overcome and add to my story of inspiration was nowhere near reality.  That expectation and all the people who expected me to bounce back ended up doing me a lot of harm.  Because I felt too ashamed to speak up about how much I was struggling, and I didn’t get the help I really needed until so many things snowballed.

author: @Kebutor

Explaining to a Paraplegic = Para-Phrasing
Explaining to a Handicapped Person = Handicaption?
Explaining to an Amputee = Amputee Degree?
Explaining to a person with a prosthetic = Prosthetic Phonetic? 
I think that is a good start, I need to know more of the nomenclature and I think I can do better.

author: @CelticAncient
text: @carolinemorgan94the sleeve or liner that is worn on our leg is a lot like a scuba wet suit. There is no air getting to the skin and all the sweat is not leaving the liner, so the skin is soaking in the sweat and not drying. So it’s really good to get out of the prosthetic at times to let our skin get air and to clean off the sweat

text: @AlwaysAMan78you don’t want that level of convenience. I used to be a machinist and we made prosthetic limbs and the machine tools prothetologists used to make them. The technology was pretty amazing then, much more so 30 years later, but there’s a ways to go to achieve that goal.

author: @LadyKiimz
text: I had a stroke at 17 it affected my right side I was in school and just collapsed. I couldn't speak, walk etc it was def very scary I was lucky my teacher immediately noticed and she and some classmates carried me to the nursing bay and I was even more lucky because my mom was a nurse and the hospital she worked at was less then a mile away so she came amd I was almost immediately admitted to the hospital. I am 30 now and I won't lie to you i still have residual, confession, coordination issues, speech issues, and I never fully recovered in my hands- I still dont write as well as I did before etc. But!!! It's been over a decade and I'm def about 95% recovered. It definitely gets easier. 😊

author: @JoelHope-mv6bp
text: In 2021 I died 2 times when I was having all of the toes that was on my left foot amputated. I have been a leader in the church Community for decades and when this happened I had my surgery I had all things that normally have to do I had wound VAC on the bottom of my left foot because of infection of course and when I was going to share it at church I never got to. I am a leader in the church but the pastor is a dictator and I think he gets up mentality because he's Ukrainian and I was hoping to share when I died on the table and I came out of everything I thought about it and I wasn't really scared because I know but if I did I was going to go to heaven. I want to share this a church and the pastor still hasn't allowed me to share it. I don't know if it gets to read this but there are some of the things you talk about I can relate to, but of course not all of it I have all of my foot on my left foot and all my left leg and that sort of thing so I didn't really totally lose as much as you did, but I have been a world-class marathon runner I ran the 1984 Olympic trials in the marathon and that was a 1983 Junior College 10,000 M champion so when I had my amputations done it kind of took a lot out of me, but since I knew I had Jesus on my side I knew I was going to be okay. And I don't know how much of the religion thing you still don't like or do like and I do not know if you read this, but just when I got kind of normalized in my life I was taking care of a young man in his home Thanksgiving of 2022 he nearly died on me in his home, I'm a home health nurse and I got him evacuated to the hospital and I thought everything's was okay and I'll be damned if I was wrong big Corporation Maxim who I was working for try to get my nursing license removed because they fucked up and did not have Emergency Equipment in the house. And something I don't know if I ever told you if you do read this I'm Jewish or should I say Messianic Jewish because I'm a Christian and I'm certain this is why maximize after my nursing license and I just found out a month or so ago after going through court on the 6th and 7th of January of this year that I did everything right and it was a waste of everybody's time. I'm going to sue them and I'm going to talk to an attorney in the next couple days because this is insane. For 2 years I knew I did everything right to keep that young man alive I went through all kinds of different Hoops I had to go through. The father was in the household at the time the kid was 18 the father was 40 and the father was screwing everything up his son was dead and I know it, but I had to stay centered and focused. In the meantime I got evicted and I had a number of other Financial issues rise up it really beat me up because what Maxim did and that's why I am going to sue them. There are times I mentioned Tia's to you but if you're sure that the doctor said stroke I get that. I mentioned Tia's because I thought you weren't 100% on board on what doctors told you and I thought it maybe would be something that would nudge the doctors in that direction because you have no Aphasia or anything like that and that's very much more likely to happen with somebody had a TIA then a stroke. I hope that we do talk because you seem like an okay person and I'm not flirting but I am if that makes sense because you're a female and I think you're funny and I don't know if I talk to you the same way if I were a male, but I'm just being bluntly honest. I'm not flirting in the sense of every time you turn around Joel's going to be there trying to flirt with you. But I just thought I'd throw that out there because you do bust me up a lot of times with this thing you do. Have a blessed week and weekend

text: Accidents happen, but I think there was some INSANE negligence from the gym she was training at to just let her just go home after having a stroke. There needed to be trained medical personnel available for when freak accidents like that happen, especially with something as dangerous as BJJ. Whoever she was training with should not be allowed to be in business anymore.

author: @thundergirl6518
text: Hello Jo. I just came across your channel. I am so sorry about your stroke. I do understand balance, vertigo, dizziness, disequilibrium etc. I been living like this for over 12 years. Not one Dr. has been able to figure out why my vestibular system is damaged. I did have on sided TIA many, many years ago but they say no connection.  I am stuck homebound with no family or friends because I can not get out to meet people or when I do my illness scares them off. I can not even walk five minutes and my body rocks and aways from chronic dizziness. I can not pre-plan an outing etc. I though about trying to start a you tube channel, really do not think I would be good at it, I find most people get annoyed when I complain about the condition I am living in, but you are a natural, very relaxing to watch. I will pray that your healing is fast. I wish I had more help. It is so hard being somewhat alone in the world. Stay strong Jo!❤

Unfortunately, I am a disabled  Senior on a limited budget. Please know that you are in my thoughts and prayers. Take care if yourself.

author: @AnnoyingNewsletters
text:
2:55  _Letting people take care of you and take you for a walk like a toddler..._

Immediately post stroke I loved going for  *_walkies!_* Standies, not so much.

We went to Aldi to get me some groceries, and it was about the perfect sized little store that I was able to get around it without being too exhausted, but standing in line at the checkout was excruciatingly unbearable, so much so that if I wasn't paying attention, my numb left hand would push the cart forward impatiently.

Not quite alien hand syndrome, but it definitely needed supervision, especially at meal time; otherwise I'd find it playing in the mashed potatoes or something.

Our running joke was that the reason was that I'd developed taste buds on my finger tips, and that's also why they felt numb to the degree that it felt like when you are wearing latex gloves and touch your fingers to your thumbs and there's just those two thin layers extra. 

It -was...-  *_it is still_* like I'm always wearing medical gloves. It's like gloveception when I do wear gloves for painting miniatures with the airbrush.

author: @drcorydc1
text: Strokes are scary. My wife had a stroke 2 years ago. Fortunately, she is doing well. The mental fatigue was a surprise. She can only do few things per day before she's mentally exhausted. The cognitive loss caused here to retire. Although, from the outside she seems normal. She suffered some depression because she sees what she can't do anymore. She was recommended "Healing the Broken Brain" by Michael Dow. I hope you have a good support team to encourage you. A stroke is not always visible from the outside so others may not understand what you're going through. Stay positive. Best of luck.

author: @alicekristinmense4739

So yeah, strokes can have many effects and can be different for a lot of people. I wish you a speedy recovery and take care of yourself!

author: @leighalbrey1011
text: Yes i am a stroke survivor two  Had a ankle fusion and they didnt give me the blood thinner after the opp thats the first stroke  .Then was going in for a opp on my heal and they had to change the warfarin to clicksane and the dosage was too low as it should off been 100mls and the dosage they give me was 40mgs so had the second stroke but i got to the hospital this time for the wonder drug  i had to learn to talk .Then Ten weeks after having the stroke i had to go and have below the knee amputation as the ankle fusion was no good to many problems .They done the amputation while i was awake as after having two strokes in two years they gave me a epidural to be on the safe side. The amputation is a new way of life  learning to walk  and having the two strokes i am getting there its not easy  i go toa gym two days a week and i have one on one with the physiotherapist they have helped me a lot to regain my strength.ILive in New zealand.

author: @CoopersAmputeeLifestyle
text: Hang in there. I had a minor set back a few times with pressure sores while out fighting forest fires. I was just on my leg too long. author: @danaserine5895
text: Don’t know how or why I came across your channel because I don’t really relate to your experiences at all except that I also had a few neck injuries around 9-13 years old and am finally getting treatment through blaire upper cervical chiropractic. What stood out to me is you fell on your neck at your accident. Is it the same side you lost your foot? Is your current injury on the same side? A neurologist is great for the neurological injuries but your bones may be contributing so please also see an upper cervical chiropractor if you can! So many of my current problems are from not treating my c1-c2 subluxations sooner and mine are anterior subluxations which compress the brain stem.

author: @alitucker2348
text:
I have not had a stroke, but I have been through so much as well and I wish I could give you a hug. 

I am 35 years old and a triple amputee thanks to flu A & septic shock. I lost my second foot, which had previously been reconstructed with a flap from my back, on 4/3 due to a 1+ year battle with osteomyelitis. I have five fingers on my left hand and no other digits or notable extremities. (I was in perfect health prior to 12/22. I am also a wife and mom of 7, and barely 35.) 

I am only commenting because I relate to your difficulty right now when it comes to asking for help. We were self-reliant for so long, but right now we are at rock bottom. Asking for help is so hard. So vulnerable. I am lucky to be alive. WE are lucky to be alive. 

I know you are swamped with fans reaching out, but I wish I could give you a hug. ❤ If you have a spare moment, and Facebook, please feel free to reach out to me. I don’t know anyone like me. (No pressure, but my account is fb/aliconcha) Take care of yourself! You are an inspiration!!

author: @anthonyfox9510
text:
I had a heart attack and a left hemispheric lacunar infarction in 2019 (aged 56) but didn't know I'd had them. Thought the tiredness,  confusion and other things was due to my pre-existing fibromyalgia and diabetes. Didn't discover what had happened until a year later when a CT scan threw up the "incidental finding" of a clot in my heart. Cardiologist said being diabetic meant I didn't feel the pain... 

So many meds now that I rattle, but stable, on the mend, have some right side impairments, dexterity issues, emotional lability, but not giving up, nor falling over or walking in circles (dark humour helps).

Spent more time in and out of hospital for 6 months during lockdown than in the past 30 years.

Talk to people all the time, try new things, learn new skills, keep your brain occupied. Don't think why me, woe is me, it's happened for a reason, fight, fight, fight, keep moving forward, you have a new purpose, a second chance.

P.s. didn't catch covid, despite, age, diabetes, blood type, being in and out of hospital, etc, etc. still coof free👍🤞

You are an inspiration, your vids are funny, thought provoking and very welcome. You've been through a lot and your spirit and determination shine brightly.

Hope to see new vids while i work through your past ones.

💜💜💜

P.p.s.

Was on so many meds (23) and going to the Pharmacy regularly that they gave me a job as a Pharmacy Dispenser, now qualified and sharing med experiences with others (on that one, not had that one, didn't like that one...)

I have just discovered you and you have inspired me so damn much.. I used to climb ladders, trees and cut the down, for 25 years my legs were my 401k....and then one night a month after I survived the ga prison system I overdosed on some shit that I didn't even want or even ask for, I died 3 times before I actually made it to a hospital just for them to give me the same drug that I was dying from, when I came to after 2 months I did wake up with both my legs but they just decided to quit communicating with my brain, the docs say it was Bc I was dead for so long but they were honestly the reason that I was dead for soo long!!! They mis diagnosed me and waited 3 days before they took blood to see what was in my system! Now I'm sure ppl are gomma say all the bullshit about well you did drugs thats what you get ..blah blah blah, ..all I did was smoke a joint amd the other ppl that smoked that joint with me died, one person being the mother of my child, the Weed was laced with feytnal!!! 
.......you inspire me bc I have not given up and will not give up, I still have two dead legs thay I know worked and they will work again! And your thoughts on therapy hit my soul!! Therapy is where I go-to get pissed off and let the words fly but I feel soo much better afterwards...jo baby girl,  you are amazing dont let your light fade away , no I dont know what you going through but i know that them waist belts and walkers are far behind you!! It's been 5 years and I still swallow my pride 7 days a week and keep trying, I almost wish prosthetics were a option but they say it's my brain not my limbs..I just turned 35 going on 5 and it's a beautiful disaster. And I'm not sure my pain is called phantom pain but I cant sleep more than a hour or two at a time bc my legs catch on fire and try to run away from my body , but shit I wish they would do that when I ask them to!!!! author: @frugalprepper
text: I had a similar stroke in my cerebellum on October 12, 2023.  They got me in and on the clot buster within a half hour of symptoms.  I was dizzy, lost my ability to keep my balance and had really severe double vision after the stroke and it was blurry.   I also got dizzy when turning my head from side to side.  I stuttered and still have an occasional problem saying a word.  What helps is take moment, visualize what you want to say and think of the first letter, and it will come to you.   I was in ICU for 3 days, the first day was really hard, I had severe nausea, and just felt like crap.  I had a walker for a while and couldn't drive.  I had a small problem doing fine tasks with my right hand and I reloaded ammo until I retrained my hand.   3 months later I hade made a pretty complete recovery from it.  It took a lot of rehab, and doing the exercises over and over.  The core body exercises will help you to balance again.  At first I just made myself get up and wash one dish.  Within 4 weeks I was walking around the block (slowly but I did it).   I had to get up and make myself do stuff and my brain slowly relearned everything.   Today I am back to work and living a normal life, and my vision is fine.   All I can say, is YOU WILL GET BETTER!  My stroke was $107,000, but I had insurance that paid 87,000 of it.  They are taking payments on the rest.

author: @marieradatsz4126
text: I had a split carrotid artery stroke 6 years ago,the worst thing at first was the fatigue. I was completely wiped out with any physical exersion. I still tire very easily and I struggle with word finding and being in crowded noisey places. Fear at first was my biggest enemey, I was fearfull of doing anything the longer it has been the less fearfull I have become. My advice to you is to take each day as it comes listen to your body and rest when it tells you to and trust me it will. The numbness in your hands will subside but be mindfull that it will raise its ugly head when you are tired. Your brain learns to retrain your affected limbs but when it gets tired it forgets but this improves more with time. Its a very emotional time but just know it gets better over time xx

author: @atUndyne
text:
So glad that you're still with us - strokes are no joke.

My mom got Guillain Barre (immune system attacks the nervous system) a week after the both of us went to a concert. Not exactly the same boat as a stroke, but a serious complication all the same. She was a physical therapist, someone who would go into work to see how long she could go without sitting. Active on the job and after. She had treated people who got what she did, and she was in denial over what was happening while it was progress. Over 3 days she went from fully active to a quadriplegic. Went to the ER twice and they nearly (accidentally) discharged her twice as she lost feeling in her arms. 

It took time for her to recover. I stepped in as her caregiver for a time, but there were many hard days in the first few months. Fortunately for us, Guillain Barre is something you can recover from. She gradually learned how to walk and care for herself. To get there though, you gotta start somewhere. Some days you might notice something more, others will feel like you're in quicksand. And just like turtles, slow and steady wins the race.

This will be something you recover from. Don't beat yourself up over the what ifs and allow time to take its course. 
Your crochet cardigan is also ADORABLE!

author: @czerniana
text:
I had a TIA a few years ago, which led (finally) to my MS diagnosis. I have had to learn to ask for help, and give up a lot of my independence.  It is… not fun.  Not fair. Every day is different, and the whole ‘one day at a time’ thing is ridiculously accurate now. I just try and take it physically and emotionally one hour at a time.  It’s a good day when I can concentrate on one day at a time. I hope you have a speedy recovery <3 Keep up with the crochet!  It really does help, plus you can make a bunch of neat stuff.  

If I could help I would, but they don’t give enough on SSI to really help often =*(  So take an internet hug instead!

author: @dmkays
text: @FootlessJo Im praying for you to make a speedy recovery. You are an inspiration to me. You seem to be very strong. Ive had 4 surgeries on my lower back, and am constantly in moderate to severe pain. In addition, i am slowly losing my vision. I havent been able to drive the past 5 years, because outside during the day, im snowblind. I am blessed to have the best wife in the world. She keeps me motivated, and doesn't seem to mind being my chauffeur.  There were two things I was going to recommend, but you have done one. They are snow skiing and Scuba. I have worked with Telluride adaptive sports. They ski with me, serve as a good pair of eyes and keep me from further injury. They are great with my paraplegic and amputee friends. The second is Scuba. They say there's healing in the water. You can google HSA, and they can help you get certified and find volunteers where you like to go to the beach. I prefer south florida. I dive with a group of disabled veterans, and there is even a quadriplegic who loves to dive with us. It takes 3 volunteers to make that happen. One holds her tank and propels her. A second keeps eye contact with her, and with different blink signals she can tell him to pinch her nose so she can equalize, and several other signals. She lives for opportunities to dive or hang out with friends. It will seem like no time and your stroke will be behind you. Never give up and get back to doing what you love to do. You have a lot of fans praying for you. God bless.

author: @sandrao8002
text: I had a left occipital stroke - lots of similar symptoms and very similar feelings about it. You will recover. You will get stronger. You will need help. You will have good days and bad days. Be kind to yourself. Cry. Scream. Rage. Hug the ones who love you because they are feeling scared and worried as well. Most of all, never give up. It gets better every day, especially when you put in the hard work of rehab and healing. Your life will not be the same, which can bring on all kinds of feelings. You are doing fantastic. Thank. you for sharing your story and bringing awareness to the reality of younger people having strokes.

author: @ChuckBaker
text: Jo, I just recently found your channel and I have been looking at your videos. I am sorry that you're going through all of this with the stroke. I know how hard it can be not being able to do what you normally do. I had a heart attack two years ago and had to have triple bypass surgery. After the surgery, I was flat on my back and could hardly move. It not only affected my body but my mind as well. I didn’t like having to depend on people, and I didn’t know if I was going to be able to go back to work. I don’t want this to run into a book, I Just want you to know it does get better. If you don’t want to talk to the people around you, then you can write down what you're feeling in a journal. Or tell your dogs the way you’re feeling. They accept you unconditionally and they won’t judge you for what you’re feeling. And just one more thing. I know you're going through a lot but don’t forget about your channel. You probably don’t feel like you have a lot of energy to make videos and that you might be letting people down, but you have people watching who care about you, and they want to know how you're doing good or bad. So don’t shut them out.

author: @Seizure_Sam

Jo, its 7.30am here on good Friday here in Australia where I am. For whatever reason you popped into my head, i just stopped by your channel to see if you had any updates.

author: @nataliepitt5102
text:
As someone who had a stroke at 7 years old, I now consider myself lucky that I couldn’t comprehend all the things that could have gone wrong. I didn’t grasp that it could have been so much worse and being Canadian, it never occurred to me that medical debt was even a possibility that anyone would have to worry about. Being so young, the concept of “l’ll never be able to” or even “I can’t” never crossed my mind. 

I’m so sorry you’re going through this! It sucks massively and you should absolutely take your time! But what originally attracted me to your channel was your determination to live the life you wanted and your ability to find a path to get there. You will undoubtedly do so again!! 

PS: As a fellow crafter, when you’re further into recovery, look into quilling (or as I like to call it “old timey paper twirling”

author: @jeffbentley4829
text: Hey Jo I've seen a few of your videos and one I didn't watch but it kind of struck me what's the one about you leaving your faith. The first thing I'll say is I'm not a religious man! But I do have faith in God, if you have true faith you can't leave that behind. Realize this I belong to an old Brotherhood one that has Brothers of many religions , we all get along as religion is not an issue, wise men realize this, that God did not split the religions man did that. Some man decided that you needed to get down on your knees and pray to the east, or dance around and sing the glory of the Lord , I don't believe God wants that all he wants is your love and your faith in him. You are a girl who has been through much adversity , I have been through my fair share as well you can just call me a half crippled been dead guy, who would trade you his meat foot for the one you got if he was able and probably have the best end of the deal because it looks like you can make yours work. That's better than I can do LOL the good Lord did grant me a good sense of humor. Whatever has happened remember God doesn't make mistakes, the sad thing of it is he doesn't give explanations either. In the Bible were called God's children and just like children you want them to learn something. I think we're all here to learn something what it is I have no clue, but I bet it's not hatred anger selfishness nor prejudice. I'm sure people have looked at you in the loss of your leg, now you're stroke and other adversities looked with pity in their hearts or looked down with you forgetting the old saying there but for the grace of God go I. Either way you want to look at it I'm not that much farther or ahead of you with my adversities. Just remember there's something more to us, and something more out there then we understand in that spirit I am going to say here's some love to you little sister, I pray that you find the faith you need and possess the strength to get you through all of this. You have already come a long way and showing great resilience I think you're wiser and stronger than even you know. Excuse any typos in this I'm using speech to text because my arthritic fingers will not type. And for your laugh of the day Parkinson's runs in my family my sister has it, my Grandmother Had It and I'm wondering if I'm next. The good news is there's a lot of hardware stores in the neighborhood and if I get hard up for cash I'll just go down and get myself a job as a paint shaker. You got to look for your blessings where you can find them I bid you good luck and good day!

author: @EnigmaMan0000
text:
Happy Wednesday Jo, was thinking about you and know from personal experiences how tiring, frustrating, and seemingly never ending and Uber Slow and at times disheartening and overwhelming the recovery process can be. As well as how sometimes over time a lot of the initial support you were getting tends to dwindle down so I just wanted to send a quick comment of support and encouragement and tell you how awesome and amazing you are! 
It takes a lot of real and genuine courage to keep going every day in the face of all the different overwhelming adversities!!! 
It's one of those rare things that nobody has any idea how mentally trying and difficult recovery from any really serious illness or injury is unless they've gone through a recovery process themselves and it's also something you wouldn't wish on people you don't even like! 
Then there's the fact that you're going through physical, emotional, and spiritual healing and recovery simultaneously or almost simultaneously because when our bodies are assaulted by a serious illness or injury it can't help but affect our emotional and spiritual well-being, welfare and health as well! 
So just know you're seriously supported in thoughts and prayers and like others who've been through similar situations although non are exactly the same you've got some die hard supporter's who want nothing but the very best for you!!! 
Stay strong and take care of yourself with plenty of rest and whatever you want to eat. A hint... Ice cream is a great treat to boost your morale if you get down!!? 😁
😁❤️‍🩹🙃💜👨🏻‍🦲

author: @SueMcLam
text: It took two and a half years for my brain to re-wire.  My biggest and longest side affect was fatigue. I had one shitty neurologist who said that I should be over it, when I actually wasn’t. author: @Lily-ls2ow
text: Poppinng in late to send some good energy towards you, cause recovery is a marathon, not a sprint. I myself don't have an experience of a stroke, but my dad went through one over a half year ago. It was a brainstem hemorrage, with mortality rate of 80%, right side of his body and his speech got severely affected. My mom was told by a doctor that "just because he can speak today doesn't mean he will be able to speak tomorrow". Fast forward to now, he is speaking just as well as he did before the stroke, he is able to walk with a cane and his right arm started to regain some movement even though we were told it would be unlikely. Everyone's journey through recovery is individual, but there is hope and things do get better even if at the beginning you're just trying to get through each moment. All the best for you!!!
