author: @equesdeventusoccasus
text:
I want to thank you.
I became an AK amputee late in life. (I joke that my preferred pronoun is cyborg.) I was told because of this I needed to "moderate my expectations." Most of what I have been told by my VA therapists can be summed up by, "You are already retired, just use your wheelchair and occasionally use your artificial leg." Both things in quotes are actual things Veterans Administration Physical Therapists have said to me.

I have pushed myself. I go to the physical therapy gym everyday and hit the exercise bike for 30 minutes with my leg pushing as hard as I can. They now say I have exceeded all of their expectations. I’m still waiting to exceed my own. 

The other day, a physical therapist held me up as an example to another AK amputee. At first I was pleased  by that. Then I remembered your videos with Annika, showing how different two people with similar disabilities can be. Even though we have identical amputations, are similar in size, age, branch of service, etc. he is not me and it was clear that he didn't have my drive… at least not on that day.

I've been disabled decades longer than I have been a cyborg. That might sound like an additional hurdle and it is, but it makes you stronger. I know nothing about the other guy, except he had never ridden an exercise bike since he lost his leg and didn't believe he could do it. I might have made the mistake of trying to motivate him by well intended words that the therapist would have agreed with, but still would have been wrong to say, but remembering your videos, when he asked how I could be doing it so much better than him, I simply said, "I have been doing it for a long while, you are just starting. Stick with it and you will get there."

Thank you for always reminding all of us to be considerate first.

Doctor didn’t think I needed a scan cause I had one 9 years prior and of course because I guess my age..too young..I then suffered the second and worse one the next morning. author: @andyvan5692
text:
one easy way to remember if you are having a stroke, is the acronym 
FAST: F=facial drooping, aka like at the dentist after needle anaesthetic; A=arm or limb weakness (less capacity than before{for us with disabilities}); S= speech, a slurring of words, Spatial disorientation(a lack of balance, dizziness); T = TIME, aka time to act FAST, you are in early stroke symptoms get help NOW!!

author: @carlyar5281
text:
Jo, I just started watching you in January and was one of those new subscribers. 
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. In almost every video I watch I find myself wondering if you have a connective tissue disorder like Ehlers Danlos Syndrome (EDS). People with EDS are at a much much higher risk of spontaneous arterial dissections, like you experienced. If you see this, please talk to your doctors about being assessed for EDS. It is a genetic disorder, so there’s no cure, but it influences how our tissues behave, and we have higher risks associated with certain medical procedures, so additional precautions are necessary to keep us safe. 🙏🤗❤

author: @martinebon4333
text: I had stroke like symptoms after an episode of burnout in January and I just thought it was just stress or psychosomatic symptoms. I had intermittent dizziness, balance issues, and brain fog. It was basically trying to learn how to walk again since the dizziness was really moderate to severe. There was a point I had to use my hiking sticks to walk since it felt like I was going to fall. I went to ED 3 x and I finally was seen by the stroke team and neurologist, my CT scan was normal. On my 4th time going to ED, I was really struggling with my balance and now have permanent double vision. My MRI showed hyperintense regions in my white matter highly likely caused by cardiovascular disease(hypertension). This perfectly explains my gait disturbances, brain fog, dizziness, and double vision. I am now aware Im of higher risk of stroke and vascular dementia. I have now ensured to have major lifestyle changes such as a proper diet and exercise. I have also been monitoring my blood pressure to ensure that there would be no further damage caused to my brain by my poor lifestyle choices.

author: @michellemartinov6267
text:
One of the worst and biggest symptoms in both strokes and heart attacks is DENIAL!!!!! It’s better to be told no you’re not having either a stroke or a heart attack rather than “were so sorry you got here too late to take advantage of the clot busting drug we could have given you”.  
Did you know that dizziness or worst headache of your life are symptoms of a stroke? That extreme fatigue can be the symptom of a heart attack especially in women? That right sided chest pain or even abdominal (think belly) pain can be a sign of a heart attack once again especially in women? 
We have a saying in the emergency room - any pain of anything above the waist, rule out the worst possible scenarios first like heart attack or aneurysm leaking then go to the next possible diagnosis like reflux or bleeding ulcers. And there’s 3 types of strokes - bleeding kind, spasm kind and the clot kind. The last one is the most common. Same thing for a heart attack.

author: @Mary-op8hi
text:
And this is precisely one of the ways that medical gaslighting unalives and disables - after initially seeking help only to be  dismissed by medical personnel saying that our serious symptoms are nothing but “a little anxiety” or “growing pains” or what the f*ck ever else, we learn not to trust ourselves. We learn to see ourselves as “always being dramatic”, just as you said dear Jo. Medical professionals please, just because you don’t know, doesn’t mean it isn’t real. Please make an effort to question your biases about women, chronic pain, etc.

And to lovely Jo, I cannot thank you enough for what you have done and will absolutely continue to do for our disabled (especially us chronic pain and mentally ill) communities. We now send all that love and healing energy back to you. Please be gentle with yourself, EVERYONE needs such care at some point in their adult lives, and our loved ones are happy to give back in such a way. Remember, you are a beloved rockstar, ain’t no silly little stroke going to keep you down 💕🥄💕🥄

author: @MeldaRavaniel
text:
For universal healthcare advocates (or skeptics), I recommend reading "Priced Out" by Uwe Reinhardt. He advocates for social insurance (effectively Medicare for all, as opposed to social medicine, like what they have in the UK) with a "rugged individualist" opt out option, and provides data to support that our current system is not only cruel but MORE expensive.

The main problem with social insurance is that if people can choose not to sign up until they need it, it results in a price spiral that escalates uncontrollably. Instead, he suggests that everyone under 25 is automatically covered and then, at 25, you decide, once and for all, to opt in or out permanently. For life. Everyone who opts in, and has a paycheck, contributes like we already do for social security (preferably on a progressive basis), and medically necessary stuff is all covered, completely, no bills. Ever. If you opt out, you don't contribute to the program, and you can choose to pay out of pocket for whatever you need, buy your own insurance, build up an emergency savings account, etc. If you need care and can't afford it, you will receive it, because it's the right thing to do. BUT, you will be required to pay the program back, whether through automatic paycheck garnishing, through tax returns, estate reclamation upon your death, etc. You're welcome to be ruggedly individual and live by your value of "i should prepare for the worst and not be a burden to society." But we'll still care for you if something happens to you that's outside of your preparation.

It's cheaper to be a good person. It's time for universal social insurance in the US.

author: @MeldaRavaniel
text: I should also mention if you opt in, you can also choose to have extra insurance on top if you feel it's necessary, for whatever reason.

author: @AllySun3
text: You probably already practice therapies… but I truly believe in frequency technologies.  I have witnessed them helping people.  Binaural beats is an easy way to get some sound frequencies to our brains.  You can find some for stroke as well.  Our brains have plasticity, meaning they can create new neural pathways where damage is in other regions.  It might take a bit, but I hope that might help you get your art back, because that breaks my heart for you, but I also have faith in our brains!  I am sending good thoughts, vibes and prayers that your art part of your brain to hands, gets rejuvenated and finds your joy once again!

author: @ClaytonLovendale
text: Jo, you are still an artist. That never goes away. Do not give up, keep at it. Your skill will find different pathways. But it can be overcome. I used to play the guitar. I played for 43 years. 4 years ago Do to cervical degeneration disease I ended up with a pinched nerve in my neck. It paralyzed my left bicep, left wrist, and left hand. With about 30%-40% . I couldn't go to work. I couldn't even tune my guitar. I had no strength to span the strings for even the simplest of chords. Over time, from exercise, and forcing my triceps I gained back maybe 25% recovery. I was able tune my guitar, though I still had trouble spanning certain chords. My old skills were just not there. I need to even want to get back into playing again. I need a challenge to just push myself. To pick up the guitar. It isn't  about your old skill. It is about overcoming adapting and looking forward to the challenge, and take a sense of accomplishment in the bit you gain back. Each day is a new day, and each day is a small advancement. Be glad that you can. If you can't at that time, drop back to what you can do. It will come.

author: @elleofafriend3140
text:
First of all: You ARE an artist! Not "was". You're an artist dealing with an injury. 

Amazing healing can happen & doing what you have in your example is probably improvement over just a month ago.  Don't give up. Neural networks can regrow. If it doesn't return to pre-stroke ability there are other art mediums to dive into that might give you the same therapeutic advantages AND revive your artistic endeavors! 

I understand this frustration!! 

I didn't have a stroke but was talented at drawing when I was in high school. I could do pencil drawings that were similar to black & white photos. But life & my self esteem took a hit on my artistic abilities. I was diagnosed with benign essential tremors (no known cause or fix) & then suffered fingers falling asleep & not being able to hold pen, pencil, or paint brush. So avoided doing art. Always thought issue was caused by my neck. 

Fast forward 28 years later (at 56yrs old) I find out I had SEVER carpal tunnel in both hands! Had surgery & instant relief from numbness & only then did I realize the pain & discomfort I HAD been living with!! Because that was gone! My fingers never went numb again but my arms didn't feel heavy, weak, or ache!!! I was shocked realizing how all that was connected! It wasn't really ME being weak! It was the pinched nerves! I have been able to draw & paint since then & I should do art daily but I put it off.

author: @karenparker7830
text: Also, turn to Italian clay to sculpt in 3-D using your fingers and tools.  This will strengthen your fingers as the clay is firm. Then begin to make silicone molds of your sculptures. Also the medium of using oil sticks is great.  You can spread them with your fingers and using tools, like a comb.  You can make your own scratch boards and any part you feel needs redoing - just scrape it off with the pallet knife and redo that little area.  I love to use slick poster board or a slick surface as the canvas.  You will grow as your dexterity comes back.  I promise!

author: @Mamá_Matriarch
text: From someone whose been there, I am so sorry. In all my OT, while I will never regain that fine motor control, there are Occupational Therapy aides to help with holding pens, pencils, and the like. Look up writing aides. It helps reduce the fine motor control needed to hold the pen so you can focus on the design aspect.  My favorit is using 1" PVC cut into ½ - ¾" slices. Use s hair band to attach two of the pvc rings to a pen so it looks like it has wings.

author: @jjwalter5897
text:
Similar issue here.  I used to draw all the time. It really was a therapy.  I got an infection in my cerebellum that was somehow related to Lyme disease and now I can't draw a simple circle.  Some motion came back, but not the art.

I've lived with the impairment for 35 years.  At least I can walk again. But the loss of my art not only cost me my best hobby, but a fledgling business.

Our lives can be changed suddenly.  Live it like you love it while you can.

Wishing you my best hope for your full recovery.

author: @kilbymorgan8626
text:
May I suggest a different art medium that may be less frustrating? Pixel art.

When I was 16 I was given medication that gave me a permanent shake and numbness in my hands. I know what it's like to not have command over your hands. I still wanted to do art and after trial and error I finally settled on pixel art and I'm happy with it. 
If you want something less computerized I suggest getting some rubber stamps, or stamp markers, and grid paper. Then try to recreate something you made before as practice. 
My profile picture is my first pixel art piece.

author: @EtrnL_Frost
text:
I feel you on this.  I was a vocalist (barbershop, bass) and suffered from sudden deafness in my left ear.  Harmonies and music meant more to me than I can describe.
The deafness is permanent. My left ear is permanently out of tune by about 1.5 notes vs. right ear, and it took me a couple years to ... Deal with it.  
Unfortunately, it's 100% permanent.  I am physically incapable of hearing the things that once brought me joy, and half my brain feels like it's hallucinating the missing pieces.

You'll get there. Neuroplasticity will compensate, and you'll be back, and probably better than before.  I guess, this is my weird way of saying "hey, it's not permanent, hang in there.". But I totally feel you, being disconnected from your art and passion

author: @brianm744
text:
Here's a bit of advice from someone who underwent something similar.  During the Coof, I was diagnosed with throat cancer. I underwent chemo and radiation therapy, which affected my sense of taste, and, consequently my ability to swallow.  
We take the ability to swallow VERY much for granted, since we have the instinct to swallow from before birth. We learn to swallow solid foods as in infant.  We go through life, oblivious to this fact.  Then, when modern medical science takes that taken-for-granted-ability from you so that you can fight the very body trying to "unalive" you, it's extremely frustrating having to relearn HOW to swallow.
Why? Because the nerves and the muscles that one uses to swallow have been severely affected by radiation and, to a lesser extent, chemotherapy.
One of my speech therapists (yes, speech therapists are used to take you through the process of relearning how to swallow) gave me this bit of advice when I was becoming very frustrated with my lack of progress.  She said something like "Think of it as having the joy of to learning again how to appreciate something you REALLY liked to do and is good for you - how to eat and enjoy what you eat!"
From this, I learned not to be so frustrated at coughing every other bite of food or drink and started focusing on the process, of making every swallow deliberate, and focusing following the therapy exercises my therapists gave me.  
They were difficult at first, tiring, and made my whole face ache. But I pushed through that, and now?  Well I again enjoy the foods I did before (still not the same as before, just different).
Think of it this way.  You KNOW you love art. You KNOW you love creating art.  It's the HOW you created it before? That's gone.  Don't try to RE-create what you did before.  Create what you can do NOW.  I'm pretty certain you'll be blown away at the results. As in "Wow! I didn't know I could DO that...."

author: @izzy1753
text: I know this is unsolicited advice (and I am NOT an artist by any stretch of the word), but I had the equivalent of a stroke when I was five, and there were short daily exercises that really helped improve my dexterity, fine motor and precision. So I would do things like try to wrap as many elastic bands around a cling film tube in under a minute (competing with myself), playing this wooden pin puzzle game (can't remember the name), and generally doing crafts that involved moving my fingers a lot. Completely understand that it will be different for adults, and this might not be useful at all, but thought I should share just in case, especially if this is affecting your experience of creating art. Hope you can get back to finding joy in your art as soon as possible!

I was a pretty good artist as a kid. It got to a point that adults watched me draw because they didn't believe me. 
When I was 12 an elderly man hit me with his car. I was out for a while. Severe knock on my noggin. It took some time but eventually I went back to school. 
That's when I found out I couldn't draw anymore. Everything works the same. My handwriting is still flawless.
I just can't make by hands create what's in my mind's eye. 
I'm 42 years old now. I cannot draw anymore and it still makes me sad to this very day

author: @marksmart4347
text: Find a good, reputable reflexologist and get 2 or 3 treatments. My mom had a brainstem stroke that should have killed her. After surviving & P.T., she had very limited use on one side, a feeding tube & her vision in one eye was severely affected. An amazing reflexologist convinced my parents to let her try & help. After 5 treatments, all signs of the stroke were gone. Blood flow to the part of your brain that controls fine motor skills is affected. God Bless!

Funny little exercise that expands what you can paint? Its what my mother did after her stroke. Kids at the school she worked at loved those.

author: @CeaseEcho
text: Try pastels and charcoals? You can blend things and smudge them until you feel you got the right shape. I find it a more forgiving medium and even when drawing digitally I tend to use the smudge tool more than drawing crisp lines.

author: @erickahokkanen310

author: @stevengeorge337
text: I admire you. I had a brain injury that gave me a lot of relearning from hémiplégique to basic autonomous skills to music and that is where I stopped. Tying my shoe laces ok playing the guitar nope, the emotional frustration of having to relearn, too much. Your courage is admirable and time will shorten as muscle memory and neuro plasticity kicks in. Bravo you, you are amazing. Also masking tape and masking products are great for colour bleed prevention. You are allowed to use assistants and new techniques adapted to your condition. Wax and rulers is a whole new world 😊

author: @Absurdtheistic
text:
I needed this... I just spent seven hours in the hospital for the umpteenth time just to get the same answer as always. "Everything seems fine to us." I'm honestly in such a dark spot that my drive home I was sincerely hoping another car would hit me. I won't do it myself  no matter what, but I'm just so tired of the bs of living.

But seeing your positivity in the face of a literal stroke does put it into perspective, especially knowing you as a creator. 

I just need to keep going. One day, a doctor will figure this out, or I'll die trying. Not because it's my only option, tho it is, but because I deserve to hear "you were right" before I die

Just wanted to say thanks. It's hard to keep the will to live strong when dealing with chronic conditions, but we must persist

Monet had problems with his vision as he got older. He just rolled with it and from that came Waterlilies. He also had cataracts, which made his color pallet shift to yellow a bit because that is how the cataract affects your color vision. His art wasn't worse, just different. 
Your art is not worse, just different.

author: @musicalshadow101
text: I have an aquired brain injury - I used to be an artist so I feel you! It can be so frustrating but I found that going back and comparing yourself to how you were “before” is a recipe for negativity. 3 and a half years on, my advice is to figure out what you ~can~ do, and go from there. If shading and colouring is too much, focus on your line art - it’s still fabulous! Or try another medium. It’ll be messy and frustrating but eventually you’ll find enjoyment in something. I think of it as an opportunity to experiment and explore new avenues!

text: As a fellow stroke survivor, I can relate, it's been 20 years since (wow time flies) and I can barely sign my name with my then strong dominant hand, but that has been because I use a computer and I don't need to write,  (besides I learnt to write with my left hand but that takes me some time and is a struggle) but if I was in your situation I would apply myself to exercising my hand as much as possible! that pesky fine coordination is something we take for granted until we loose it, so please apply your time and effort into exercising it, IT WON'T COME BACK BY ITSELF, YOU HAVE TO EXERCISE IT! and you can do it for sure find a specialist that can help you, and be consistent, if you want something you have to be consistent! best of luck to you!

author: @pgreen211
text: Back of the brain is called the cerebellum and it specifically coordinates motor control. Strokes in this location damage the patient’s ability to control muscle function. We call this ataxia. If someone has a sudden onset of poor muscle control, tremors, poor balance, etc, they should be evaluated for a stroke right away.

author: @cfi1598
text:
I suffered a number of head injuries as a child. several times my forhead collided with concrete once by another kid pushing me off a ladder at 4 years old.
I do remember going to the doctor to have fluid drained on 3 occasions. When I was 11 I was discing one of our fields and the pin that held a bar on the bucket came out and the bar struck my head on the left side. Still hurts to this day almost 70 years later.
But here is the reason I write this. I have always had difficulty drawing and coloring like she says that she has since her stroke. I believe that those head injuries have caused me some issues in school and other functions, but I have worked hard, learned to fly and taught flying for a number of years.
My oldest daughter has had a number of strokes that I believe are related to the Vaccine for covid. As she they started after she got the vaccine and there have been a number of people having strokes after being vaccinated with the covid vaccines! She is now barely able to function because of being misdiagnosed on the last stroke by hospital staff!

author: @Celadors
text:
I've had the chance to look into some stroke research after my mother recovered from one.  The best tools to recover function aside from practice are things where you have to vary pressure.  You may find it easier to get colouring skills back with watercolour or paint, than with pens and markers because you can feel and see what you're pushing against.  

Practice of any kind will help, but hopefully this gives you something more to try.  Hand exercises with something like a ball of "slime" will help develop the sort of pressure your body needs to get used to reacting to again, then you can tune the motor control finer again.  I hope things get better quickly, don't let anyone tell you that you've lost function, we're way more neuroplastic than old studies say.  We don't stop learning at 25, they just stopped measuring then.  My mother got her artistic skill in 3D modelling as well as her speech back after 6 months and she was over 70.  You've got this.

author: @danjohnson6800
text: Healing is the slowest work we do, it is undeniable. We can do wonders if we first adjust to this new speed of life, of growth. Your art is really BEAUTIFUL! It will come back around, perhaps in new ways, new and even better, switching to something different perhaps, but with a new maturity. You are still SO young! I look forward to see what you will do in your future!

author: @danjohnson6800
text:
I feel for you! My own experience with a stroke and major depression is to push into the therapy as hard as you can as long as you can every day, for as long as it takes in months or years. I myself struggled with words and clear connected reasoning to produce good results I wanted. I have pushed into a couple of games on my phone, Word Finder, Sudoku, and Solitaire. Over the course of months I am no longer at a loss for the words I'm looking for, and my methodical reasoning skills are nearly fully back, so I can plan and execute.

For physical hand manipulation skills, therapists have exercises for that--I'm sure you already have been and know them. I have found therapists of all manner simply don't go far enough, push us to push ourselves long enough, to dedicate the hours in training every day, the weeks and months it takes.

The adult mind is plastic, though it changes slowly and takes a lot of effort. Like a material such as steel, it bends best when heated (meaning you push beyond your limits and make yourself strained and stressed, then learn to cope with it and stay in that moment), that is when the changing brain activity grows the fastest and we can make the most progress. There is a limit each day, and there are days of backsliding, but a generally upward trending slope.

I can imagine for hand and finger control and reconnecting, perhaps playing an action game with a controller or joystick. This is just a starting point. Find a game you can play a long time and repeat, perhaps several of them and rotate as the mood suits you. 

I'm not kidding about the level of dedication, or the time, or learning to cope with the frustrations we'll uncover in our subconscious. I started 2 years ago and played a couple thousand games of solitaire, a thousand of sudoku, and God knows how many of Word Finder. But 20 years of memory loss, being defeated by long-ish logical sequences of activity I'd need to perform, and reasoning are eliminated.

I'm 69 now and I got a new phone so my game counts started over. Now I'm up to 500 in solitaire  and sudoku. I win now over 90% of the time and can do it in under 10 minutes. When I started with sudoku it would take me 2 hours to play a single game.

3 months ago I fell while power walking and broke my hip. The hospital therapy staff told me to lead the pain with the medication, rather than trying to follow the pain and medicate it away.

That worked fabulously. I took the medication, did the therapy, pushed into the exercises which they educated me on what was safe to do at each stage, then ate well pushed fluids, and rested a lot. The next day, the hip and leg were a little bit better, and the pain was better. 

That was the recipe  for healing!

Rinse, repeat. I have continued to do the exercises and physical therapy, well beyond what is recommended, again finding that pushing strongly into the pain, but still within the margins of safety, causes the body to really get in there and start fixing things. Also the exercises teach the body where the new limits of motion and strength need to be (right where they were before the accident), rather than succumbing to the lifestyle choice of letting the pain limit one's choices, actions, and activities from the get-go, resulting in seriously limitations in walking and movement from then on.

I have full mobility back and am no longer bound by the pain. I never took a full course of pain medication each day as I was determined not to become addicted. I always skipped one pill a day at least. I stopped all the prescribed meds after 3 weeks and rarely use Tylenol . Sure I still have pain, especially after sitting, but you can get used to getting past it. It goes away when I'm off it, such as sitting back or lying down.

There sure are days I want to quit physical therapy but I won't. I want my full mobility back.

same as I want my full mind back, once I figured out how to get there. I've had a lot of counseling and did not come up with these on my own--I've only put these pieces together--no longer have the income to support counselors, so the school of hard knocks is my latest teacher. Absorb what is useful, put together the pieces that you've learned, copy what works for others, understand what works with you and what doesn't. Then start trying things, keep track again of what works, like in a diary.

The world is wide open to you--your brain is made of flesh and blood not steel, and can be persuaded to change. Give it a reason, give it an unyielding intent to grow and get better, and a commitment to its healing that goes on from now to eternity, period. Then you'll have it. What else were you going to do anyway?? Really! We get to choose our outcomes!

author: @lenaeospeixinhos
text:
It's so weird cause the brain remembers how to do things and sends the order the way it always has but then it doesn't happen or doesn't happen the way it's supposed to. And teaching your brain a new pathway to slowly start to get things done again in any way that resembles what you want to do is so lengthy and so strange for an adult. Besides, you keep accidentally defaulting to the old pathway and getting frustrated...
Anyway, it's a process but success is in the horizon, stay strong ❤

author: @dreamwolf7302
text:
I had a stroke when i was 8, caused by a medication i was on for epilepsy.

I had to relearn how to speak. I was bilingual before that, speaking both English and Algonquin, my tribes language.

Its been 24 years, and i still sometimes make mistakes when speaking.

But you know what? I'm a pathologist. I have a Ph.D, im almost done with my second.

A stroke isnt the end of your life, not if you survive it. Its just a speed bump.

author: @elohssa3949
text: Had a bilateral lung transplant and because of some of the medication I’m on I lost the ability to keep my hands steady. I used to pinstripe cars and I also did painting and now I’m lucky I can actually print much less try to paint. Haven’t given up I practice and I’m getting better but it’s been two years. This sucks.

author: @pikeyMcBarkin

author: @shawnafinley1703
text: I had a stroke in December of 2022.  It presented itself with numbness to my ring finger, pinkie and about 1/3 of my palm of my right hand.  My ring finger feels almost normal now, but the pinkie and palm still bug me. Kind of like when your face is thawing from dental work.  I have to concentrate to keep my handwriting from getting sloppy and it’s annoying.  That and dropping small things if they are in the side of my palm.  I was hoping to be back to normal by now. It could be much worse though! Hope that you keep progressing and find ways to keep doing what you love. (I started building Lego and I think it might help, as well as being fun!)

text: I’ll tell you one thing. And this is an old truth as old as the sun. “Lay down with dogs and you’ll wake up with fleas”. author: @MissLilyputt
text: I had what I thought was a regular cough but about 5 days in I had a little shortness of breath and went to the ER and was told I had bronchitis. They started me on meds and everything seemed fine. One of the meds was albuterol to be taken whenever I had shortness of breath. No other explanation was given about it. Well I proceeded to completely empty this inhaler in a matter of days. Made myself tachycardic several times because I thought it was just my anxiety flaring up. When I had shortness of breath and had run out of the medication I went back to urgent care only to be told that I had asthma caused by untreated bronchitis. That I was supposed to have only used 2 sprays in a day and if I required any more I was supposed to go to the emergency room not take more medication. I could’ve damaged my heart but no one ever told me that. I still have asthma about 2 decades after diagnosis.

author: @chasewighton4064
text:
Oh my god, story of my life. I have several chronic illnesses and this is a regular occurrence for me at least twice a month I have to ask myself “is this just two of my illnesses interacting badly with each other while a flare is happening, or is something else going on that has potentially dire ramifications for my life?” 

“Is this just a new way for my chronic migraines to make me suffer, or is this a brain tumour or an aneurysm?” 

“Is this just my mystery chronic pelvic pain combining with my fibromyalgia to really suck today, or am I having another ovarian torsion and need surgical intervention before it explodes? Or maybe it’s appendicitis, or scar tissue has adhered to my intestines (again) but in a way that’s twisted them in a dangerous way?”

“Wow, I can’t put any weight on my right foot and it’s pretty swollen, is this just a worse version of the foot tenderness I have from the fibromyalgia bc I pushed myself too hard yesterday, or did I somehow break a bone in my foot? Or maybe it’s gout? Should probably get that checked just in case.” [I did not get it checked]

Queue constant checking of the Emergency Department wait times for all the hospitals and urgent care centres in my region and making my decision based on that and how mentally well enough I am to deal with all the bullshit I frequently have to deal with from medical staff due to my particular diagnoses, the medications I’m on, and my gender identity and age.

author: @Rose-kj7rz
text:
This is a great video!
I have an appointment in a couple months to be checked for fibromyalgia. I only seem to have flare-ups though every couple years or so. And those flare-ups always come with an "unrelated" illness I'm going through.

This time, it's been a 9 centimeter endometrial cyst that needed to be surgically removed. I was at the urgent care and ER so much that it was getting embarrassing. 
They didn't find anything other than the cyst. However, I was having pain that literally felt like a heart attack at times. Multiple times during a month long period.
I also usually don't get textbook symptoms with illnesses. I get lots of "unusual" symptoms (which left me walking around one time with a bladder infection for 3 months!), and I have a very high pain tolerance (I showed up at the ER with acute appendicitis in my 20's walking upright and with what felt like the flu).
With all that going on, when I do get ill, I'm constantly running back and forth to Drs until I can figure out what's wrong.
It's super annoying and embarrassing when it does happen. Drs treat you different. Like you're wasting their time and they get irritated themselves over it.

author: @pl-we9km
text: I have the same thing. It actually made me a hypochondriac, because I like you said dont know if its friday or if I am in real trouble. My doctor now makes me feel horrible. It is so hard to get a appointment, she has stated that she has to see "real" patients first. And when I sit across from her now she has this bored look on her face, while she tells me I am fine without any exam. This had let me to walk around with a extremly painful overy cyst for 2 months untill it eventually burst and I had to be in hospital. So now because Im so scared all the time, the stess is making my fybromyalgie be at a all time high, and I cant mentally go to the doctor anymore because Im so traumatized. I am on the waitinglist for a new doctor but in my country that can take 6 years :( So yeahh totally relate to your story sweetheart

My Mum mainly puts up with it now so she only really sees doctors/hospital when she feels she has to. But she's also aware when there's a new problem because as she puts it, it's not her pain.

Sadly her local doctors mainly put everything onto her fibromyalgia without even really looking, but years later, actual specialists find other conditions that are far too late to deal with properly.

I've only see this but cannot imagine what it's like to live like this constantly.

author: @RainbowHeron
text:
And then there’s the day when the ER throws you out, you actually do start dying, but because the ER threw you out, you have no confidence in returning. Then you get the day the ER outright lies to you, to get you to leave. And yet another day when the ER just outright abuses you. Followed by the day when the ER admits you, only to ignore you. Don’t forget the day when you’ve been in the ER so often, they look at you funny, because you know the drill. One day you’ll be in the ER and you’ll realize it’s better if you just stay home, because you’re the most competent one in the whole building.

That’s when you’ll gravitate towards integrative medicine, and holistic healing, and God willing, the ER will become irrelevant, because a patient even almost cured is a customer lost.

God Bless you, friends & fellows!❤️

author: @Dawn-Songs
text: Fibromyalgia is one of the " invisible" illnesses .Because there's no visible bandage or plaster cast , many people unkindly accuse sufferers of malingering . I wasdiagnosed in 2006 ,but it took over 8 years to diagnose .It plays tricks with blood tests which keep showing as OK . I was walking with two sticks and burning all over ,but my GP was saying it was " all in my mind" .On advice from someone i demanded to be referred to a Rheumatologist who diagnosed FMS ( Fibromyalgia  Syndrome ) It can be totally debillitating some days and extremely painful . If anyone reading this is being told pain etc is all in their mind , do as I did and demand to be referred to a specialist . 🤍

text: Having your own urinalysis strips helps a lot, as does knowing how to check your own blood pressure, temp and heart rate and oxygen levels. I've survived sepsis due to kidney infections twice and yes it's exactly as you have said, however once you go septic, your body KNOWS that it is dying and that IS different! Ideally you never let it reach that far though. Learn the symptoms beforehand and get into the habit of checking your obs on every rough day.

author: @PBthesquirrel
text:

I have Narcolepsy, which is an auto-immune disease. Without going too far into it, if my immune system goes into hyper-drive, my Narcolepsy also gets more intense. It was actually the reason I found out that I had a kidney infection in the beginning of this year! But also, when I got a tattoo, I slept for the next 3 days bc my body was healing from the tattoo. Life is weird..

author: @SatumainenOlento

author: @beccab8015
text: There's also the fact that when you live in constant pain, what looks like a 5 on a normal person's pain level is like an 8 or 9 for us. We've learned how to function in excruciating pain. So when we say our pain level is an 8-10, we aren't believed and labeled as drug seeking instead of a person who has a severe kidney infection. This was after I had surgery at the same hospital 48hrs prior where they knew a urologist had put a stint in my ureters which is what caused the infection to begin with. They didn't care that I was 💩& peeing blood. I was able to speak and so I must be exaggerating my pain. 2 more ER visits later they finally admitted me and put me on IV antibiotics. But they still limited any pain medication even after they KNEW I wasn't lying or exaggerating.

author: @donaldshimkus539
text: Wow, mine was slightly different, but a long the same lines. I'm 68 and had been sick for a number of years but didn't know it because the illness would be worse at times and when it wasn't, I thought I was ok. I wasn't ok, just not AS sick. I accepted it as normal mostly due to aging. One problem that cropped up from time to time was a bladder infection. Even the urologist couldn't understand why so many times and for a male. I was always given antibiotics which helped till they were gone. Sonograms showed my bladder never came close to emptying. We concluded it was my prostate which didn't enlarge much outward, but closed off the exit from the bladder. The urine always left behind would stagnate and cause the infections. I soon had laser surgery on my prostate and my health has been improving ever since. I've just recently realized I was sick for years and didn't even know it. When your body seems to be telling you something is wrong, listen to it and get the proper help. Your body knows.

author: @pjsplace5665
text: Sounds a lot like an auto immune disease. Some are caused by a deficiencies such as potassium & iodine. A huge percentage of people suffer from these 2 deficiencies in the US. Unsure about other countries. Checking the labels for a daily vitamin, supplements, or food such as avocados might be helpful in closing the gap … consider researching it and the RDA for both. They possibly work in concert in the rebuilding of new tissue.

You seem.very active which helps if you can push through bad days but once you have a real flair up it can set you back months.   Worse part is I think i passed it on to my youngest.   He was diagnosed  with Jeuvinile arthritis  but never presented serum positive.  By 20 he would end up using a cane and crippled up in the fall every year much like I do.   Only thing is i never really suffered near as much when I was younger.  People look at you and see nothing physically wrong and assume its all in your head.

author: @actuallywaffles5267
text:
My mom gets some really bad cramps, and a couple years back she was in pain. Her stomach was absolutely killing her all day. Dad tried telling her to go to the ER, but she said she was gonna wait for Motrin to kick in then it'd be fine. She'd had cramps worse than this and she was used to it.
All night long she tossed and turned, and never got more than an hour straight of sleep.
Finally, at 7 am, my dad just made her get in the car and drove her the 45 minutes to the nearest hospital.  The doctor looked her over, then they took her back for some tests, and within an hour she was in surgery. Her appendix was near bursting. Had she waited another hour she'd be dead.

author: @LeonardoHamato
text:
I don't have a chronic illness but I do suffer from abdominal pain because of a mix of autism, anxiety, stress (anxiety and stress are almost purely because of college), and a bad diet because of said autism. 

My abdominal pain is so common that when my friend who does have a chronic illness and has regular checkups because of it, when they told me they had a uti, I did some research into it, like I do with their other medical stiff so that I know of I should carry something extra on me when we're hanging out or something, I would go to the ends of the earth to make sure that they're in the least amount of discomfort when we're together. I found out, I've probably had multiple uti's over the past year or so and I've just not noticed because I thought it was something else. Apparently spicy/hot pee isn't normal? 

That friend has made me discover so much about myself it's honestly insane, didn't even know I experienced sensory overloads till they told me that what I experienced wasn't a normal thing, now I own multiple different ways to lessen the stress on my senses. 

And I'm just realising, maybe the reason I dislike wearing my prescription glasses is because seeing the world in 4k is subconsciously stressing me out.

text:
I agree🤬 I have the Sickle Cell disease type ss chronic pain, high pain tolerance and asthma. One time I went to the hospital because I was in absolute agony. When I finally got a room some nurse who was either new to the disease or is just racist or even a complete idiot came into my room screaming "Stop yelling for more medicine!" Like btch! I am in AGONY. Anyone is gonna scream when in agony dumb@ss! 🤦🏽‍♀️
There were other times when I went in and the doctors are usually always trying to get me to go home despite how much pain I am still in. They think that I love the hospital, that I don't want to go home. Just because I can move a bit more doesn't always mean I feel good enough to go home. I mean come on. I know my body better than anyone else. Plus, I may love the hospital beds, but everything else sucks. The food tastes like vomit now, I always hated the way morphine would make me feel when it wears off, and being in the hospital would always change my sleep schedule. Apparently my sleep schedule is gone now...
There were even times when they would want me to walk quite a bit because my legs were hurting, but I could tell that wasn't the answer. There are times were I am in a lot of pain but have to move in order to pack up. I'm just pushing through whatever pain I have when I can. It's also annoying that being on a period can put me in too. 
Ever since I accidentally almost completely cut my finger off in October, I started to follow my instincts. Everytime I followed my instincts when I could, things went perfectly, but when I couldn't or didn't, things would go wrong. I've also noticed that if I asked or told a family member something due to my instincts and they'd ignore it, things would go wrong for them a lot more than for me. Still not sure why I get roped up exactly but 🤷🏽‍♀️whatever...

Back then when I was still lying, I realized that ppl would listen to my lies but ignore the truth🤦🏽‍♀️ I have never once lied about my pain and yet I am treated like I have. I like cleaning, I don't mind school work, and I don't mind moving around. It's just that either I am in too much pain to do so because of some negative energy or I just so happen need to use the bathroom or eat before I do any of that.
What's worse is that God made stop doing work and just completely pulled me out of school some time afterwards. Everytime I went to do some school work my pain suddenly shot up to the point I would end up falling asleep, but I could play games...
I'm not sure why He did it still, but we aren't exactly on talking terms rn... I have learned well remembered the truth about how evil He is :/
I'm tired of ppl ignoring me despite me being right all the damn time. I like being right all the time don't get me wrong, I just don't like being ignored. They don't even let me try to explain myself sometimes or they do something so stupid it puzzles me so much I forget what I was going to say ;-;
I'm surrounded by absolute idiots🤬

author: @galespressos

author: @outlawrebelgirl333
text: I have fibromyalgia, lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, asthma, and type 2 diabetes. My pain level is off the charts yet all they want to give me is Tylenol or Motrin. I never know where one starts and another ends, I have so much going on. Ive almost died so many times  especially from infections. I got an MSSA bacterial infection from a navel piercing. It was in my muscles and bloodstream then walled itself off in my spine after I went to the ER. I had sepsis and fevers up to 104. I had to have emergency surgery on my spine to remove the infection. After I got out of the hospital I was on a strong IV antibiotic that made me angry, suicidal, and anxious for 2 months. What a nightmare that was! Oh and how many times I was misdiagnosed over the years, telling me I have this or that like MRSA and locking me away in a contagious ward of the hospital. They put me in a room with an older lady that had scabies which literally ate away her legs! Her legs were literally 2 raw stumps! I was so mad and said I was going to sue the hospital if I caught that. And they wonder why we dont go to the doctors or hospitals!

author: @flxmkr
text:
My mom died from nephrotic septicemia. One day she was fine, the next she wasn’t. 

The first severe symptom we should’ve paid more attention to was her mites on the walls. 

She called me describing the mites all over the walls. I had just rid her house of a moth infestation a year prior to this, and there were swarms of moth worms in cocoons under the bird cages, he dog had just died a few days before, and Dad had mite repellents for the birdcage, indicating there might have been an infestation at one point. 

So when she saw mites, I believed her. 

She was hallucinating. By this time, the infection had reached her brain. 

I could curse myself for not driving three hours to check it out; but I can’t change the past. 

What I CAN do, however, is warn others so they don’t get sucked down the same drain. 

UTIs, bladder infection, kidney infection should all be treated. 

I wipe front to back, check it with a UV light for red, pink or orange (indicating bacteria), wipe if it shows again, then spray disinfectant mouthwash on toilet paper and wipe that front to back.  I check myself for red, pink or orange residue with a UV light, and make sure it’s all clean.  I don’t take chances. 

I refuse to die with dignity when feeling undignified with a UV light might save my life.

author: @aximatic
text: I had the exact opposite experience. No chronic illness, but one time I had abdominal pain that I could only relate to bad gas maybe but it lasted 24 hours and all of a sudden it hit me like a ton of bricks: appendicitis and all of my logic was like "absolutely not" I mean I heard the thing where people with periods don't catch appendicitis cause it just feels like bad cramps, this is just bad gas, I've felt extremely bad cramps and this is a 2 on the pain scale. But I was paranoid enough to go to the ER and imagine my hypochondriac ass when I WAS RIGHT??? But they also found ovarian cysts on the CT scan so it also got me to get treatment (after a horror story caused by my first gynecologist) but now whenever a medical condition pops into my mind, breast cancer, one of the cysts ruptured, I then freak out cause the last time I went to the ER for a paranoid thought, I was right! I did find out that people usually get to the ER 18 hours after the pain started so surgery was my only option other than wait a month for surgery cause anti-inflammation pills exist but I waited too long

author: @lindalane7341
text: 90%of all Fibromyalgia is actually Late stage neurological Lyme disease.  Only ten percent of Fibromyalgia is actually fibromyalgia.

author: @lindalane7341
text: It is most likely Lyme Disease especially with kidney infections.  Lyme is an infection and it attacks vital organs like kidneys, Liver, reproductive organ, the heart, your head, and so much more.  I was misdiagnosed with Fibromyalgia for years when I had Lyme.  I deteriorated.  They refused to test me but eventually I tested positive with Elisa and confirmed with Western Blot.  Testing has a high false negative rate.  Test for Co infections too.

text: This is going to sound crazy but I’ve had fibromyalgia for 26 years.  I stumbled onto a supplement that has 99.99 erased any symptoms.  When I stop taking it (because I think “its gone” and Im done with it), within 2 weeks every inch of me aches.  I start taking it again and within about a week, no more symptoms.  The specialist I saw said he believed it was the magnesium in the supplement that was doing the trick….however..I tried different brands of the same supplement and within a few days all the symptoms returned so now I just stick with the specific one that works for me.  specialist also said “What helps one person may not help another”…He seemed to think that it has to do with trace minerals lacking in our systems.  Hope this helps someone as I know how debilitating fibromyalgia can be.

author: @ambermcallister5980

Dealing with this right now!! 
EDS, fibromyalgia, among other things... I thought I was dealing with POTS issues and was sent to a cardiologist. The stress test didn't go well. The look of alarm on the techs face was something to see. She stopped the test 4 and a half minutes in, and asked repeatedly if my chest hurt. All I could do was laugh and tell her I felt the same as I have for going on 2 years. Follow up appointment had me start medication while we wait for my heart cath... suddenly I now feel the chest pain along with the shortness of breath. Super fun playing "is this something new or just a Thursday" lol.

author: @kevinwalker7392
text: To the author and anyone else reading this with chronic pain syndromes. Your pain is not caused by anything physical. More than likely it is being caused by your brain subconscious to deal with deep seated emotional pain and stress. Please understand that I am only trying to help you. I know you’re not making it up and faking the pain. The pain is real caused by your brain subconscious. The syndrome called TMS was explained by a smart PMR doctor named John sarno. His book healing back pain is cheap and likely at a local library. The audio book is free on you tube. No doctors, no shots, no pills, no co pays, no supplements, no lies. Your pain is treatable if you’re 100 percent honest with its cause. Your subconscious will stop you from faking if you don’t believe it 100 percent. You can beat this pain. It takes time, patience and 100 percent authenticity. Read John sarnos book “healing back pain”.  Don’t become a cash cow for a corrupt medical system.

author: @Rob-me8vp
text: For the vast majority of people diagnosed with Fibromyalgia the diagnosis is wrong.  They usually have myofascial pain syndrome.  They present the same with pain in the same locations and they are both diagnosed by exclusion as blood work and imaging are ineffective to make a diagnosis.  Fibromyalgia is a CNS condition where the brain perceives non painful stimulus as pain whereas MPS is a local muscle condition where there is an accumulation of painful and inflammatory chemicals in parts of the muscle.  Treatment is different.  If massage hurts, but later gives you relief then it’s probably MPS and not fibromyalgia.

author: @merin5230
text:
This is so true. I have permanent nerve damage due to an epidural abscess. Along with longstanding fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue. Its invisible. Nothing to see. But you know, you get used to the pain. You literaly get used to the pain. Pain that would drop a healthy person to their knees on the spot. But if its a bit more severe one day, is it due to "something worrying" or is it just that you need a chiropractic adjustment to realign your spine. Or is it because you just slept in an awkward position and woke up in pain with a "neck thing" going on which will definitely be relieved with an adjustment or the pain relief that we have on us at all times.
Its really hard to tell. We truly have become accustomed to severe pain and have developed management routines to be able to live any kind of life.
Being in pain is exhausting, so crashing fatigue is common. We're not lazy, just exhausted. Enduring pain at this level just sucks energy.
And we may look fine. May even feel fine,(compared to how anyone unaccustomed to chronic pain) but if we say we need to go somewhere quiet and just lie down for an hour, were not being rude. Were in pain amd need to take pain relief and are genuinely just completely depleted and need to recharge. And after a while, it gets taken for vranted that you are able to tolerate pain and therefore can do what everyone else does and saying we need to rest is attention seeking.
This lady is right. I get yelled at for doing too much sometimes. But how can we tell we're overdoing at the time? Stuff needs to be done. I live alone. Im not supposed to vacuum the stairs or change my own bedding, because the angle of bend required aggravates the herniated discs in my back. But i also dont have money to hire a maid to do this atuff for me. And I absolutely refuse to live in a dirty environment. The place isnt sparkling but its ok. It wouldn't be ok if i didn't "overdo things".
Its a strange thing to diacuss. Theres so much qrong in the world and we often feel that our difficulties are not as urgent as they really are.
Anyway, I love your lovely dog.
Keep well,
Sleep deep and dream well.

author: @Imperfect_Stranger
text:
I've had Fibromyalgia for 43 years and it seems I may actually be ADHD as well. Fibro has so many symptoms that rotate or just turn up every 2, 5, 10 years as well as the amount of time I experience each symptom has absolutely no pattern or regularity. This makes other medical issues so hard to identify let alone whether you're just having a bad Fibro day or really need medical help. 

I have never understood the 'Fibro flare ups' people talk about. I am always sick, nothing really 'flares up', some symptoms just rotate, but, the fatigue, pain, and cognitive issues are always there.

As for the medical field, forget it, very few doctors can do more than prescribe drugs. It's horrendous how many feign ignorance because we are in the too hard basket.

author: @rosaa658
text:
I totally understand you , 1000%. Because it took more than 20 years and after 3 opinion , one Dr 👨‍⚕️ finally listened . And spent a lot of time asking me questions, about my symptoms . And finally he did the 13 points test , over my body . 
When he finally gave me the diagnosis, I cried happy 😭tears . Because finally my condition had a name , Fibromyalgia   !
I wanted to hug 🤗 the Dr.  but he said no , Hospital rules . I was told by first Dr., that I just wanted drugs . 
The second Dr., told me it was all in my head . And that it could also be Peri menopause .  
depresión , well  in case I did get depression. Because no Dr., would listen . And some of us still get depressed , because you’re always in pain . And exercise helps a little , in my case . Hope whoever is having symptoms, please don’t give up change Dr’s . Get 3 opinions 
if need be till they listen to you . 
I refused to get medicine, because of bad side effects & I’m dealing with gastritis .
Thank you 🙏🏿 for sharing your experience , and some people don’t know, or haven’t heard what fibromyalgia is  !
❤️🙏🏿🤗😘👨‍⚕️😢

author: @JuneyJones
text:
It took her an HOUR to even be able to sit up and she was shaking and unable to speak, and not a single person thought to call 911?! 
At an absolute bare minimum, they should have insisted that she be driven to the hospital!

That's absolutely shocking and has pretty terrifying implications for the rest of the people who practice there. 

In most cases, the key to surviving a stroke and reducing the damage they cause, is getting help as quickly as possible. Every minute counts. In  a situation like this where no one knew to call 911, someone could have easily died or been left with severe brain damage due to their inaction. 
From an outside prospective it sounds like this place needs much better training on how to handle emergency situations and how to recognize the signs of major medical events, like strokes, seizures, heart attacks etc, that might happen on the mat.

author: @CathrynAnaGlenday-u7c
text: I was just about to add an 11th thing to your list of things you wish you were told prior to your BKA, don't have a stroke, and to my horror, I saw this video. My stroke was due to an autoimmune disease. I still want put in a plug for primary stroke prevention. Being an amputee complicates stroke recovery. Please watch your blood sugar, cholesterol, and blood pressure if you are an amputee. Amputation gave me my life back after 35 years of chronic pain and 25 reconstructive ankle surgeries. Strokes are brain injuries and brain injuries can be healed. A good speech therapist or a psychotherapist trained in Integrative Cognitive Rehab Psychotherapy, can help. It has been 5 years since I had total left sided paralysis. I am now about 85% recovered. Slowly, oh so slowly, brains heal.

Logically I knew that I couldn't cause her stroke, but I couldn't stop crying for a couple of days while she was in the ICU. In the end two good things came out of the stroke 1. minimum additional disability (she was already disabled) 2. the stroke allowed us to talk. We were no longer a couple, but we were the best of friends until she passed eight years ago.  BY THE WAY I'm in Littleton.

I can drive I can work Having a catch with my son with baseball is a little bit more difficult now but all in all, I’m incredibly lucky. I wish you continued health and success and my prayers go out to anyone who is a stroke survivor reading this as well. Take care of yourself watch your blood sugar watch your blood pressure. Keep the weight off

author: @lyfandeth
text:
I would say that we have to thank the NFL for bringing brain injuries to the public awareness over the past 20 years. All the issues with TBIs and concussion among the players have made many of uz more aware of what concussion can mean and do. And along with that, stroke awareness and the need for immediate attention to that type of brain injury as well. 
 Certainly way more so than it was 40 or even 25 years ago. And as part of that, doctors and therapists have learned more about what genuinely helps in the aftermath.

author: @Mikeye1234
text:
Your video came up as a YouTube suggested video, and I'm glad I watched it, because you have a platform as a you tube uploader which I am not.  but I though I'd use the comment section to tell my story.

Sorry this may end up being a lengthy post.   If you don't feel like reading my lengthy post, AT LEAST READ THE LAST LINE!

Earlier this year I had a stroke.  I am a 37 years old male and in extremely good shape (I run 20+ miles per week and work out 4 days per week, all with a healthy diet). I was sitting on my couch watching TV one night and I was hit by a sudden EXTREME  light headedness and I knew something real had just happened to me.  I am glad you said your remarks about not wanting to go to a hospital, because I felt the same EXACT way, and I've always felt shame for not doing anything about it that night, so it was comforting to see you make the same comments.  it was short lived, and after it was over I didn't feel much different than before (and no, it wasn't a TIA).  So I went to bed and went to work the following morning.  Fortunately for me, I worked at a plasma donation center which staffs a bunch of nurses.  As soon as they saw me that morning they MADE me to go to a hospital, even though that was the last thing in the world I wanted to do.  I did in fact have a stroke the previous night.

As a healthy, young man, after the doctors ran every test they could think of, they finally asked me about my personal life.  That is when they discovered the extreme amount of stress I've been living with over the last few years, and ultimately labeled it the cause.  This condition costed me my job (indirectly), and only increased the stress in my life.   So, Miss Footless Jo, while our causes may be different,  our stories are much the same.  It hit a nerve with me when you said you couldn't practice jujitsu anymore.  I can understand how much it must hurt to want to do something soooo bad but not be allowed to as it may cause another stroke.  That is a feeling that many people may sympathies with, but few understand.

I am just very glad that you are able to share your story with the world.  I suspected I had a stroke that night, but didn't want to go to the hospital for it.  I just hope that people reading this will have the courage to seek medical help when they feel something isn't right with them.  If I had done so that night, they MAY have been able to dissolve the clot that killed a portion of my brain (thalamus) and I wouldn't still, almost a year later,  be living with the symptoms.  

So thank you for this video, as it is comforting to me, as well as I'm sure countless others who share the same experience.

Since this is a long post, for anyone not bothering to read everything,  IF YOU FEEL SOMETHING IS WRONG, SEEK HELP!!!!!!

author: @PatriciaAShelton-no6cq
text: I totally get that you have issues going to the ER. Not counting wait time  just the overall experience . I have been thru so much this year. Diagnosed first of year with a rare cancer in my left ankle called leiomyosarcoma.  Only symptom i had was sharp pain occasionally while walking. To the point i would stop an it would immediately go away.Went thru several months of that and decided well i don't know what it could be but it isn't going away  thinking maybe it's just aging an over doing things since i am retired i spend hours most days gardening, not just vegetables but all kind of flowers shrubs trees. I also do all lawn work,including weed eating for up to two hours even in the heat of summer. I got into growing roses spending quite a lot on some varieties. But kids grown,retired i found something i loved to do. Went to an orthopedic Dr, first thing was an xray, from there something was seen,so sent for MRI  then after a week i went in for results  which led to an apology abd told they see something but don't know what it is,so they needed anothet MRI with contrast.  Following week i was told everyone in tge building had looked at my images  even got. an oncologist to look at them. That's when it hit me,bone cancer? No it wasn't coming from the bone,but i needed a special orthopedic surgeon/ oncology.  Only two in the state so no matter which one i chose the round trip would be two hours each way. So here goes a year of nothing but hospitals, tests,so many imaging done before an after radiation.  Against the recommendation of my surgeon, i went for limb sparing surgery,which meant i would need 5 weeks of radiation.  That started in May, everyday which was a hassle.  Only good thing,no waiting ,after more time tho before they started,it was getting in to see the Oncologist  and radiologist.  Blood work,more imaging more time just to get started. In between that i had to have bone density test an a vascular assessment.  Then a plastic surgeon to reconstruct my ankle. I was burned severely on my ankle,heel so bad i couldn't walk  so i got my first experience of what was to come. Finally surgery to remove the tumor was in August this year 2024,went home bandaged up on a wound vac.  Exactly a week later went in to have reconstruction surgery, where a flap was created by cutting fat and muscle from that side of leg, pulled down and some was just stitched to my leg above the ankle,the other was pushed into the wound .Went home same day. Had a stent running from under the foot  back of heel all the way up the back leg an ended right under the knee.  It was so painful, i could barely hang on for a week to go back and have a skin graft to cover it all. Yet another trip,having to always be there at 5 in the morning.  All had been on out patient.  Routine everything, before i went back to surgery i was asked did i know an understand what was being done today.  Yes an i explained about skin graft to cover what surgery i had a week ago.  Put under an woke in recovery an i was feeling my right thigh for bandage, cause that's where the graft would be taken from.  I didn't feel anything an i asked but got no real answer. Dr had gone, when i finally woke up better i realize i was on a wound vac  again. The surgeons assistant came by and tokd me unfortunately the flap died because bandage around stent to hold it in place was too tight and had cut off the blood supply.  Why my foot was so swollen an a bad sore had created on top of foot.  Nothing left  to do except amputate.  Well all this an i had not seen my foot because it had always been bandaged up until next surgery. I had wound care to come to the house for several days until i went in for amputation.  When wound came the first day an unwrapped my leg,i was horrified.  Words can't explain what my leg loojed like,not to mention the gapping hole in my ankle, where i coukd see bone and tendons.  So on to the next surgery.  Stayed one more day,sebt home the next. I had already purchased a potty chair an a nice walker i used to get around with ,The walker had a seat, so i put my wound vac machine under the seat storage, bent my knee onto the seat and pushed myself around like on a scooter.  But none of that would work now, so i got a wheelchair two days later. It was finally good just to get up and be mobile. It was good to be able to cook food. I had done without so much, between not feeling good, an all the weeks of surgeries which turned into 6 weeks back to back,the traveling so far i was about done for.  I kept my spirits up but it's so hard at times.  I just want ti get back to some kind of normal.  I got my finished prosthetic two days ago.  My knee hurts so bad,an all i was told is get physical therapy somewhere.  I put the prosthetic on an wear it for two hours,walking with my walker.  I can walk without it,but the pain is so bad i am afraid to.  It's quite heavy  heavier than i thought. It's a lot like yours, air pressure an all. I am so sorry you are going thru this, you've conquered so much with such a positive outlook.  You're going to bounce back once again. As for me ,my last visit with my orthopedic surgeon, she wanted me to go have blood work and more images done. I just looked at her and said, " for what?" Looking for cancer? And what then? Chemo doesn't work on this cancer,only treatment is to chop off wherever it is. Maybe lung,kidney, another limb. Nope i am done. I am sure if it apoears I'll know.  But it will be too late then,well there's always hospice. I refuse to spend whatever time I have left in hospitals being sick,when i could be home living just as long without doing anything.  Actually my 5 year survival was good if i had done nothing, but i wasn't tokd that until it was all over with. I think people have had leiomyosarcoma for up to ten years in their leg before seeking treatment.  Then it's the same treatment.  Limb sparing if its located in thigh,for there's more room to get a clear margin.  So little is known about this cancer. It's so rare, most oncologist will never have a case. I think it's something like 4 cases may be diagnosed each year world wide. Only cause known is from radiation. How buzzare,when i was on radiation for 5 weeks.  Come to find out, some oncologist won't use radiation on patients because of that. Thinking it could make it spread.  All we can do is move on,learn from our experiences. Now you-know-what to look for about strokes, i know more about this rare cancer,and how i would have handled it. You're young, you got a lot of living to do.  You are active so you will bounce back and be doing great. I came looking at your videos about prosthetics. As i went thru watching from the beginning, is how i came to this video. When i feel bad,i listen to Rise Up. Whew,but some days it's just so hard.❤

author: @whitney4646
text: I’m so sorry this happened to you but I have to thank you for posting this video. I’m 24, never had any health issues more than the flu, don’t smoke, eat a vegetarian/pescatarian diet, started training for a marathon earlier this year, and 4 weeks ago I had a severe stroke. At my worst I couldn’t move the entire left side of my body or speak at all (only moan). Luckily my husband was home with me and noticed less than 15 minutes from me having the stroke that he needed to drag me to the ER. I’ve made a full recovery already but mentally I’m still terrified every day. It’s been comforting to me to hear other’s stories as I don’t know anyone around my age who has had something like this happen to them.

author: @VariantAEC
text:
I've had a lot of head injuries.
Scans nearly two decades later, showing abnormal lesions were noted as congenital by the doctor.

I used to have frequent headaches for a very long time. For most of my youth from around age 8 to age 17, I had nightly migraines. Almost a decade after becoming an adult is when I had my first CT scan for confusion and notable memory issues. Then, an MRI, and both showed abnormalities, one was a tumor that doctors missed, which was finally 'discovered' 8 years later. Thankfully, the tumor is not growing, but it might not pass as benign as it might be affecting me in other ways. The jury is still out on that front.

If doctors don't know what's wrong, they can't help you. They also don't generally have the experience to know when something is really wrong. I did run into a male nurse who suspected my neck was causing my migraines and recommended getting that looked at. I eventually did, and my neck was messed up. It's probably also from the head injuries, but the doctors list it as congenital. Every cervical joint has moderate damage in my spine.

The doctors insist that this shouldn't cause pain. A less than a year later, my back is in a similar state. All of a sudden, a few lumbar joints being out of wack is a big deal even though they're in a similar state as the least damaged joint in my neck without any burst discs or dislocations of any spinal joint then or now.

Doctors need to make up their minds... I think the only reason they don't take neck pain seriously is that in the US, you can not readily claim disability for neck injuries. Why? I really don't know. Maybe that type of injury is too common is my first thought.

[Edit: Two words were mispelled, not anymore.]

author: @edwardfortier2335
text: My stroke was very different then yours, I woke up one morning got out of bed, started making the bed. And realized my right arm and hand were crossing my left one, I knew exactly what was going on but my arm and hand were not functioning right, and I couldn’t talk but I could walk straight and text on the phone I just couldn’t talk… when I took a sip of water it dripped out the right side of my face… when the ambulance showed up I could walk knew where I was could text just couldn’t talk… come to find out I was in a-fib and threw a clot and my blood pressure was through the roof, I hadn’t taken my blood thinners in about 6 months because I didn’t want to or had time to visit doctors because of work… when I went to the hospital I knew exactly where I was could answer there questions by text but still couldn’t talk, they ran an mri and said I have a clot the size of a pen cap, and gave me an IV of some experimental medication and by 9:00 that night I could talk move like nothing had ever happened… since then I make sure  take my medication regularly and I’m scheduled for an ablation so this doesn’t happen ever again…

author: @MOAB-UT
text:
You really should learn to PLAY an instrument. This will help with neuroplasticity- which you need. You are getting some understandable cognitive fatigue. It will change day by day- did for me. Over time, this can improve a lot. I have been there- not a stroke, but brain injury. Bounce a ball when you walk- as much as you can. Check pupil size. I have one that is bigger- that is the blurrier eye.

You are doing great.

author: @Bearthalamass
text: I told my girl friend i was feeling funny, and looked right at her and my vision looked literally like tunnel vision, with just a little bit of vision in the center, and everything black around it, then the black took over what little vision i had. Then it got to the point i couldnt control my tongue and make words. Or control my head, i remember my head was pointed towards the ceiling and i couldnt point it, straighten it, my tongue was just rolling around in my head and so was my eyes i think....and i put everything i had into it and shook my head real hard, and it all went away. I didnt go to the hospital for two days. I was tired but felt fine except i felt like i was drunk. I would be walking down the hall bouncing off the wall like i was drunk. No matter how hard i tried, i couldnt make it down the hall in my house without sometimes walking face first into it. When i went to the hospital they told me i had had a stroke. Bout 4 months later i had a widow maker heart attack. The life flighted me to houston where a doctor named mel gibson did surgury on my heart, and said all 4 arteries were almost completely blocked, 98, 99%. I wasnt overweight at all. Just a bad diet.

author: @andrewoats
text: I had six strokes a few years ago. In hindsight I should have recognized the first five but I was only 43 and active so it didn’t cross my mind till number six gave me more classic symptoms. One of them was during karate class, my vision went totally black on one side for several minutes but I figured I was just tired. I’m still dealing with deficits but I kept training and I just take a break if needed, usually during weapons because waving sai around while your hand spasms isn’t smart. My first black belt was about six months after and when my instructor mentioned that there were gasps from family members in the audience. It is crazy, like who even survives six strokes much less go on to earn a black belt?

author: @ClaytonLovendale
text: There is another martial art you should try, low impact, no, or very little contact, great exercise, great focus for you. Is Tai Chi. I love it, it helps with balance, it also helps with footwork and feeling an integration of my natural body with my prosthetic. I found it makes my right leg as close to natural as my left. I now practice for health, and to keep some practice in my life. It also is to keep up with self defense techniques. I also practice Arnis. Solo practice. Also for focus, movement, and exercise. So you can still practice martial arts. Just not the heavy stuff. Most of all, pace yourself. You get tired, rest. Of course check with your health team to get cleared. Make sure your coach, sensei, Sifu. Or trainer understands your requirements and is working with you, he knows what to do if you have an episode, and he doesn't let ego get in the way. Same for you, don't let ego get in the way. If you have had enough. You have had enough. STOP!!!!. Walk away. Don't pushit. Live long and prosper, may the force be with you. That last really invokes Trekkies to the outer limits.

author: @michaeljohnson9969
text:
I had three stokes, caused by a disintegrated heart valve due to an undetected infection .
  I was in a coma for awhile. But In six months I was walking a few dozen steps and feeding myself. 
  At the 8 month mark , a clot broke loose and I had seizures requiring necessitation. 
  I ex told me she was too young to take care of an invalid and divorced me. Took my kids too.
  It’s been ten years. There are parts of my body that still don’t work. 
  I have a part time job. I can drive. I can ride a bike . 
  What you said about working to appear to function normal while tons of stuff you are doing “manually” in the background of your mind is such a great description. Getting tired so easily… etc. You put that very eloquently . 
 You have the same denial I have. I use it to keep fighting for more. 
 People will forget what happened to you. They will see you improve and see you function, but they will forget how hard you are still working to be yourself. Always be proud of the work you have done , and remember that you now have PTSD from the emotional trauma that nobody understands that you still are working hard to function. Get help for that too. 
  Love yourself and take it a victory at a time.
