author: @misspillow3
text:
i got chills watching this. i understand exactly how it feels to not be able to control your body or see properly and not being able to process whats going on. 
6 months ago, on the way to the bathroom, my arms were jerking uncontrollably, my vision was like static, i felt sick to my stomach and ice cold, i couldnt think properly, and i ended up losing my vision entirely and collapsing.
4 months ago, while i was taking a shower, my upper body was jerking, then the next thing i knew i was on the floor with my arms tense against my body, my vision fuzzy, my mouth wide open, my hearing muffled, and not being able to think. then again i was suddenly pacing back and forth with fuzzy vision, intense feelings of deja vu, and derealization.
2 months ago, i woke up and my arms started jerking. i could hardly speak, then the next thing i knew i woke up to fuzzy vision, intense feelings of deja vu, derealization, not being able to think properly, and my mom there. she told me i had a seizure.
later that month i got an eeg, and my diagnosis came back as epilepsy, so im on meds now and im doing better.
nothing has ever been more terrifying than not being able to control your body, feeling weird sensations, and not being able to process whats going on. some of the things you said really reminded me of this and it resonates with me.
i really hope you're doing better 🤍

author: @gwillis9797
text:
You would think self-defense people, students, and masters would be calm enough to " spring into action " because they have defense training. WRONG!! 
SELF DEFENSE CLASS OF ANT SORT HAS NOTHING TO DO WITH TRAINING FOR CIVILIAN DRAMATIC SITUATIONS.
The same goes for owning a firearm. Owning and using one in a situation are two very different things. 
You would have to be trained not to ( freeze) and to help. That's two different things, too. Some people have the personality to react in dangerous or emergencies automatically, such as not freezing and reacting or not freezing and defending. 
Emergencies are dangerous, too, and being prepared is a lifesaver a lot of times.

author: @blazecal
text: Yeah, I had the same experience without the pain.  My arm went limp when I was typing a final paper for college, and I thought there was something wrong with the keyboard.  I can't remember if I went to the hospital the next day or the day after, but I drove to the hospital thinking I was ok to do that and promptly ran over a curb.  I got to the hospital and the admitting nurse said she thought that maybe I hurt the ulnar nerve in my arm.  She was about to let me go home when I said, "What about the disorientation?" She decided then to consult with the neurologist on call, and I was admitted right away.  The next day a friend came to visit me in the hospital and later told me that you sound more like 'you' now).I kept thinking I had forgotten to bring my contact lenses with my because I couldn't read the phone, but I had.  There was nothing in my family history, or anything in my body that gave any indication that this was going to happen to me.  I asked the cardiovascular specialist, "What happened to me?  How serious was this?" He looked at me for a moment and said, "Well, if you hadn't had a stroke, I'd say you were perfectly healthy." The only thing that came to mind was the tremendous amount of stress since I had since I had just gone back to college for the first time in 45 years.  I can see the gray spot of dead brain in my CT scan (or was the CAT?)  I have trouble spelling.  My typing is not as easy as it used to be.  The third finger on my right hand doesn't work quite right, and as a result, it makes it really hard to play the Native American flute -- which is really a shame since I had gotten in the habit of playing it a few minutes a day every day for a couple of years which is very rare for me.  The worst is that I have trouble keeping my schedule for classes -- or anything else -- is difficult.  I've walked into the right classroom on the wrong day, and I've shown up 30 minutes late convinced that I was on time -- it felt like things were disappearing or changing on my calendar by itself.  I'm getting better slowly, I think,, although I've done little therapy on my hand/finger and really need to get back to that. And, yes, I am a little scared that I might have another and that this time it will be worse, and I won't be able to finish college.  I'm 68 years old.

text:
Each person is unique as are you interests so what I would like to write are just some suggestions for you to think about maybe something gels with you maybe not.
I am a swimmer, I love swimming and I used to do Karate, I have to admit the reason I prefer swimming over any other sport is becasue when you are in the water its you against you, it's a world that is so quiet and private that it is relaxing. Of course swimming is awesome exercise and you can go from casual low energy to super high energy exercise yourself.
The other thing is you art, I cannot draw to save my life but I have art in my head and the only way I found I can get it out is by using a computer. I know people say using a computer is not art but I disagree, what my hands cannot do on paper they can do using a mouse or drawing pad and keys on the keyboard. I don't get enough time to do this but when I do I get so immersed in the process I forget where I am. 
So my suggestion is if you cannot get your art out through your hands, see if you can get it out using the amazing tools we have available today, computers.

author: @jeffreyquinn3820
text: I had a very similar experience twenty-some years ago about twelve hours after a car accident.  There was no evidence of arterial dissection or stroke, which was the first thing they looked for as I was shoulder checking (while parked) and was rear-ended by someone going easily 20kph over the speed limit.  The likelihood was swelling in the same parts of the brain supplied by the vertebral artery, so no black holes on brain scans, but possibly a lot of micro-damage. I lost fine motor skills in my right hand.  (I also needed eye surgery & couldn't put full weight on my left leg for two years, but those weren't brain-connected.)  I had the strange experience of, several months after learning a skill with my left hand, it would return to my right hand.  (I still can't write cursive, but I think that just means I'm Gen Z and no longer Gen X.)  It was a good 5 years before I could track anything with my eyes.  (Yes, that's a completely different part of the brain.)  I guess the point I'm slowly rambling towards is that even 20+ years later, I'm still making occasional recovery & progress.  Anything that challenges my brain in other ways generally helps, which is true for pretty much everyone over the age of 12.  Other than that, I found that there's a sizable minority of people who are offended by a disability that isn't immediately visible, and that being an asshole politely back to them can be very therapeutic.  (Apparently it's my fault they criticized my writing ability because I wasn't wearing a sign . . .  )

author: @briandeschene8424
text:
I’ve never watched your postings before and did not know of them at all. No idea why YT added this to my recommendations. But watched it all. In case there was some purpose behind it, I feel that I need to do two things.

1) Give you financial support since you commented that this support gave you peace of mind and allowed you to concentrate on healing. This is most important since you are most important.

2) Tell you that I had to give up my running because of a knee injury. I was told that the impact of numerous footfalls would cause it to recur because of my knee’s particular configuration, Eventually disability/surgery. I always “knew” I wasn’t “normal” but sheesh!  :-)

It’s ten years later and I’ve replaced it with mountain biking which is now essential to my mental and physical wellbeing. Best of luck on your alternative journey.

author: @Spacemonkeymojo
text:
Arterial dissection in BJJ can occur if someone is trying to really crank a rear-naked choke or is really trying to go for your neck hard. There are scientific journals online that describe it. I don't get why people roll so hard at gyms ffs, it's not a god damn competition environment where you're trying to compete for a medal. People who roll hard are egotistical idiots who don't actually understand what BJJ is, you're not supposed to brute force your way into submitting someone else, "jiu jitsu" literally means "the gentle art" and if anything you are relying on your opponent to waste all their energy, you aren't supposed to be expending all your energy like an idiot trying to submit them. There is a really bad culture in a lot of BJJ gyms where people tend to roll hard for absolutely no reason and it is usually ALWAYS the newbies who do it. 

Shame on the moron who put you in that position. If you haven't told them what they did to you, you need to tell them or get them banned from the gym, BJJ doesn't need people like that around. I cannot believe some egotistical moron robbed someone else of jiu jitsu during a basic rolling session because they had some weird ass point to prove against a smaller person of the opposite sex. Like wtf, fk that dickhead.

author: @kathiehope
text:
My dad had a massive stroke just over two years ago. Unfortunately his stroke happened during the night and it was hours before he got treatment. he now lives in a high care facility as it left him completely paralysed on his left side and with cognitive impairments, he needs more support than we are able to provide. We’re just grateful he survived, that he can speak and that his gentle and kind personality hasn’t changed. 

Thank you so so much for sharing your experience. Many people know the common signs of stroke but there are so many ways a stroke can impact you based on where in your brain has been impacted. There’s more and more research now about the cerebellum also being involved with cognition and language, the right cerebellum specifically for language. Just talking about your experiences will help so many people understand stroke better! 
Thank you again 🧡 I hope your recovery continues smoothly and that you enjoy dancing!

author: @carriehollyland3596
text:
This is what I am battling right now.
Had a recent fall on the steps (didn't hit my head, no broken bone) I ended up with whiplash that strained the muscles in my neck and damaged my vertebral artery causing an artery dissection with hematoma and severe stenosis of that artery.
I previously have suffered from vertigo and migraines prior to this fall.
But they both have become much worse since the fall.
I now get blind spot auras with my migraines (which is not how my auras normally were prior to the fall).
The migraines also make me extremely nauseous, even to the point I am getting sick.
I have lost a decent amount of weight now because of it.
The muscles in my neck keep spasming, which is extremely painful, and makes any movement, including even yawning, swallowing, or speaking, very painful and makes me light headed and dizzy.
The very first migraine blind spot aura that I had since the fall happened only in my right eye and lasted for about 3 hours.
I have had more since but those ones last an hour or less and occur in both eyes at the same time.
The doc doesn't think I have had a stroke yet, but they have me on a blood thinner trying to prevent one from happening, and they have also changed up a lot of my other meds due to them causing an increased risk of stroke.

author: @MissxLariz
text: I had a strange experience when i was somewhere between 20-23 years old and im not sure if it was some kind of small stroke or just a glitch in my brain. One day i stood up from the couch and stopped in front of the tv because suddenly the news was talking about something interesting. Out of nowhere my head felt weird im not sure if it slightly hurt for a moment but i know i felt a little light in the head and my legs felt heavy. Maybe i also got slightly nauseous (can also be because i was a bit scared of this strange phenomenon). It was only for like 5 secs and after it was gone i wanted to tell my mom about this feeling but i couldnt speak normally. Its like i couldnt even understand my own language anymore and i tried to talk but i barely could form a sentence. While my thoughts made sense my speech was struggling to say words. At this moment i felt weird, im not sure how to describe that feeling. My mom thought i was trolling but after a few minutes it was gone and i could talk normal again. I explained my mom wat happend and she doesnt know either. She just waved it off like it was nothing. So far i havent experienced it again.

author: @CarlVandenberg
text:
Thanks for sharing your experience. I had an embolic stroke in February and I could relate to most if not all of what you went through. I actually woke up having the stroke and I couldn't understand why I couldn't get out of bed or grab my phone. I was finally able to sit up and use my phone to call 911. I didn't completely lose the ability to use my right limbs (the stroke affected my right side) yet I still feel the effects today (numbness, tingling, weakness). I also have enough dizzy spells and poor balance that I still haven't gone back to driving yet.

Thanks again for sharing your experience. I don't remember a lot of what happened during those early days after the stroke and in a way you filled in some gaps for me. :)

author: @feiryfella
text:
I'm STAGGERED no one called you an ambulance, simply speechless!. My best friend was staying with me once. He came to my room and said 'my legs feel weird' I noticed his speech was a bit off, so i made him dial 119 (non-urgent line) so I could hear him talk more. He kept slurring so I grabbed the phone off him, made him do the rest of the FAST test-he couldn't raise both arms, so I said 'He's had a stroke, send everyone', we had a bike responder there in 10 minutes and and ambulance 10 mins later. He was in A&E in under the 'Golden Hour', we were met by a stroke speciialist immediadtely who stayed with him through scans, tests etc, made sure he had clot busting drugs which you need WITHIN AN HOUR to be truly effective! He was in hospital for 2 months, had a load of intense physio and he recovered. Love the NHS! x
What happened to you is CRIMINAL! I know you love your dojo etc, but their ignorance and dereliction of care towards you is terrifying! I understand your reluctance to seek help once you were home, but you should already have been in ICU!

author: @charlottebuddhistvihara1354
text:
I was washing dishes one day, at 56 years old and in overall good health, when suddenly I felt as though a hand slapped me *hard* upside the back of my head with a a loud *pop* sound. Dizziness hit, and I couldn't stay on my feet. I stumbled to a seat on the floor, and prayed. 

Called a friend who expressed skepticism assuring me it's nothing, I'm just imagining things. Then called another friend who came immediately to take me to the ER. 

Walked into the ER while my friend parked, and timidly informed the lady at the desk that I think maybe I had a stroke. I had to repeat myself, then her eyes widened and she reached for a phone, while gesturing me towards the waiting area. Before I even got seated someone came and was escorting me into the back. 

They took my symptoms very, very seriously and started doing tests followed by more tests. The doctors insisted that I stay overnight to get one more scan to rule out some kind of brain stem stroke that they said would specially match my symptoms.

The next day, after that last scan, I received a diagnosis of a migraine and a prescription for dizziness (Meclizine). I'm prone to migraines, but this stroke imitator was a new version that I had never before heard of or experienced. 

So I understand why this healthy young lady didn't want to take her symptoms seriously. But anything like these symptoms could be life-changing, and the ER staff will not scoff. If it's a stroke every minute counts; it's safer to get it checked out promptly than to risk being sorry later. 
- Ayya S.

author: @retim9290
text:
Not really sure if I had a stroke or not but I’ve done something ridiculously absurd to myself just recently. I was drinking alot with my friends and didn’t eat as much so I threw up a lot after. 

After that, the right side of my jaw was aching a lot. Thought it was a toothache at first but I felt the pain crept up to the joint that was connected from the jaw to the cheekbone. Read up about it and it was called Temporomandibular joint dysfunction(TMJ).

I Waited at a few days and thought I was ok, so I my wax pen really hard like three times. That’s when the right side of my face tightened up and felt like it was ripping apart. You could definitely see that one side of my face was thinner than the other. Pain was real bad but, I was able to bear it. 

It was when I drunk water with a closed mouth that hurted me the most. 

I recalled an immense surge pain that that shot out from my neck, to my face ripping jaw and up to my temple. It was so bad that I started sweating a lot and my heart was beating faster than I thought it ever could.

Feeling real dizzy I stumbled to my couch in the living room and layed down to watched my sister play her video games. 

I layed down there in a trance with a heart beating like a rockstar drummer performing his solo. I thought I was going to pass out right there and pass on.

Fortunately I was able to slow my heart rate down but then I couldn’t hear it beat at all afterwards. All I heard was static and rain fall for a few minutes. 

 I slowly forced myself up because I had a feeling that if I stayed down, I wouldn’t have gotten up..didn’t go to the hospital in which I should have.

The first few days, my little member was hurting after urinating. It went away after because I had drunk lots of water to filter out waste/toxins (or something of the sort). Not 100% sure if it’s gone but it doesn’t hurt anymore.

But, The left side of my body is now weaker than my right side. My heart has been randomly palpating through out the day. Ive been getting random back and abdomen pain, constipation, blurry vision (like what was said in this video), brain fog. My armpits and groin area had a small purple lump on them in which I  think are called swollen lymph nodes. I’ve done a lot of research to try and “self-diagnose” my self because hospitals are expensive and I can’t afford to stress my people out anymore than the stress they are bearing now.  Looked up a lot of the internal organs relating to the heart like the brain, kidneys and others. 

I eventually told my parents. I wasn’t sad that I’d gotten it, I was sad that I had to tell them what I did in case something really did happen to me. I thought they’d be disappointed but it was quite the opposite and liberating. 

It’s been a week now and I’m still suffering from the damage I’ve done to myself. Haven’t went to the hospital yet but I’ve been following a healthier diet now, drinking LOTS of WATER(which helped with the random back and abdominal pain), taking more short walks and breathing excercises. I’ve also stopped my bad habits and have been focusing towards my health now because I never knew how important it was until that moment. 

I’m thankful that I’m still alive today and got to tell my family that I love them. 

I don’t know what to expect next but I’m focusing more on the positive aspects of things now. I will also go to the hospital now if I really must and have applied for Medi-Cal

Tough read but, May we all live our best life 🙏.

author: @UltimateGoldenGirl
text: Thank you so very much for sharing. I got sick in 2011 2 days before my husband was moving to another state for a year and then me and my 3 children would follow a year later. I got sick. Had no idea what was happening. I had "every symptom in the book" where it was thought it was psychosomatic and I began to believe that for a short time. I shook quivered was numb and felt a chocking sensation. My doctor told me it was menopause. I got worse and worse. After 4 months of deterioration I drove myself to the ER on Thanksgiving night when my husband was home to watch the children. I told them my mother who had died came to me in a dream and told me I had a lesion on the left side of the brain (I thought that meant cancer)....so the doctor listened and gave me an MRI or CAT scan and as I told the woman who was scanning me what my mother said she was fascinated as she was looking at my brain on her end. I get back to the ER, and the doctor said "Mamm, your mother was right you have a lesion on the brain and we are going to keep you. Is your mother a doctor? I said "no she passed 2 years ago." That dream saved my life. They kept me in the hospital and it took me about a year to get totally through it. It was the scariest time of my life while raising my 3 small children. I am so grateful to be thriving. I take life one day at a time. Thank you for your video.

author: @robertwhiteley-yv1sy
text: You probably had a clot caused by your leg surgery that had been there for a good while and moved while you were training.  Right hemisphere strokes are more difficult to recover from than left hemisphere strokes. If you have a left hemisphere stroke the right hemisphere can learn to take on new duties usually taken care of by the left but the left hemisphere can’t learn right hemisphere duties. The left hemisphere actually insists that the right hemisphere doesn’t exist. You seem fine but I would consider getting close family members to ask you questions that require understanding of implied meaning. Those who have right hemisphere strokes seem fine but their understanding of reality is broken.

text:
Hi, my Mom had an occipital stroke.  She thought it was a migraine too, but Dad made her go to ER after 24 hrs.  There was no injury for her, but afterwards, she couldn't see the left side of her plate at the hospital.  She joked that her brain put her on a diet.  The OT put a red ribbon on the left side of the plate where a napkin would be, and she just had to scan until she saw it. It returned to normal after her brain healed.  She still has trouble following conversations.  

Your crochet projects look good.  Have you tried knitting? Bilateral things help more with neuroplacicity than unilateral ones.  It might help you get your drawing back.

author: @marywenzel3199
text: You are an inspiration to me as someone with a motor disability and a tendency to feel sorry for myself.   You are one tough lady and so animated.  Your attitude is amazing. I’m sorry about the loss of your beloved jujitsu but I believe you will be a kick ass dancer and make it look easy and stylish despite your prosthetic. I was wondering if you enjoy swimming because that seems like it would be an ideal exercise in the lower gravity environment. You certainly want somebody to help you keep from slipping on the wet deck but when you’re in the water, you are a cushion from injury and the motions of swimming could also help to heal your brain pathways. As an orthopedically compromised person, swimming was recommended to me as an ideal exercise for someone with balance and mobility concerns because you’re fully supported by the water.  I will be rooting for you and your recovery. I guess you could say that I also have a TBI since my diagnosis is cerebral palsy that affects my gait, balance and leg strength.  I can mitigate these a little through exercise but this is what I have to live with. I have a feeling that 13:23 you might surprise yourself and make an awesome recovery.  You might also consider Tai chi, Which will have some of the elements of the martial arts discipline you’ve had to give up but it’s gentle with no jarring and very minimal risk of falls. Chair yoga is also very forgiving. You have a new subscriber. Keep staying awesome!

author: @laylahope.
text:
I wonder if contact improvisation would be allowed for you. It's a form of improvisational partner dance that reminds me of jui jitsu in how being in contact with somebody, listening to your body & their body & adjusting accordingly gets you in that flow state.

Either way that's amazing. I also found dance after a slightly less severe "cerebrovascular event" and a lifetime of illness/disability. It's allowed me to practice experiencing my embodied existence as play-- the body as a playground, not a prison, and disability as a creative constraint, not an unfair limitation.

You and others sharing their stories has made me feel so much less alone in this. Thanks & wishing you all the happiness and health.

We always think it wont happen to me it only happens to other people until it does I guess and that feeling of oh no this is actually really really bad is terrifying I've felt that before more than a few times and  I'm sorry you had to as well I can't imagine what a stroke would be like  ima also an artist and love to draw id like to suggest doing art on the computer that you may be able to do the tools work in a way that would be easier for you possibly in things like illustrator there's a site photopea that's a free version you can try. Glad you are still here you are such a strong person it's amazing wish you only the best and that things get better for you.

author: @johnprezioso270
text: I had my stroke 4 years ago. I recovered about 10-15% from how I was immediately after but now it is virtually impossible that I will get anything else back. My life is utterly destroyed, with no hope for recovery. I don't say this for sympathy, but because I want you to know that this is not your future and to stay positive. You are so young and energetic (and are obviously used to adversity given your previous disability) and don't ever stop believing that you will be best you that you can be. If I were given a choice between you or me being permanently disabled, I would choose it for myself every time. You are an inspiration and you will do many more wonderful things. Love from New Jersey.

author: @tietosanakirja
text:
I may know a fraction of the fear you must have felt. After the acute "plague," I struggled for a long time to think about anything complex, listening to people, or follow a plot on a TV show. I got really tired really fast, and was unable to work. I thought my brain was done for.

It's scary to hear how similar some of the symptoms were to a stroke, but also, wow, stroke sounds infinitely more scary! I can't even imagine.

(Sorry. This is me processing the feelings this video brings up and parts where it intersects with my experience. I really don't mean to compare or claim to know what you are going through.)

author: @ronmaximilian6953
text:
I'm glad that you're here and making new content. I'm sorry about the difficulty that you've been having since you had a stroke. 
I don't know what kind of migraines you get, but complex migraines can mimic some of the symptoms of a stroke. Unfortunately I know this first hand both from going to the ER with a migraine thinking I had a stroke, and going to the ER with a stroke that I hoped was just a migraine. In December 2020,.I got the not cold going around and it caused my blood pressure to go out of control. This caused me to go from two migraines a week to a migraine everyday so I went to the neurologist, where I found out about the problem and was put on blood pressure medication. Unfortunately, it caused a dizziness especially when getting up. So a week later, when I thought I had what was a bad migraine and was extremely dizzy I didn't really think of it at first. In fact I took my rise of triptan which probably make things worse. About an hour later, I was floored by blinding pain when I got up to get some water. Fortunately, that prompted me to call 911, which didn't properly work because I couldn't talk properly. I ended up sort of getting dressed and dragging myself into my buildings lobby where I was able to get help and an ambulance. It turns out that it had other mini strokes and attributed them to migraines before the major one.  I am no way I'm comparing myself to you, but it was certainly humbling to use my late father's cane for a year, once I was able to walk with assistance. Learning how to write and type again was a lot of fun.  I'm lucky to be mostly recovered and have the ability to train interns and new employees who can do a lot of the physical IT work, which I'm no longer able to do.      
I had to all but give up hobbies of computer gaming and shooting. I've had to find new hobbies and ,, when in-game different types of games.  I don't have the same memory I once did and I have to give myself a lot of extra time to learn new things and reinforce them so I'll remember them. And a year into recovering, my mother and quick procession got cancer And then had a stroke herself. But I had to move on without complaining for her sake and because my sister, who had recently given birth was in no position to help. Sometimes, life gives us a lemons And when we make lemonade we get food poisoning. But there's no choice other than to move on. Sometimes it's a step at a time, sometimes it's a leap, and sometimes it's a leap forward and a step back.


I could never imagine going on video talking about my issues. Your braver person than I am and I'm sure that you have the tenacity to slowly recover or at least make the best of your abilities.

author: @Angi_Mathochist
text:
I'm glad you're feeling better. It sucks that this has taken away Jujitsu from you. That's just the worst. :( I hope your dance, or something else, will bring something similar back into your life.

It's so weird that I came across this video the other day (earmarked it, just found time to watch it today) just at random, because... I had a stroke this week, too. Mine was nothing like yours -- I probably had the most minor stroke in the history of strokes that have ever been noticed, probably. Here's my story, if you want to read it.

Strokes usually happen early in the morning, so it's possible that while mine actually happened, I could have been asleep. Or maybe that's what woke me up that morning. There was no pain or anything, that I knew of. I just woke up losing words. Not many words, just certain ones, but common ones that I use every day. The first thing was that after taking one of my meds (empty stomach) I usually set an alarm for 20 minutes to take the rest, and I for some reason couldn't figure out how to set the alarm. I just sat there thinking "okay, now I need to set my alarm" and then not doing it, and 5 minutes later looking at the clock and going "um, I'm waay too tired, I need to set the alarm before I fall asleep" and then not doing it. I couldn't really put my finger on *why* I wasn't setting the alarm. I thought I was just really tired. I wound up just sitting through the 20 minutes and taking my meds, and thinking I'd feel better when I got more sleep. Later my wife came in to water some plants, and she left a curtain open, but I couldn't tell her what she left open because I couldn't remember the word for "curtain". I finally just waved her off and said something like never mind, too sleepy. I have Lupus and Fibromyalgia and I'm on pain meds, and I"m in my 50's, so losing a word here and there is par for the course.

Later I got up to feed the dogs. I got the dogs in their crates and my wife walked in while I was standing there trying to figure out how to turn the lights on in the room. They're all on verbal commands and I could not remember the commands. She asked what was wrong and I said I could not remember how to turn the lights on. I pointed to which ones and she turned them on. I was able to feed the dogs but I was a little slow measuring things up. I asked my wife to make me a shake for breakfast, when she got a chance -- but I think I gestured. I did not have the word for "shake". A bit later, she had forgotten, and I texted her (far end of the house) to remind her, and I had to Google to find the word. I looked up fries... then it took me forever to find the word for hamburger in my mind... then I scrolled down and found a restaurant I knew should serve shakes, scrolled down its page, and finally found the word for shake. When she brought me the shake, I told her I still couldn't remember the name of the light  next to me, or the name of the smart computer that sits next to me, and then when she told me the name of the light, it sounded wrong and I didn't believe her until she turned it on and off for me.

We were both starting to think something was wrong by this point. She called my doctor and the nurse was offering us appointment times the following day or later. Stupid nurse -- someone should talk to her about that, I think. She wound up sending us to an Urgent Care. This was like 6-7 hours after first symptoms. Everything I see online says if you have signs of a stroke, don't even get someone else to drive you -- get an ambulance, because they can start care in the ambulance that can save valuable time. By the time I got to care, the timeframe for getting the care that could have stopped things from getting worse (if they were going to get worse) was long past. We didn't notice things were wrong because the symptoms were so mild, but the nurse should have noticed and sent us straight to the ER ASAP! Which is what the Urgent Care doc did.

The ER doc thought I was probably having something related to my Lupus, since I didn't have any other signs of stroke other than a few missed words. But I had a CAT scan ASAP. And he came back surprised. Yep, I had a stroke. 2 brain bleeds, one in language, one further back. I spent the next 2 days in the hospital getting multiple tests done. They don't seem to know what caused it yet. Followed up with my Rheumatologist today and will see Neuro next week for a more in-depth heart test. It's possible my Lupus has something to do with the Stroke. I'm anemic and my platelets are low -- those are chronic -- and those could be part of the cause. Now I get to take baby aspirin every day, when I've always been told by my Hematologist NOT to take aspirin because of my low platelets. But you gotta do what you gotta do. 

On the up side, all my words came back within a day. If this is the only stroke I ever have, that's fine by me -- it wasn't that bad, just a little scary not knowing what was going on. The really scary part is knowing that it could happen again, and next time it could be much worse.

author: @theprodigalson4003
text:
I’m here for you jo. Thankyou for sharing. It’s 4 am where i am. And ive been doing a lot of thinking tonight. Seeing you there on the couch, knowing where you may be, competing in jiujitsu if not for circumstance, knowing you undestand. And that if none of these things happened to you, you and I both could’ve been like those nurses or people that make you so averse to asking for help or sharing anything about what’s going on. It makes me hopeful. Those people that look down on us because they can walk just fine, or their hands work fine so why can’t yours, they don’t get it, and we do. Let that give you hope. You sharing, me watching. I become better from tbings like this. So Thankyou, earlier tonigjt I wasn’t sure I wanted to continue, but I think I just found a reason to 

The only way others can undestand a burn is if they feel it themeslves, or someone does a really really good job of explaining the pain and why they wanted to touch it to begin with. We can make the world better, slowly, baby steps just like recovery, but we can. Keep sharing. And don’t stop fighting. Keep putting that one foot forward, I believe in us

author: @mortalgaytor
text: I've had 3 or 4 large cysts removed from the side and back of my neck (thanks cystic acne!), and being that it was the Navy that removed them, they were done sloppily and without caring about the looks, and even incompletely so that they came back and had to be removed again. So I've got several gnarly looking scars across the back of my neck. Thankfully, they've faded over the years and aren't as bad. But back when people would ask about them, I'd casually say I was stabbed in a knife fight and just leave it at that without further explanation. More recently, I had another cyst removed right next to my nipple, so I guess I'll have to come up with a story for getting stabbed in the boob if I ever find someone who wants to see me topless 😂

author: @ellerj641
text:
I have a scar on my neck. I was born with a hole in my neck and my mom said that when she would feed me, milk would dribble out of it. She was told it would be a very minor surgery and a small bandaid. I came out with a huge bandage. 

For years I had this scar. But when I reached a certain age, it started causing a lot of problems. Apparently, it looked like a hicky. I got so much sexual harassment for it, that I decided to remove it. Well, it got removed, but now I have a scar that's a line going across part of my neck. It now looks like someone tried to slice me. But no one bothers me for it anymore.

author: @kathleentaylor8840
text: Hi, Jo, I know it’s not really funny, but the joke would be a razor blade suicide attempt?  I’m just getting caught up with your million sub fame at YouTube now just hours after you posted about your stroke, I’m so sorry, my plantar fasciitis evolved into neuropathy that I am like 24/7 treating with various types of pain management.  I don’t always know when something is working but seems to be working at least for a while, then I change it up, really important to change up routines to improve them I find.  You are the most high energy person I have ever known.  You are absolutely unique, and me too.  You and I are so completely different - it must be impossible with your energy drive to accept less risky behaviors, while I am obsessed with minimizing risk.  And I truly am naturally an introvert, while I know you are truly outgoing with the public, and I really do hope you feel better.  Now that you already do have a million subs you could enjoy books more, like books are a safer sport, right?

author: @iknowiwillforget
text: A scar will not overshadow your natural beauty, your kind demeanor, or your effervescent spirit. If it puts them off, lucky you; you won’t have to waste your time weeding out flimsy  knuckleheads and porous characters. And another thing—> I’ve been an amputee for a long time and at some point I came to understand that it’s not about me; they are not looking at me and judging me. They are totally consumed by their own wants and fears, ambitions and desires, and their deep anxieties over the uncertainties of life. They don’t have time to judge me; they’re too consumed by their own lives. If anything, having realized this, I should try to help them. Freedom comes when I shift from being concerned about what they think about me to becoming creative about how I can be of service to them. Other days, I’ll take a nap in the morning and one in the afternoon 😊

author: @justinkelleher3405
text:
"So there I was; in the jungles of the Amazon. I saw a tribe of uncontacted natives! I was scared, but the chief came to me and greeted me with open arms. I went with them to their village. It was a sight to behold! Many a hut were placed around the land. A fire roaring with the unmistakable smell of meat cooking over the open fire. We ate hearty and drank to our hearts content. The tribal chief had labeled me "Inba Canabna" meaning "single leg beauty" in their language." 
Suddenly, a war cry could be heard from outside the village. Multiple tribesmen grabbed their spears, bows, and daggers. One of the tribesmen handed me a spear, urging me to fight alongside them! Without hesitation, I grabbed the spear, put my running blade on my leg and ran into battle! After several moments, I felt a sharp pain in my neck. One of the attackers' arrows had hit me! I fell to the ground. When the tribe saw me on the ground in pain, they immediately rallied to my aid, which put a great fear into the attacking tribe, scaring them away. The tribe had taken me back to the village to give me aid. When I came to, the villagers rejoiced with delight, knowing I was going to be ok. After a few days, I had bid them farewell. Just before leaving, the tribal chief had given me a new name: "Inba Canabna Trinda", which means "single leg beauty warrior". 

I'll be damned if this isn't the most epic story of a neck scar😂😂

author: @Tofu_Bunny
text:
Spearfishing. After trying to line up a double shot (you missed the second one but you did manage to get the first) a shark appeared. It went after your fish before you pulled it in, you decided to let the shark have it but it got your foot as you were leaving. You didn't have your speargun ready because you had just used your shot but the instructor did want you to take a knife and to dive in pairs. You pulled it out and in those intense 2 seconds the shark bit further up your leg. You're screaming underwater and there's so much bubbles coming from your mouth piece. You stab and stab but don't make much progress, the water makes your wild swings slower, the pain and shock don't help. You notice a spear sticking out of the shark and it let's go.  It didn't die but started swimming off in a cloud of blood. The spear came from your diving partner who you just met that day. He helps you to the surface after putting your mouth piece in and you get helped into the boat. It does not feel like enough, you can't breath in deeply enough.  They use a makeshift tourniquet to stop the bleeding. They also put a shirt on the side of your neck to stop the bleeding. Bleeding? From your neck? When? How?  The Spear he shot to save you! It was so close to hitting something vital and he didn't have time to get in a better position to line up the shot. You wake up in the hospital...
I dunno. I tried. 😐

author: @JenniferInEM
text:
I tried asking AI. It failed... and no, I didn't give it a name to use. Oh lord...

Sarah had always been an adventurous and spirited individual, embracing life with open arms and an indomitable spirit. Despite the challenges she faced as an amputee, she refused to let her physical limitations define her. Her resilience and determination were an inspiration to all who knew her.

When Sarah was diagnosed with a cancerous lymph node in her neck, she knew she had to face another battle. However, instead of succumbing to fear and uncertainty, she approached this challenge with the same courage and resolve that had carried her through life thus far.

Instead of undergoing surgery, Sarah opted for a more holistic approach to her treatment. She embarked on a journey of self-discovery, exploring alternative therapies and embracing the power of her own body's healing mechanisms. She delved into the depths of mindfulness and meditation, finding solace and strength within herself.

As Sarah embarked on this path of alternative healing, she encountered numerous obstacles. There were moments of doubt and despair, times when the weight of her situation threatened to overwhelm her. But through it all, she never wavered in her commitment to her well-being.

Sarah's unwavering strength and determination inspired those around her. Her friends and family rallied around her, offering unwavering support and encouragement. They witnessed her unwavering belief in her own healing abilities, and they marveled at her courage and resilience.

After months of dedicated effort and unwavering commitment, Sarah's lymph node miraculously disappeared. Her body had responded to her holistic approach, proving the incredible power of self-healing.

When people asked about the scar on her neck, Sarah no longer recounted a tale of surgery and recovery. Instead, she shared the story of her courageous journey of self-discovery and healing. She spoke of the power of the mind-body connection, the importance of holistic therapies, and the unwavering belief in one's own abilities.

Sarah's story became a beacon of hope for others facing similar challenges. Her heroism was not defined by physical strength or feats of daring, but by her unwavering belief in her own resilience and the power of self-healing. She taught others that true heroism lies within, in the depths of our own determination and the unwavering belief in our ability to overcome.

author: @vlmellody51
text:
If, on the other hand, you want to get rid of your scar, go get some marigold plants. Throw the stems and flowers in a food processor or blender along with a little neutral oil like any cooking oil and process them down into a thick paste. Spread it thickly over your scar and cover it with a close fitting bandage. Change it out twice a day or until your scar is gone.

I've used it on my keloids, and it has really worked.

author: @TheChildOfElmStreet
text: My personal favorite made up scar story is always "attacked by a bear". When I was little my dad (an often scarred auto mechanic) went on a camping trip and had to fix a friend's car and got hurt, and came home with a gnarly triple gash on his arm that scarred. So he made up a story about how when he was camping he was chased and attacked by a bear, that bear chased him up a tree, and all the extra weight bent the tree far enough my father could step off and continue running, and with the weight shift, the tree pitched back up, jettisoning said bear into space where it then stuck in the sky and became what would be known as Ursa major or the Big Dipper. Lol I was like 5. But I always thought it was a fun story to tell even after I got older and realized obviously it wasn't true.

author: @ExposingReflections
text:
It was the time i was in prison in Tegucigalpa...all a mistake,  i assure you. Anyway,  my cellmates and i had just broken out to freedom,  and were hiding in the jungle.  We were awaiting the brother in law of one of the ladies I was with. He had this old Beaver aircraft. Incredible machine.  Could land in almost any small clearing. As we were taking off,  I was returning fire at government forces on the ground, and a stray 30 cal nicked me.
 But heavens, I haven't had a good dust-up like that in some time.  What are you doing this weekend? Feeling adventurous?

 Reminds me of the time I was in Myanmar prospecting for rubies (and longingly rub your nubbin leg). But thats a long story for another time.

Arnica cream, available in most drug stores is recommended by some top cosmetic surgeons in the USA, to stop scars from forming post op. It works, but that too is another story that requires more time. 

Best of luck with the surgery. Love your attitude and strength of character.  I know we only get to see the good days, but if you need cheering up on a crap day, just holler.

Also, i think a t-shirt that says "steel toes save lives", could inspire a lot of interesting stories. The number of replacement feet, too. Four-foot Jo could be short until you meet her, 7 foot Jo the reverse.

author: @marshall4759
text:
Do not do this! This will void any warranty and put you at a safety risk. There are manufacturers torque and pylon requirements.
Your Prosthetist should not charge for minor adjustments.

author: @Crew9t
text:
I genuinely don’t know what warranty you’re referring to. I’ve been an amputee for 15 years and have never once had a warranty on my pylon. My feet have had warranties, even my foot shell, but never the pylon. I’ve worked with three different prosthetists over the years, and each one gave me the same tool I used in this video so I could make minor tweaks at home.
Yes, many prosthetists will make these adjustments for free, but it’s not always practical to drive to the clinic every time you need a minor tweak, especially in the early stages when changes are frequent. That’s why most prosthetists teach you how to make them yourself, as mine did.
Before posting this reply, I checked with the Amputee Coalition Facebook group and a few other amputee groups I’m in. Not a single amputee had ever heard of a pylon warranty. My own prosthetist also confirmed he’s never seen one. And no one could explain how adjusting the length would void anything anyways, considering these pylons are specifically designed to be lengthened and shortened as needed. So that is in the scope of “normal usage”.

author: @ILive2Rescue
text:
It’s awesome what Nike does for amputees!! I wonder if any of the other shoe companies do the same thing.
I’m so curious about your experience at nail salons that I’m going to call a couple salons in my area (Satellite Beach, which is 10 miles south of Cocoa Beach) and ask them if they give amputees half off. It’s half the work, so imo you should get half off. And not just a coupon for your next visit, because you still “have to” tip, so they’re really not giving you that great of a deal. 
Btw, people never cease to amaze me. I can hardly believe how often people randomly walk up to you and ask you how you lost your leg. 🤯

author: @Crew9t
text: Oh wow, you’re from Satellite Beach? That’s actually relatively close to me. I’m only like 40 minutes from Cocoa. I actually just got some tattoo work done in Cocoa a few months ago. Did you actually call the shops? I’m curious what they said.

author: @ILive2Rescue
text:
Crew9t Hey Johnny! I most certainly did! As a matter of fact, I called 12 different nail salons in Brevard County (both beachside and mainland) requesting the price for an amputee to get either a mani or pedi. Without fail, every single nail salon informed me that they wouldn’t offer a discount just because someone was an amputee. They told me that it wasn’t based on the number of limbs being serviced, but the amount of time and materials that would be required for the job to be done. That made absolutely no sense to me. I even tried “arguing” that it was half the job, should take half the time, and therefore should be half the price. Unfortunately no matter how much convincing I attempted, none of the salons were willing to budge on their pricing.
I even had one salon owner tell me that it wasn’t their fault that someone was involved in an accident that left them an amputee, so why should they have to “suffer the consequences”. That was the 12th and final telephone call I made, as it was so discouraging and disheartening.
I obviously wasn’t about to release any details about you, your accident, or any of your personal information, as I wanted the reactions to be genuine and organic. However, I’m now curious if any of those companies would have second thoughts if they knew who I was quoting a price for.

author: @Crew9t
text: Hmmmm. That’s very interesting. I almost feel like I should make a video going into these salons and have them tell me to my face, before pulling up my pant leg and showing them that I am the amputee in question.

author: @Crew9t
text: @annaj1998 No, I mean occasionally I will feel a little bit of pinching or rubbing, but that’s very occasional and usually passes before I even get to the point where I have to adjust anything. The only exception is when I end up with a hole in my outer sleeve, like I showed in this video. But that’s because once there’s a hole I no longer have a proper seal, so I start sliding around inside of my socket.

author: @Letsroll47
text: Too much work I call my tech and have them do it, that’s why insurance pays them

author: @Crew9t
text: I 100% agree it is way more work than just having somebody else do it. I mean, obviously lol. Unfortunately I don’t have insurance, but that’s OK though because my tech does it for free and doesn’t charge for that regardless. The only issue is, I don’t always feel like driving 40 minutes to my prosthetist, sitting there for 15 minutes, and then driving 40 minutes back home lol. However I can call them up, have them mail it to me, and then do it myself at home on my own time. At this point though, I actually don’t even buy them from my prosthetist anymore. Now I get them delivered from the amputeestore.com. It’s on AutoShip and I have it delivered every three months because that seems to be about the frequency that I end up needing a new one. But yes, it is definitely easier to have someone else do it lol. If that’s a viable option for anyone, do that. This video is more for the people who don’t have that option, or who just have them mailed directly to their house because of time/ location constraints.

author: @WhozOnfirst
text: Are the sleeves custom made for the circumference of your leg specifically or are they off the shelf like small, medium or large?

author: @Crew9t
text: They are off the shelf. There is a little bit more detail than just small medium and large thankfully. They go off of both length and circumference. Off the top of my head I don’t remember my measurements anymore, but I’m pretty sure I’m a medium circumference large length. I may be a medium/ medium though. But if I went up in circumference to make it easier to fit over my socket, then once I got down to the lower portion of the socket which is substantially thinner, it would be all  bunchy and would not have a good seal. I learned that the hard way when I thought I would cheat and go up a size just to make it easier to get on, and then had to seriously tape the hell out of it in order to make it work.

author: @janedoex1398
text: @christopherbarrette7686 Omg I'd love to but I can't barely afford rent atm . Not to mention a ticket for a flight to the US. Horses kept me sane until 13 when I saw our "trainer"  let a 4 year old one run over a course over and over again in 45 degrees Celsius because everything over 3 mistakes wouldn't count as a perfect round and he wanted 3 perfect rounds until he was done with it. It was a pony mix. 3 years old. Quite a bit of work yeah . But a small breed. I was too young to remember but I knew they often require to be older to be ridden in .....IT was TORTURE. We cooled and dried the poor thing for hours and I walked away from that stable crying my eyes out and never went back into the riding industry in my country. It's either cheap ( dirty, no good trainers) or expensive aka only if you own your own horse and pay the fees even if you treat it horribly and obviously can't cope with an 3/4 arab .....

author: @Emilystarks
text:
Hey man, I know it’s been a long time, but I heard your story involving a dispatcher and man, I understand what you went through. I was in a similar situation as you, but it was meant to be a routine surgery to remove recurring cholesteatoma. Ended up in me nearly bleeding out on the operating table. Woke up being put into an ambulance. I still remember damn near death gripping the paramedics hand next to me and asking him if I was going to die and that it was okay if I did and to tell my mom and dad that I love them, when they called out to Lebonheur what they were coming with. Blood pressure plummeting, right inner carotid artery severed, the amount of blood loss, etc. I’ll never forget the look on the paramedics face after a 17 year old girl asked him that question. He had this moment of just sadness in his eyes before he told me he wasn’t going to let anything happen to me and I could tell them myself. Turns out I passed out from blood loss after that and ended up having to be resuscitated before they got to the hospital. I later got to see that same paramedic and it was a tearful reunion for both of us to say the least with lots of hugs. Thanked him for saving me and everything. 

I might have ended up losing the rest of my hearing after that freak accident during surgery, but hey, I’m thankful every single day that I lost my hearing instead of my life. 

Keep going strong my dude. Things aren’t always as bad as it seems.

Wild, right? I must be an exceptionally good liar. Oh, and let’s not forget the three TV shows that featured me on episodes regarding this accident after interviewing multiple people involved besides me and still decided to air this “lie” to millions of viewers, one of which is streaming on Amazon Prime right now.

After a week of driving now I have built up my arm strength to lift it up with one try instead of 3 or 4 try's! It is hard! I hope to get measured for a prosthetic as soon as my stump heals. I have fallen twice on my stump, once in rehab and once at home on carpet in my bedroom so maybe it will take longer..How long did it take you to get a prosthetic leg? Did you go into rehab? author: @Crew9t
text:
Thank you for the replies, otherwise I probably would not have even noticed this comment. That being said…
Hi Lianne. You are correct, I did grow up in a gated community with strict Christian parents, although I would argue I didn’t “grow up“ in Country Creek, as I lived there for only four years and we moved out just after my 13th birthday. I would argue the neighborhood we moved to next is where I grew up. I digress, because I think your point is that I lived in an affluent neighborhood, and I will acknowledge the neighborhood we moved into was even more affluent, with the house we moved into reaching over $750K at the height of the market. 
All of this is to say that none of that matters in pertinence to this video. I moved out and left for New York at 17 years old. I celebrated my 18th birthday in my apartment in Queens. By the time I moved back to Florida, we were in the height of the market crash beginning in late ‘08, my parents had lost everything, filing for bankruptcy and having their house foreclosed on, and had moved into a house in the area I mentioned in this video. At the time of my accident, I personally was also living in the area, about a half a mile away from them. 
But yes, Lianne, as a pre-teen, we lived in a gated community.

text: @johnnyarm3181 you sound like a butt. The last few minutes of this video he talks about the OSHA investigation and how the employer was found to be at fault and how they had faulty machinery, a lack of several state required safety features, etc., and multiple times in this video he refers to locking out and tagging out the machine and says a couple different times that the machine was locked out and tagged out specifically when it started itself up during the accident. It was obviously not his fault just based on this video alone, but if you watch the documentary about him that’s on TV, they go into detail about the OSHA investigation and all the fines that were given to the company, the lawsuit and settlement afterwards, etc. If I remember correctly the company had to file bankruptcy because they were fined so heavily. So no, this very much was not his fault, but then because he makes a video where he talks about how it wasn't his fault, you start making up other hypothetical scenarios as examples of how he can't ever be at fault for anything? You just sound very dumb... either that or like you know him personally and have some grudge against him lol. Are you the plant manager he talks about in this video?

author: @Prof.Tarfeather
text:
You may have been great at what you were doing but you were inexperienced. And here's where accidents can happen in Companies when the proper protocol isn't met within the safety standards?
The more experienced crew should have been moved to the later shift with a supervisor on the scene. As they might have at sometime experienced or had gone over the scenario of a jammed Bailer?
Nobody would put their hand inside a garbage disposal if it jammed unless they first flipped the switch to the off position? And I've done maintenance for Apartment Complex's where the garbage disposal wouldn't shut off at the switch? I had to shut the breaker off. 
Therefore, this is where experience,  proper training, and safety come in? 
This is clearly the Company's oversite!
You should've turned the machine off before clearing out what was causing it to jam? Once the machine's blades or gears are free to move, they will and did with you inside.
Because of a rediculous thing our brain does to the smartest of us! Because we are wired not to think if that's possible? 
Our brains are lazy replacing memories with repetitive things all the time? We repeat motions all day long and don't realize it? Thats why certain repetitious jobs can become hazardous and why there's a need for supervision and why a standard number has already been established in the time-frame alotted to complete the work?
 Because the safety measures were already established for that specific machine in a certain time-frame as to the number of pallets that should be reasonable without jamming the mechanics and keeping the operator focused and safe!
The issue here is young inexperienced men who love to work and have a great work ethic will prove to their employers how valuable they are?
That's awesome and commendable. However, a good employer will keep his employees safe train them well and be fair to all his employees and keep not only to the safety standards but make sure there's no competition in the work environment that could make it unsafe? If adding more shifts the skeleton and lower hour shift at higher pay should have gone to more experienced employees. And one manager or supervisor position for that crew.
They layer you off..Right? Because they hadn't hired you on permanent yet! That sucks!
I haven't heard the whole thing yet?
But I'm getting it.
Walkie Talkie type Radios are used by many Ranchers and Companies throughout California. The same Towers and systems used by EMS and they've saved lives on many occasions during storms when guys have been stranded, a horse went down on top of the rider 20 miles from the ranch, etc...etc... Even a basic Radio Shack set of walkie talkies for a place like that would have been a great idea? 
What would have happened if you lost your phone in the Baler?
I'm so Happy you're safe and alive. Your 911 call really got me! I have 3 adult Son's, 6 Grandson's and 2 Granddaughters ranging in ages from 16 to 4. You thought of your Parents and thanked the 911 operator asking his name?
You're a selfless person to be that traumatized and think of others.
God Bless you!
Your Mom taught you well.

author: @keeneyeful
text:
WOW, so much to learn from from this incident, I'm glad to hear such a positive outcome.   As an industrial medical responder I can't emphasize enough:  Having an emergency plan and knowing how to apply it being the first - Including knowing where you are in order to to direct emergency (internal and external) responders!  Always take emergency calls on radios seriously, ensure that proper machine guards and other safety procedures are in place to prevent these things from happening in the first place.  This could have easily become a fatality if not for the actions taken by himself and those he credits with saving him.

John, thanks for sharing!  Enjoy life!

author: @therayven3147
text:
Yo what happening OG... Watched your 911 history recently on urmakers channel, I believe it was the second video in his "most disturbing 911 calls" Playlist... then before here, watched your video when you showed the photos of the accident scene... then came here... so awesome to see and hear the whole story from the human side of it... you are a true OG... I'd imagine someone screaming uncontrollably, but you... you were so calm and talked the call taker through it... even threw in a couple of wisecracks as well... "how many officers do you need? Ots a bailer"... was my favourite...


Awesome job to Chris? Was it? The cop? Anyway, awesome you two are still good friends...


And I love your spirit... you don't boo-hoo about your "disability", you go with it... you do almost everything you do now without your leg that you did with it... I understand about sand and soft mud and such, it's hard to get your footing when can't feel it underneath you... hell, you even go rock climbing, that's impressive...


One good thing that came of your accident... it was the video featured by "urmaker", I believe it was the 2nd video in his Playlist, that led you finding your fiance (probably wife by now?) Hope y'all are happy and doing good...


Man, it a miracle you're still alive, and I'm happy fir you... may you have many more promising years...


Take care and God bless...

So a few months go by and the end of June 2019 my leg wound opened up and I got another severe infection. I had to get an above the knee amputation!!! I was a person that was skeptical about stress effecting the body. But now I know!!! It can as much as open and infect an old wound!!! Please listen when I say stress can destroy your body!!!

author: @chandyrec
text:
I had a car on top of my hand between the wheel-well and a tire.  A guy was helping me change the front drivers side tire on my Mom’s Delta 88 Oldsmobile (BIG ass car).  My Dad always kept a full-size spare tire in the trunk.  When we were trying to put the new tire on after we removed the flat, the car wasn’t high enough, so the guy told me to hold the tire while he jacked it up higher.  We were on a gravel road so unfortunately the jack slipped and went under the car and the car fell down trapping my hand between the tire and the wheel-well!  I was screaming when it happened and it took three people to lift the front end enough for me to pull my hand out.  For approximately 20 minutes I didn’t feel anything, no pain, nothing.  I think I was in shock because I was totally calm, even joking around, but then suddenly, after some time, the pain was like a sledgehammer just smashed my hand and I started screaming.  
Adrenaline is a crazy thing, so I totally understand why you were so calm.  I was fortunate that I didn’t loose my hand and after a year, it was completely back to normal.

author: @Crew9t
text: I’ve noticed that over the past almost 11 years of being an amputee, my thought process has definitely changed. When I was a brand new amputee I was very nervous to be around children, because I was afraid that they would inadvertently be rude just out of their simple lack of social understanding, and I also was a little concerned that I might even scare them. If I knew I was going to be somewhere that there were a lot of younger children, I would purposely wear pants rather than shorts. Now, all these years into it, I have learned that children are generally super excited about prosthetic legs, they think that they’re awesome, or they think that I’m some sort of modern day pirate, meanwhile it is the adults who stare, elbow each other, or will blatantly walk up to me and just start asking me extremely personal and borderline rude questions. The kids are great, the adults suck lol

All things worketh together for good, our friend here is a great testimony. Within half an hour I did my research on this guy, and within those precious minutes, I must say that you John, impacted my life. I want to say thanks to the Dispatcher for being so patient with him. Satan would have you be deep down in depression, but you're a fighter, SOLDIER! You're the one who give me courage to write this on your channel here :). We love you guys and John, keep up the good work for others are looking on at your legacy that you have within you, take care. Love from Jamaica!!

author: @sunshinem.7741
text:
The no rubbing rule is to not rub the chemicals into your skin 😭
It's a chemical hair removal method, that thins/softens the hair until it can fall off. And it takes the outer layers of skin with it. That's why you can't use it in succession; you need time for the skin to heal.
That said, it's still a neat and pretty easy thing to use. Hopefully it continues to work well for you!

author: @TheGreekPianist
text: I can’t express how inspirational you are to me, Johnny! My name is Jonathan and I have a severe disability too (but not as severe to the point where you suffered). But it prevented me from living life for a long time and I couldn’t do what I loved (especially playing music, which is a passion of mine). But people like you really gave me a motivational “nudge” to keep a positive attitude, live life to the fullest, and not be defined by my condition. I’m now able to play the piano, drums, and guitar as much as I can without having to worry about any constraints or limitations. I go to college like an everyday guy, have a social life, and a girlfriend who doesn’t judge me by my disability. You’re so awesome man and you keep me going! 😁👊

author: @TheGreekPianist

You handled such a traumatic situation with so much bravery and strength. I admire people like you so much and I’ll always be a loyal follower of yours. I’ve subscribed to your channel too. You made me realize not to be sad or depressed over my disability (because I was for a long time). But now I try my best to stay ambitious, optimistic, and keep physically and mentally active. I really hope you stay safe during this global crisis bro 😷😃

author: @Crew9t
text: Ok Lannie. I’ve contemplated this response for a little while now. I’m essentially a public figure on here, so I understand and fully expect the flirtatious thirsty comments here or there, and I’ll usually play along a bit because it’s slightly part of my Crew9t persona. I even flirt in my videos themselves sometimes. But whenever someone comes along and flirts to the degree that you do, I usually just ignore it. Hence my replies to your comments on my three other videos, but lack of reply here. I also normally don’t involve myself in arguments or any form of back and forth. Taking into account that my gf is more than capable of handling her own, I decided that this didn’t warrant an exception, and decided to not get involved in this particular back and forth either, even though it involved her this time. But I have to be honest. I don’t mind the flirting. It’s humorous to me. I don’t really consider it disrespectful to djqueentee, because you have literally no shot in hell, so it’s so minor it’s not even a blip on the radar. Her’s or mine. Hence she’s never replied to you. Don’t think she didn’t see your other comments. She did. She just never replied. Her finally replying to this one signifies you having crossed a line. Maybe in and of itself, this comment wasn’t too much, but being the fourth video in a row where you’ve commented as such, I have to agree. But that alone wasn’t enough of a problem to warrant my attention. As I said, djqueentee can hold her own. I decided to get involved because you came for her. It’s one thing to flirt with me. But your “where u at doe” comment was too far. You will not disrespect my woman on my channel. Now I could have just deleted your comments and blocked you from commenting again, but I want this up so anyone who ever comes to this video might possibly see it. Lannie, maybe you’re trying to be funny. If so, cool it. Maybe you’re serious. If so, I’m taken and don’t appreciate it. Whatever the reasoning, stop. No more flirting from you. One more time, and you’re gone. Also, I think my gf needs an apology. Capisci?

author: @greenbeans9748
text: Hey there! I just found you after listening to your 911 call on a different channel and I really love all that you're doing for others. I think it's really neat that you turned a scary experience into something to grow stronger from and to help others who are similar to you. I am not an amputee, but I am definitely not straight. The problem with some people is when the hear that someone is part of the LGBT community, they may assume we are "the trouble-making kind." I'm not just talking about the ones who think we're evil or gross, I'm mainly referring to the people who assume we're like the extremists who police and lecture others non-stop with political correctness, have volatile tempers over trivial matters, ruin businesses, etc. (Twitter and Tiktok have excellent examples of what I'm talking about). It really sucks because most of us are not like that and it hurts being lumped in with the ones who give us a bad name (I hate people like that too, but most of us are humble, kind, and don't care if people don't support us or not as long as they are being respectful). Anyway, I'm really happy you're doing well and that I found your channel. I know this video is an older one nor am I sure that you'll see this, but it's always nice seeing another person who wants be kind to others. I hope you have a wonderful day and I'm looking forward to seeing more content from you.

author: @Pippis78
text:
Welcome to female reality. Up until I was 30yo there were hardly any occasions of men talking to me (without a reason) that didn't turn into asking for a phone number or smth. So you learn pretty quickly to just say "I have a boyfriend" - which I literally didn't - to avoid all the cringe and bother.
But I think that's a bit of a cultural thing. In my country people are really shy and we don't do a lot of small talk - and men usually need alcohol to be able to chat up women.
But I have probably said that to many guys who were just being friendly ...maybe.

It was mind blowing to travel in Ireland were people casually hang out in pubs and talk to strangers. It was so refreshing to be able to talk with men and just say "k bye, it was nice to meet ya" at the end. I decided to be more open and friendly after that - men were still always trying to hit me on, but I would just very firmly tell them that I can have a chat, but I am not "interested" and sometimes added having a boyfriend still.

But that "fake boyfriend" shield actually pisses me of nowadays.
It's messed up that men will not leave a woman alone if she just says she's not interested. They are more likely to respect another man and his "property" than respect a woman and what she wants or doesn't want!

Edit: Oh, and I'm not super good looking.

author: @LtDan-lj7oj
text:
Johnnie - I'm here (subbed) by way of a 911 call dispatch documentary which is uploaded to Full Throttle TV YouTube channel.

Having suffered a serious spinal cord injury as a 20 yr old (May '76), and being instantly paralyzed below the belt, the neurosurgeon told my family that I'd never walk again, as they were certain I'd severed my spine. Nobody told ME that...until 1 1/2 months later while in physical therapy, on the parallel bars. I lumbered my way to the other end of the p-bars, my girlfriend broke down crying, saying " they told me you'd never walk again!"

Well I AM indeed walking! 
    You asked the 911 dispatcher if he'd pray with you... I know that many were praying for me when I was 20 yrs old as well. Prayer works! Looking forward to perusing through your content.

Finally- I would have responded to the grocery store woman: "You DO??? So do I!!! What are the odds???"

             God Bless You!

author: @anpyon2
text:
To answer your questions: no, you are not missing something, this is skepticism towards men in modern-day society. Here's how it is: women in society are raised to be wary of men. It's important we distinguish well-intended kindness from attempts at flirting or possible ulterior motives. And yes, it is hard to make that distinction. It's important to set limits when talking to strangers, and let the other person know what they can expect and what they cannot expect if you're hesitant, and see from the reaction of the other person if they had any further intentions other than friendly conversation.

Now, there are many ways to go about this, and one 'technique' is the "I have a significant other" line. I personally have never used it because I have never been approached like that (save for my husband when we first met but that was mutual), but I have seen it being deployed at the gym a few times - often used as a last resort, mind. I really hate this line and it irritates me that this is one of the most effective ways to fend off unwanted advances, because it indicates that the other person (man, woman, nonbinary, anyone) has more respect for someone they have never met or might not even exist for all they know, than they have for you. I find that abhorrent. But... as I said, it's effective, sadly.

Hope this was a bit helpful. :)

author: @Crew9t

When a guy only finally leaves you alone because you tell him you have a boyfriend, after numerous times telling him you’re not interested already, that shows he has more respect for some other guy who he’s never even met than he does for you, the woman he’s trying to get to go out with him 😳 That’s an amazing way to look at that.

author: @Lady8D
text:
I'm only 0:36 into this video but already I'm wondering if you've ever heard of/been tested for Prosopragnosia (aka face-blindness). What you're describing, people knowing who you are but you don't know them - story of my life right there! I used to think I was just a jerk or something but then I heard of & got tested for FB & my life started making a lot more sense all the sudden lol. I "see" faces but don't recognize em...sometimes I don't even recognize em as faces but that's rare. Whatever, so many words to say: if you often find yourself in situations where ppl know who you are but don't know them, think about looking into it =)
K, back to vid. Again, sorry so long, I suck at concise

author: @eiosti
text: For anyone thinking it's an ablism thing, I'm preeeetty sure it's not. It's like if you've been friends with someone for a long time and you come out as trans or tell them about some other disability that's pretty integral to who you are. Like none of my friends know I have autism. If they saw me rocking and stimming and vocalizing and having a meltdown, they'd be pretty shocked, because it's like. A huge part of my life that never goes away that they'd never seen. Except with missing a limb it's more visceral because it's literally like.. I know this person. They're a person with like. Arms n legs n a neck and a head. And then you suddenly find out oh what no wait they don't. Almost like how you see yourself as having arms n legs n a neck and a head until somethings amputated and then you don't.

author: @eiosti
text: Actually. You Still see yourself as having all your arms n legs sometimes and then you get hecka fantom pain because your brains like ah yes and I will move my totally existent leg now

author: @CarolynDenison
text: @kristinzeiber23 My husband is a mechanic for Uhaul. He say's (jokingly) all you need is super glue and duct tape. Rather then put new motors in my door window' she went and bought me a newer truck. So I gave him my old truck, thinking he would fix it and use it as a work truck.  The wipers went out. (something electrical) so instead of addressing the issue, he rigged up two wires from under the hood and ran the wires to reach the inside of the cab. So, now when it's raining he just plugs the wires to each other. Lol... The washer fluid, and all the speeds are a thing of the past. Lol... They go one speed and that's it. His old truck has two mix matched tail light lenses. From a Chevy and a Dodge. The truck is a Ford F-150. Glad he never asked me to go with him anywhere in that old ass red truck.  A repo man was picking up a neighbor's car. Didn't hook up n the front, instead the back. Steering was locked with the wheels turned. SO He flies past my house and took out the entire side of old red. Dented it up from bumper to bumper. Yep... Took a lot of super glue to hold in those brake light covers. I have to hand it to him. He didn't spend much to make it legal again. lol...
