author: @trishnelson5944
text: About service dogs, my son has an epilepsy dog so we went to the zoo. You have to go to their guest service, get a tag, a collapsible water dish, show papers etc. We did all of this and at one point sat down. This adorable little girl came over and asked if she could pet the dog and I just didn't have the heart to tell her no but while she was petting him I was explaining what a service dog does and how he has a job and normally they shouldn't interact with others while they are at work. I was educating her in a kind way. This security guard came over to us and asked if our dog was a "real service animal" . It really irked me so I told him yes, pulled out my paperwork, showed him the tag from THEIR service center and he says, "well you're not supposed to let people pet him" as if I don't know how it works. I said, "I'm educating others about service animals but if you'd like my son to fall over and have a seizure right now I bet I could make that happen". He made that little girl feel like she had done something wrong, he made my son and me upset. I don't usually have anyone ask to pet him because most people know that he's working. To me it was an opportunity to teach that little girl. Who knows she might grow up to train them because of my dog but that butt nugget may have dampened that desire. Rant over. P.S. Although our dog has not left little poopy presents in public areas, I would never expect someone else to pick it up, my dog, my mess!

author: @mavisme4645
text: This is actually WHY service dog handlers prefer that businesses ask us about our dogs being legit.  When people see a dog take a dump in a store, or snap at a child, or sniff at merchandise from shelves, it gives real SD teams a bad name.  And believe me, those untrained pets walking all around the store are a HUGE danger for real service dogs.  My own service dog has been jumped 3 times during her career by these 'imposter' dogs.  Besides the threat of being injured, it really messes with their training.  I know of quite a few SDs that had to be retired early because they were attacked by a fake SD/emotional support dog and were unable to focus and do their jobs after that.  SDs are EXPENSIVE and very time consuming to train.  Handlers NEED them for daily life.  The #1 fear that SD handlers have is another dog attacking their own SD!!  PLEASE LEAVE YOUR PETS AT HOME!!

My service dog had an accident in a hotel once and it was on the carpet. I cleaned it up the best I could and noticed the cleaning lady and helped her clean it. 

If your dog has an accident the you need to clean it up no matter if it’s a service dog or a pet

text:
Poor guy lmao I have a worse story from my old job involving an old guy, luckily I also got our of dealing with it lmao
And here where I live, service dogs do have to have some identification. I've had to kick people out for their dog not having service animal identification.

author: @MystiDawn
text: I did ask those questions, and she couldn't tell me what task the service dog performed. Because it wasnt a service dog. She just didnt want to leave her car running to leave the dog in her car. And my boss told me if she couldn't somehow prove it was a service dog, I had to ask them to leave. She couldn't show me any papers or identification, or tell me what service the dog provided.

author: @MystiDawn
text:
Well this happened about 9-10 years. I didn't know any of that. I did what my manager told me to do. I was told by my manager than if they didn't have any kind of 'proof' that it was definitely a service dog, I had to ask them to leave. And so that's what I did.
Thank you for informing me  I'm definitely glad to know better than I did then. There had been incidents after that, that I didnt ask for any kind of 'proof' because service animal or not, I love dogs. But at that time, I was told that I had to ask them to leave, and just did what my boss said to do, because I was told they had to have identification and just trusted that my boss was right.

author: @MystiDawn
text:
@Crew9t this old guy used to come in every morning, we all knew him and had him order memorized. I was the assistant manager at this time. So I got to go to the bank every morning. So I went to the bank and they were pretty busy so it took awhile, I got back and my co worker was standing outside, quite upset. I asked her what happened, and apparently the guy had an accident, and it went from the table he was at, to the bathroom (which was pretty well covered in 'it') and then to the door. 
We both felt bad for the guy cuz he had trouble getting around, and this day his daughter wasn't with him, but she wasnt happy to be the one to clean it all up lol

author: @simplymeconnie
text: Haaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaahahahahaha OMG that was hilarious! I have had the small little "Sh*t Demons" and what he said is right. Once they start, just let them finish because while they may be tiny, they're full of it! Haha! I now have big dogs who can actually hold it hahhah! A chihuahua is not a service animal. A service animal is one who assists their person by being their eyes, their hearing, or even to help them balance. Usually these dogs have a ton of rigorous training and have to pass tests to become a legit service animal. Too many people are claiming their animals, it doesn't matter what it is, as a service animal, when really, they're not. I unfortunately know this because I got a dog to be my service animal instead of using a walker. Well, she didn't pass the tests, so she is a pet. Not an emotional support animal, not a service dog, nor do I try to pass her off as one, and sadly, I am one who SHOULD have one being half deaf with no balance..

The guy who owned the Chihuahua is probably off posting on Reddit about anonymous public humiliation because of his dog.  He probably didn't know what to do either.

FYI, if the situation ever arrives again, get a plastic bag,  put your hand inside, pick up the solid waste and then invert the bag.  Cart wipes will not cut the mustard, so to speak. author: @KerryBerry25
text: It makes me very sad that after all those years of “ religious” education you were never taught the truth about how we get to Heaven… it’s not about doing good things or being a good person. It’s believing that Jesus died for our wrongs/sins and trusting that he took the wrap for us. Then turning away from life on our terms and following him.  💜

author: @Crew9t
text: See but you literally say it in your own comment. At the very end of your comment you mention turning away from life on our terms and following him. I never said it was about works. I said I did enough bad stuff and was a bad enough person that I knew I was going to hell. This isn’t me saying it’s about works and that I hadn’t done enough good things to earn a place in heaven. It’s me going off of what you yourself even just said, which was the same thing that I was taught repeatedly my entire life, which was that if you truly accept Jesus in your heart, you will naturally turn away from the ways of the world. The fact is, I never turned away from the ways of the world, but instead continued doing all of the same stuff I always did and in fact as I got older got into new worse stuff. Therefore I had to question whether I had ever even actually really accepted him or if I had just gone through the motions. And by the time I reached adulthood I realized I no longer had any interest in Christianity or organized religion in general, which answered my question, that I had most likely never truly accepted him in my heart to begin with. So that prayer was a dying person’s last words. As they say, “there’s no atheists in foxholes”. In a moment of distress you will grasp for any straws you can reach, and having had organized religion pounded into my brain since before I could even talk, and having been fully indoctrinated into the Christian church without my consent and with no say or agency in what religion I wanted to follow, if any, of course I naturally went to the one thing I knew, which was Christianity and Jesus. Ultimately though, I died. Meaning he did not save me. However, modern technology and the innovations of man in the form of medical intervention brought me back to life. So in reality, none of it even mattered anyways. Ultimately, none of this is my belief or faith anymore, nor has it been for over a decade, but going off of the actual words of the Bible, I was not saying it was of works. I was saying essentially that because I was completely unchanged and still living my life according to me, doing things I knew were wrong and having zero cares, I knew ultimately I likely was not going to heaven. If it even existed. Which even in my moment of turmoil, I still wasn’t even sure I believed it did exist. But I damn sure knew I was going to say a prayer just in case, even if it ultimately did me nothing. Which in fact turned out to be the case. Now the argument could be made that maybe it’s because all of this DOES exist, however I was correct and in fact was not truly a Christian, and that is why my prayer was not answered. If that’s the case I guess we will deal with that the next time I die, if that time ends up being more permanent 🤷

Deep sadness that I was still so young & unable to do certain things. I took the path of fear for a while, became agoraphobic. However, eventually it gave me the insight to realize that life is too short to hide, and I’m very open to almost any new experience now! I am extremely grateful that I was given a second chance as well & I appreciate your insight on the topic. I don’t feel so alone anymore. Thank you!

author: @a7c777
text: When i nearly died I heard God speak to me to give me another chance to live a life for love and I later became a Christian because I realized nothing i could do could please an all powerful God and that voice told me to live with love. Christianity seemed to be the biggest embodiment of that for me. But it was a multiple time experience and the first time it was a sense of empty nothingness too. But the second time, I felt, saw and heard stuff. I do think punishment is real but i think God is pretty patient and forgiving and kind as well and really appreciates when you try your best to live for Him and others around you. Well that’s what I believe I saw anyway, but i hope that’s not preachy, I just thought id share to give someone desperately scared of nothingness after life here, some hope ‘cause I feel there’s definitely something that exists past the “nothingness phase” in my experience where existence goes on.

author: @deannamarie8389
text: I can feel you on the whole not wanting to die alone thing. I'm an ICU nurse, we have an unspoken rule: nobody dies alone, the nurse or the nursing assistant will stay in the room as the patient dies if there is no family present.

author: @meady50
text: That’s so sweet. When we had to take my father off of life support, I vividly remember the nurses being shocked at how many people wanted to be in the room with him. Im so grateful for all of the staff and how kind everyone there was; they actually accommodated to our massive family (If I remember correctly, it was around 15+ people in the room that were family) and moved him to a larger room to do the final processes so we could all be with him as he passed. My mom got to hold his hand as he took his final breath, and I think all of us being there really helped him pass. So thank you guys for everything you do. I actually went through nursing school and passed with the highest grades in my class, but once I got to clinicals  I realized I didn’t think I could 100% control my emotions under certain circumstances, so ultimately I decided to not continue going down that path. Sometimes I miss it and wish I had continued, but I just don’t feel that I can fully trust myself to be dependable if I’m placed in a similar situation to my own previous one

author: @liamadams261
text: Recently I was involved in a motorcycle accident. I didn't die but got as close as you can get. Almost bled out in the street. The cops didn't think I would make it. I needed blood transfusions. I know exactly how you feel. Listening to your phone call made me cry. The first thing you worry about is your mom and I did the same exact thing. You telling everyone you loves them. I did something similar. I couldn't talk because my jaw was broken and hanging there but I was reaching for someone/anyone's hand. I couldn't keep my eyes open or move my head to look around but someone grabbed my hand. It was my mom and some how I instantly knew it was her and I muttered "I'm sorry" and passed out. I woke up in my hospital room dazed and confused. I also had recently had a break up with my girlfriend of 6 years and tried to reach out to her hoping she would care and come back.. but she didn't care. I also got the "that has nothing to do with me" treatment. I can relate to your story on such a deep level. I broke so many bones in my body but the main thing was my femur. It broke in 8 places and the bone ripped through my leg. Surprisingly I didn't lose it but I'm unable to bend my leg any more than 60 degrees. This happened 02-04-23 so very recently. I'm still recovering and trying to learn to walk again. Your story is helping me out through this struggle in my life rn. I know how it feels to feel like "why am I still alive I shouldn't be here" and I hope I can find a reason for that someday, but I want you to know.. thanks to you and seeing how well and healthy you look in these videos. I have hope and I oddly don't feel alone in my situation. I wish there were more recent uploads to resonate with. But I'm glad there is what there is. Idk if you still use this account or not but I hope you get this message and know you helped one more person with your story and I'm glad you're still here. Thank you

author: @kayne01
text: Damn. What a touching story. I hope you are doing well and have once again found that spark for life! Physically limiting injuries are always the worst, because you always reflect on what once was. However, it is also important to look at the other side of the coin, what COULD of happened. You COULD of bled out and passed, but rather, you are alive and breathing. I hope you’re doing well

author: @liamadams261
text: ​@kayne01 I've found a sense of purpose. Found a job that I love doing and been learning everything I can about it while investing in the tools I need to do it. I have yet to find my other half. Still been really lonely and still sore from the last one. I've had a surgery to help move my leg more, it still isn't how it used to be but it's much better than the time from the last post. Life isn't perfect but I'm staying hopeful

author: @MissSunriseDawn
text: I have a cousin that's died several times. Three times in one ambulance trip due to a brain aneurysm, another time in the hospital (I think), then, several years later, he nearly died due to an aortic tear. I told him we already know he's Iron Man, stop trying to prove it. Lol He's told us about seeing loved ones that are already on the other side. Specifically, he saw his brother that had just a couple years before in a car accident. He said that his brother told him, "It's not your time yet. You have to go back." Or something along those lines. He said it gave him a lot of peace to see his brother smiling and happy. His brother also came to me in a dream about a month after he died, but that's another story.

author: @PneumaNoose
text: I hope you keep making videos. You’re a fkn inspiration man. I might lose both my legs due to disease caused by my 2014 car accident and not being able to afford all the surgeries I need. I’ve already lost most of the feeling in my feet (minus excruciating daily pain). And the worst of it is that my accident was caused by a cop that I can’t sue and he’s living on a fat pension and settlement from my car insurance thanks to the fixed police report (anyone who claims cops don’t take care of their own have never been a victim of abuse of power). Anywayyyyy… I’ve been living in deep depression knowing I really have no way of saving myself—so instead of watching my legs get GRUESOMELY amputated in a second, I’m watching my legs die in pain over years just to lose them once it becomes a threat to my life and I can be seen at the community hospital. It sucks but watching you gives me so much hope. I hope the man I love will still love me and stay by me but if not, I have hope that I can be okay on my own. I just have to figure out how I’m still gonna take care of my triplegic mother lol. She can’t sit up, walk, speak, brush her own teeth, etc. So yea… that’s gonna be a challenge for sure and I’ll need prosthetics FOR SURE. I appreciate your prosthetic  vids big time. Anyway, I’m blabbering. Just can’t express to you enough how awesome you are. Thank you for showing us the way. Stay strong man.

author: @HouseOfFaust
text: Are you sure you didnt just pass out in the ambulance? That happens a lot. I mean I dont say that to undermine what you went through and you definitely lucked out and Im sure were all happy that you're here but passing out from blood loss is pretty standard for amputations. Whats dificult to watch is people who dont pass out (not that they should). Im assuming youre right for the record and you did die, I just think its important for people to maintain a healthy and accurate scope of what has happened in their life. I tried to kill myself when I was 15  (relax I dont have the balls to kill myself anymore) and apperently wasnt even good at dying so Im still here and definitely relate to the sentiment of not seeing shit when I was konked out though truthfully I didnt die (by most people's standard). I appreciate your outlook moving forward as well. Its similar to my own though I have next to no interest in living I just dont want to kill myself to end it lol. You are never truly free until you recognize that every day youre alive is just extra and you should have been dead long ago.

text: Thank you so much for sharing this. I don’t know how I found your story tonight but I am so glad I did. I’m a medical assistant and I work for a kidney specialist. We received a referral the other day that had a medical history listed and there was a date of death from years ago. And I hit me really hard thinking about that. I thought it was interesting that it legit said “died” on this date at this time. And I wondered how that impacted them. It’s hard sometimes working in an industry where you see the fragileness of life come and go. I never thought id be strong enough to handle that sort of job but I really feel like it’s my calling to help others navigate through the tough decisions. I am so inspired by your view on life and what you are doing with your second chance. Also I’m sorry your ex was a bitch. I’m sure she didn’t realize how serious it was. 😬

author: @theactiveamputee4061
text: Hatte das Ladegerät für eine Powerbank sonst immer mit. Aber dieses Mal dachte ich, es müsste reichen. Waren ja nur 3,5 Tage. Und zuhause komme ich locker auch 4 Tage. War allerdings am Ende knapper als ich dachte. Das Knie scheint unter der Belastung dann doch schneller durch die Batterieladung zu gehen als sonst. Werd das eventuell bei der nächsten Wanderung mal ausreizen. Dann nachts auch in Hybernation-Mode wechseln. Und dann mal schauen, wie weit eine Batterieladung wirklich hält. Wenn am Tag etwa 17 Kilometer und 500 Höhenmeter gemacht werden. Und wenn ich so um die 18 Kilogram auf dem Rücken habe.

author: @Silver_Aussie123

author: @sophiehead6158
text:
So the whole contraption is bionic yeah? Like a robot almost, in and of itself. 

It has super sensitive muscle sensors  that line up with the muscles in her arm, as over 90% of hand movements are actually from nerves/muscles in the forearm....

So basically if she had memory of using her own hands, she'd be thinking about the hand moving, and that makes the muscles/nerves move enough that the prosthetic sensors can read them. 

In her case it's a bit different as she doesn't remember her flesh hands, but it's just a bit of a different thought process.

Her brand new arm can Adams family it's hand around ❤

If I ever manage to go to the US, I would like to meet you and record a video for your channel, or maybe if one day you come here to Rio de Janeiro (Brazil). author: @foxintheshell5961
text:
Cool! Reminds me a bit of the Berserker from Quake 2 with the slightly offset shoulder.
By the way, are there any plans to address motion smoothening and acceleration in software? Real limbs don't instantly ramp  up to a constant speed, move at that constant speed and then do a hard stop. I don't know if your nerve sensors can pick up just on/off states or different degrees of activation, but I think looking a bit into this, especially since it's mainly a software issue rather than a motor issue could amaze your customers and drive product satisfaction through the roof. I do also have a few ideas on how the mechanism could be redesigned to allow for both quick and fast movement and slow high precision high-torque movement, but I guess that all depends on how good your sensors are.
Wonderful stuff though, keep it up!

author: @ArmDynamics
text: Hello! Good question. The only reason that some people may find them more comfortable is because they might be lighter than a myoelectric device because they don't use batteries. As for efficiency, everyone's going to be different in what they find easiest to use. At our centers, our clinical therapy specialists teach our patients how to use their device. Body-powered hooks don't have to be charged at night, so in that way they may be considered more efficient. You can read more about body-powered hooks on our site: https://www.armdynamics.com/upper-limb-library/introduction-to-body-powered-hooks

author: @AdaptiveAmputees
text:
Wow! I am so so sorry you had to experience that. I talk about my medically induced come in a video - having to relearn everything is so wild. 
I am so glad you are on the other side. Hope you continue to heal and keep that determination up!!

author: @samnbaker
text:
​​@AdaptiveAmputees Thank you that's very kind of you to say. It's definitely an experience that's hard to connect with others about. The journey itself has been the reward. Humbling, challenging,  and all around eye opening. Finding strength within ourselves is never easy, but it's in the face of adversity we find how powerful we really are, (as corny as that sounds, haha). I think it just reshapes all parts of you, physically, mentally and emotionally. The same parts of who you were are still there, just melted down and molded in a new way. That's the best way I can describe it at least.

Congratulations on your own journey and continuing to challenge yourself 🙂 It's awesome to see. Your message really makes an impact and you're giving a lot back to a growing community of us trying to find hope. Keep inspiring people ♥️

author: @AdaptiveAmputees
text:
@samnbaker Wow… that was so beautifully said. I feel all of that in my bones. It is hard to explain to people what this experience really does to you—how it breaks you down but somehow rebuilds you stronger, even if the process is messy as hell. I love how you described it… melted down and molded into something new. That hit hard.

Thank you for your kind words too. It means a lot coming from someone who really gets it. We’re all just out here trying to figure it out, and it’s cool to know we’re not alone in it. Sending you so much respect and strength right back 💪🏼❤

author: @timcathey8476
text: Im currently 5 days post-op bka , I’m excited I made it home about 3 days ago.  I’ve be watching your channel since finding out about my surgery, for the education, sometimes comical reliefs, and as a fellow married amputee your  videos have also answered some of my wife’s questions going in to this. This is the long winded version of me saying thank you for everything you do for the channel and the amputee community as a whole.

author: @AdaptiveAmputees
text:
I’m honestly so moved reading this. Five days post-op and already home—that’s huge. You’re stepping into something so raw and real right now, and the fact that my channel could be a small part of your journey means more than words can say. Knowing it’s helped both you and your wife is exactly why I keep doing this. You’re not alone in this. Keep holding on to that fire and excitement—you’re already showing such strength. Thank you for sharing this with me; it hits deep and reminds me why this community is everything. Sending you all the love and strength as you heal and move forward.

Also: let me know if there are any particular topics you and your wife would like to see covered!

author: @timcathey8476
text: @AdaptiveAmputeesthat was a beautiful message thank you. I’ve notice strength comes from my support system. Because   Of everyone that has supported me during this time. I have been able to focus and make progress I have. It still feels surreal and like I’m telling someone else’s story. But  I’m  blessed and grateful for the future and how my story might be able to help others in the future. Thanks again.

author: @Coemgenus618
text: I am NOT an amputee, but I have lost the function of my gastrocnemius (calf) muscle after multiple lower back surgeries.  Scar tissue has formed around the nerves that innervate the calf muscle, therefore, leaving me unable to raise up on my toes on the left leg.  I recently reconnected with an old Marine Corps buddy who happens to be a professional climber from OK.  He has encouraged me to give rock climbing a try.  I had always shied away from this sport due to my aforementioned issue.  I started to do some research and came across your video.  Thank you for offering this video to the public and giving me hope!

author: @bluedeva
text: Have you tried the power knee yet? It doesn’t have the hydraulics like the X4, c leg or Orion but it’s sit to stand feature is insane as well as it’s stair ascent. It’s such a stable knee. I used to have the Rheo XC but now have the navii and I love it, as it does everything I need (stair ascent, stair decent, rides slopes with ease, rowing, cycling, scooting and lifting in the gym intuitively - no having to switch modes) So far I only have to charge it every 4/5 days. The quattro also looks really good but I haven’t tried that one nor the kneuro. Thank goodness for all of these amazing new advances in tech and just how much easier they actually make to our daily life. PS completely agree,  you really need at least a month of wearing each knee to fully understand which one will suit your needs best, as the first 2 weeks you are only just getting used to learning how to use it and building trust in it.

Check out your local amputee charities too I hope this helps

author: @Bluefox8164
text: I am a LBK amputee due to CRPS. Before the amputation, my body would constantly make me feel as though I was either being tortured with a car battery, or in a bonfire with my affected limb, or it would make me relive the fall I had that started all this. (I stepped in a hidden hole in the grass and instead of a simple twisted ankle, my foot decided to be dramatic and come off.) since the amputation, I will get bad days about 24 to 48 hours before any rain or snow. It will feel like someone is trying to use a cheese grater on the joint of my big toe on the ghost foot, and on occasion I will feel a zap every so often. But even the consistent cheese grater feeling is not as bad as everything I was feeling before the amputation. Now I was a weird kid and absolutely adored rain and thunderstorms. And if it was not for CRPS or the pain I feel 24-48hrs before rain falls, I would still be in love with rain and thunderstorms.

author: @15milemachineFairbanksAlaska
text:
I have been watching temperature and dew point.  Today here that is 35 and 25, respectively. I thought when they came up the same with the relative humidity near or at or 100%, that there was a correlation there. Some maybe, but not always.. Those are relevant factors but there are other things. Today the bar is 28.8 here with a cold wind. My pain today on a scale of 1 to 10 is about a 12. Sometimes it is higher. Horrific! I get about 5 different places around what feels in the foot area where I suffer different kind of pain. All can be severe. Sometimes Motrin alone changes it for the better in one or more of those places.. The place today seems to be un-treatable. Today is the top inside. The feeling is that a narrow slab of flesh is being ripped from my foot and the instrument pulling the flesh is jammed into the wound also. Like rubbing it in.
 I am trying to not do it inside the house but I am yelling out loud as a countermeasure as the pains strike about every minute or less. 
I once looked into whether the radiation from the Sun may play into this. I spoke with a physicist who did not hear of any correlation but said there were some German studies with that and Cardiac issues. A Canadian company had a product Farablock, which when wrapped around the concerned area would supposedly shield it from Solar Ionized particles which they claimed inflamed the nerve endings. I tried to make my own wrap such as theirs. It did not work for me.
 I am 49 years an AK amputee due to an accident. At one time I also had sensations of swirling electric shocks for 18 years. I would not sleep for a week at a time, literally! When that began to diminish after so many years there was also, at that time a change in the Solar cycle towards a minimum.
 Also there is the HARP stuff which happens to have been headquartered in Alaska, and some of that infrastructure near where I live. One site was shut down near the same time as what I mentioned about the Solar cycle. I am not saying there was anything there, but having such an extreme issue, I wonder about things.

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
Definitely weather reactive. Seems to be linked to falling pressure. All my injuries become extra…along with migraines. Weather fronts will trigger migraines for me. I have a little bit of phantom pain (I have not had severe phantom  pain post-amp). So I will experience an uptick in phantom sensations. My foot gets quite buzzy. 

Long long ago in college…after my ACL injuries…I got quite good at weather prediction and had clear differences between cold and rain sensations. After one bball game I looked at my coach and said, “I’m having a combo of pains. It’s going to do something weird like snow.” I woke the next morning to a nice blanket of snow across campus. 

I no longer have that level of accuracy. Probably too many injuries and surgeries now. I just ache. I can’t find a comfortable position. …and I have the inkling of a headache brewing. I normally have at least 24 hours warning sometimes two days. 

I got my new new leg and am starting to walk post osseointegration socket free.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
@ so far so good. Only on my fourth day so working on walking again. You know. Everything is sore from moving and working again. Plus the bone is still healing. 

I love the ease of taking my leg on and off. Also love not needing to modify my clothes.

author: @parrant8134
text: Although it rarely snows here in the Deep South it appears to me that storms in general have no affect on my phantom limb pain , AK left leg amputee here, and I have severe phantom limb pain if anyone has any suggestions I would appreciate it more than you know. I’ve tried all the medications and mirror therapy, again thanks so much.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That sounds awful, even without storms in the mix. Here's what worked for me: It occurred to me that the nerves responsible for my phantom pain run through my back, so I got my back checked out. Turns out that I had a disc bulge. When I started treating it with PT and a couple injections, my phantom pain improved. My physical therapist also checked for nerve adhesions. Turns out I had some. When we did nerve flossing/nerve gliding, it made a huge difference. Also in PT, we broke apart myofacial adhesions and worked on knotted muscles. Meds didn't seem to help me a lot, but working on the things I listed in PT made a huge difference. I've got a video that goes into more detail, but that's the quick summary. Obviously talk to your doctor, but that's what helped me. Good luck!

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
I'm a RBKA as of the start of 2024. Since I no longer had a foot to drive with, I had my truck outfitted with a new accelerator. My choices were left foot or hand controls. As I thought about which to get, I spent some time thinking about what it would be like to be bilateral. It must be very difficult for you. While my current prothesis works ok for me, I still struggle with balance issues so taking stairs requires me to hold onto a hand rail, although if I climb with just my meat foot then I can make it ok. If I was a BBKA like you, I feel like my mobility would be much more seriously challenged. I am so, so sorry that you had to have both feet removed. 😞

For what it's worth, I went with hand controls and a spinner knob on my steering wheel. It's really easy to drive. My foot was amputated because I have neuropathy in my feet which can only get worse in my residual limb as the years go by. You don't really realize how fragile your body is until one day you're laying in a hospital bed and your podiatrist walks in to tell you that your foot needs to go. I'm really glad that I did it, but it certainly wasn't my first choice.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's rough. I sometimes notice achiness in my residual limb when storms come, but it isn't as consistent as the phantom pain. Plus, the phantom limb tends to be the focus of my attention because it hurts more. I guess you could take a "snow-cation" to experience it. You could build snowmen, go sledding, and have a snowball fight.

author: @GK-ht5si
text: U Won't Believe... I Got Surgery Of Bilateral Amputation... It's been two years... But still Im.facing phantom Limb sensation 😢😢

author: @jayalbert2241
text: I lost my toe a week ago due to diabetes. And I've got phantom pain right now

author: @neeezobeatz6085
text: Why haven’t you tried TMR where they take the nerve ends and suture them to muscle ends, or even burry them deep inside muscle to prevent neuroma formation and really calm down phantom pain

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Great question- the short answer is that I prefer starting with less invasive options. I also think that identifying the main factors of why I'm having phantom pain (such as because of adhesions or back problems) influences what the best treatment plan is.

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
I suffer from the same form of stupid. I’m also the person who celebrated my knee replacement by getting a skateboard. 

I’ve definitely had an uptick in phantom pain and sensation since starting walking. The socket pressure plus moving again got things pretty active. My residuum “Maggie” has been super sore some days after increased walking. Stretching definitely helps with that issue. 

I have a Ewing amputation which means I have two muscle pairs that have been pulled and reconnected. This out of position change has had my brain trying to figure out what has happened. My biggest is feeling like my foot is twisted way out of position. It doesn’t hurt but is very odd experience. I can however activate the connected muscles in the opposite direction of the twist and it will usually relieve it. 

Frankly, it has been quite fascinating to experience the various things. 

Only 5 weeks until my stage 2 surgery for the OI. I’m hoping my walking advances enough I can go for a short hike. Working in it.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: A skateboard is a worthy way to celebrate, lol. I agree, all the weird phantom sensations are truly fascinating. I find them intriguing even when they hurt. It's just so bizarre! And hooray for the progress you're making! It sounds like it's going well, all things considered.

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
4:28 - "We all have our own style of stupid" - This is the story of my life!

I think that for anyone who may be considering an amputation and who are worried about phantom pain, this video offers hope. I know that I was quite worried about it before I had my foot removed. But I didn't have any real choice in my amputation as my foot was so severely infected (osteomyelitis) that there was no saving it. In the end, my phantom pain has not been severe or frequent, so for that I am thankful to God. In some ways it may have been good that I didn't have to make that hard decision since otherwise I might have let my concern about phantom pain stop me from making what has otherwise been a very good choice. While I'd much prefer to go back to my life as it was before, my new life is still pretty good in the grand scheme of things.

author: @jbbevan
text: Nina, I don’t know why this is showing that I only watched 12 seconds of this video as I really watched the entire thing. I don’t know how you do it somehow you strike the perfect balance between truly information and your innate sense of humor. How you mix the sense of humor with very painful subjects at times is marvelous.this is the first substantive discussion on phantom pain with actual experience and attempted cures that I have ever seen. I hope you have a good Sunday.

author: @BucketList22
text: Thank you, as a recent BKA I have been having issues with severe phantom pain and this will give me some things to discuss with my physical therapist and primary care doctor.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's hard. I'm sorry you're going through that. My phantom pain was very severe at first, then got better after a couple months. Then it got worse when I ran into other problems, such as with my back. I hope that things improve for you soon. Hang in there!

author: @BobCindyL
text: I hate my prosthetic. It turns and I constantly have to stop and put another sock on. I get muscle cramps so bad I can't walk. I feel like I am letting everyone down by not even wanting to try anymore, but, when I falter in my walking because the foot turns sideways, it gets scary. Then there is the almost constant phantom pain. I feel like a failure for pretty much giving up on it. Am I just lazy? I have a pin lock and am a btk amputation.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That sounds so discouraging. I had the same problem a year or so ago where I could only go a few steps before the socket and foot rotated. It was super frustrating. The thing that helped me was getting a new socket. Maybe that's something you could ask your doctor/prosthetist about? I had really intense phantom pain for a few years. For me, it turned out that my back had some issues that were affecting the nerves, plus some of the nerves and soft tissue were too tight and had adhesions. I made a video that can give you details, but basically working on my back and all the tight nerves and tissue made a huge difference in my phantom pain and I've felt much better ever since. I don't know if you have the same underlying issues in the mix, but it might be worth talking to a physical therapist or maybe checking your back for things like disc bulges. Good luck with everything! I hope it gets better!

author: @ersonthemesa
text: Thank you for this video....it has helped me a lot....and know to ask more questions.

author: @RuneNorwegianFlashWillem
text: Hi, i have tried many type of feets.  The one who has followed me for years now is the C161 Triton VS from Otto Bock.  VS means vertical shock system. If you go to Otto Bock´s web site you can read all about the foot. This foot is an all terrain foot. It is very good for the active person. It is good for running, walking, hiking in forrest or mountains , ride bike.. and for the person who walk and stand alot in their type of jobs.  Im a Nurse for 36 yrs and are LBKA since 86. (1986)  This foot is the VERY best I have ever used. I strongly recommend it.  I have ruined thriatlon and marathon with this foot.

author: @lrparrish227
text: I have a left BK and have gone to the pin lock system.  It is amazing and easy to take on and off.  Super secure and I love it.  I never have to worry about it coming off.  Light weight and easy to maintain.

author: @d_horqua
text: I am also a left BK and had to abandon the pin lok system due to my sleeve milking the  end of my stump. Just too much play in the end of the liner. I moved to a suction system with the vacuum pump located in the foot mechanism. It has served me well for the past 9 years but is again failing due to volume issues. I live in the middle of Wyoming where I’m finding it very difficult to locate a pros that I feel knows their craft and I can trust. I’m an amp of 50+ years.

author: @schnitzelschnizel9448
text: You don't have to go to Medical school to be a medical professional or in the medical field there.

author: @schnitzelschnizel9448
text: Get some crutches when you don't need any of it there. It is not my fault that I became a Type 2 Diabetic. It is because of Candida Albicans getting out of sink and a simple internal fungal infection not being able to get rid of it for 41-years straight!

author: @ninjahunter4784
text: I feel kinda lame asking, I got a below knee amputation about 2 years ago, it's healed, they salvaged majority of my calf muscle, and when I went to the prosthetic company, they said that usually legs are not as bulky as mine, I'm 320 pounds, and 6'4 tall I have large calf's most likely due to I've been a large male my whole life, I'm 320 pounds, but used to weigh around 600 pounds, the reason for the small story is, I feel pressure on my lower residual limb, and it's not comfortable, I don't want to complain, but it kinda feels like to much pressure on the bottom of the limb, and the person I talk to says that it could be normal, but it doesn't feel normal as to me normal would be walking without wincing in pain cause the prosthetic leg feels off its the kind that has a gel sleeve the has a socket on the bottom, but also he said initially that they were out of long sockets, and they only had short sockets, so putting it on is a nightmare takes at least 15 minutes to get it position for the socket to click in, and if it's hot it sweat a lot and gets loose very loose, to the point I feel it hurts, so I don't know, guess I'm just wishing I could get a good prosthetic, I understand they all have maintenance, but the first thing is it needs to be comfortable to a point, so any advice or info, on what could help, does it sound like a me issue or a them not listening issue, I'm kinda dumbfounded.

author: @davypig571
text: Its been a while, is there any news to share? I agree with the second opinion.

author: @guymullsr.837
text: I just want to say thank you for doing such a great job. I will continue to follow you because you spoke on everything that I needed. Please keep up the good work . 🙏🏿❤️🙏🏿❤️🙏🏿❤️

author: @onefootedphoenix
text: I will add as well if you have limb sensitivity issues there are “socket-less” options that offer more adjustability or can eliminate the need for a liner. My prosthetic uses socks instead of a liner and I would consider the Martian Bionics socket-less products as well.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I thought of your socket while I was making this. The video was getting so long, I decided to stick to common options, but I love how prosthetists can think outside the box when someone has a need. Your leg turned out so cool!

author: @onefootedphoenix
text: Some CRPS amputees I’ve seen have the pin in the bone. However, it’s easier got it to get infected.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I've wondered what that feels like. On the one hand, I hear it makes life easier getting the leg on and off. On the other hand, having hardware inside you is more invasive.

author: @d_horqua
text: I also wonder: do amps who have a pin attached to their residual bone also have to take immunosuppressant drugs for the remainder of their lives?

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: It was surprisingly hard to write. There are so many different types and varieties of legs/feet, that it's hard to make generalizations.

author: @marisamonson9647
text: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures  I can believe it. However, you're good at qualifying that it is your own experience, but then explaining why you have the opinions you do. Then, people can understand the details of the pros and cons while still recognizing that it may not be that way for every case.

author: @AprilJohnson34
text: thank you for creating this video!!! Very helpful info esp for those who are supporting people close to us that are dealing with "picking a leg". Love your content! Keep it coming!!!

author: @danaedwards5464
text: you forgot the strap method i have a strap on the bottom of my liners where the pin would normally go it goes thru a slot in the bottom of my socket and back to a loop on the top and holds with velcro

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Thanks for your input! I haven't met many amputees who use straps, so I didn't realize it's a common option. I stand corrected! (Minus the standing part...I took off my leg.)

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
I would add that the foot you try can affect how well you walk. The first one I tried was too stiff and I had to really work to flex it and get the energy return. The next one was great and I walked right away without difficulty. 

The suction system was great when I had a really snug fit. Steady and great control. Ah…then sweat. The pistoning was significant when the liner got sweaty. The other disadvantage of the suction system is that the sleeve comes farther up your leg which makes some pants difficult to wear. You might have to pull your pants down to access everything. I’ve heard this is frowned upon in public spaces. 

The osseointegration is definitely the fastest to put on and off. A quick snap and then twist of the connector and go. And it goes on in the correct position every time.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Indeed- public de-panting is frowned upon in polite society. I'm not sure how far up my vacuum sleeve and liner went up compared to your suction leg, but it was definitely higher up than my pin lock leg. I really appreciated it when I went back to pin lock and didn't have the sleeve anymore. The osseointegration sounds like it's working out really well.

author: @horrid74
text: That would be rough.  At sea the deck shifts with the swell of the sea.  To make matters worse the age sail involved wooden decks.  A slick deck in rough seas would suck.  A person able to make due in a sea environment with a pegleg wan't someone to be trifled with.

author: @Fate-guyy
text:
Yeah that’s why it was rare for a pirate to continue their career after losing a leg
The only known pirate to have continued is François le Clerc
There’s also Cornelis Jol but he was a privateer

author: @Bodao29a
text: Was wondering about buying a peg leg cosmetic and now I will, thanks for the video.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I made a video about how to make one. Basically, I had my prosthetist attach a piece of wood to a prosthetic test socket. We put a piece of durable rubber tread on the bottom of the peg. It's hard to walk on. I'm definitely more likely to fall with the peg. Good luck on your pirate-y quest!

author: @sharonrocke1929
text:
Great challenge and experiment but please don't risk strain/ injury to your other leg.  As usual, your presentation  was very eloquent.
P.S.  Your children are very beautiful.  Your "baby" is really growing up nicely and looks very smart in his glasses! 🤓  Good job Mom! 😊

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Thank you! I love my kids. My biggest complaint is that they grow up too fast. And you're very wise about taking care of my other foot. This was filmed a while ago, and I took a long break before using the peg leg again, because I wanted my foot to be happy. My physical therapist has been helping my foot feel better too.

author: @sillybitch369
text: Why not just put a crutch foot on it? Works well on my AK pegs.

author: @fls11264st
text:
Have you tried adding a piece of rubber to the bottom for traction? The same way crutches have the rubber thingies. 

Also you totally have to wear the pirate costume and peg leg  to go boot shopping and see how the store employees react to it 😂😂

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's a FANTASTIC idea! I love it! Maybe after summer, though...that pirate outfit is hot! And yes, I've got a bit of rubber on the end of the leg.

author: @fls11264st
text: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures oh yeah that jacket cannot possibly be comfortable in the summer. Hope this crazy idea comes to fruition because there’s no way they’ve ever helped a peg legged pirate shop for the perfect boot for their outfit.

author: @marisamonson9647
text: I didn't realize you did all of those events in one day! Way to go!

author: @danaedwards5464
text: great video but i think i will pass on pirate leg will be fitted for my stubbies next week starting out low to the ground because i have not walked in almost two years wish me luck

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Good luck! Honestly, the only reason why I use the pirate leg is because of YouTube and Halloween. It isn't my first choice. Stubbies sound better. Have you gotten them yet?

author: @danaedwards5464
text: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures first fitting yesterday was able to stand but need more adjustments because my hips wont go straight back in 2 weeks cant wait

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
I would think this is exhausting…and dangerous on polished floors. But I feel you are doing a proper scientific analysis of its viability. Imagine a wet and slippery ship deck rocking in stormy seas?!  

I’m in my own exhaustion now. That mix of wheelchair, sit-stand, struggle reaching, and crutches. I know you are familiar. (I’ve thought of asking my wife to do it for a day just so she will know on some level.) Plus I’m back home to my normal life but not yet back to normal energy. All will improve. Just normal post op struggles. 

Prosthetic mold is coming tomorrow and hopefully first janky steps at the end of the week. I will leave the peg leg life to you.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Have you had your first steps yet? That's exciting, even amid sleep deprivation and exhaustion. I definitely wouldn't recommend the pirate leg. I just use it for videos. I feel like you're getting through that rough initial post-op phase. Once you get home, it's like life speeds up, or at least it did for me. Then before I knew it, months had passed.

author: @CinkSVideo
text: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures  I took my first steps yesterday. We were able to refine some things in the test socket and get a foot that works for me. Because I have an osseointegration, we selected a foot that has a lot of torsional movement. I was much more comfortable in the second foot than the first, which felt pretty lousy. Typical pressure at my kneecap and behind my leg. The rest of my leg felt ok. Granted…I wasn’t letting go of those rails yet. I go back on Friday to test the next check socket and give it another go. All in all it was okay. Not great but not terrible. First steps.

author: @jbbevan
text: Yes, Nina, who made the peg leg?  Your husband?  I think you should smear a good glue on the bottom and then sprinkle sand on it...shake off the excess...let it dry and set up...and voila...more traction (but it will scratch you wood floors...no problem for Costco, though).

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: My prosthetic clinic made the leg. We added a bit of rubber on the bottom, so it isn't as slippery as it was when I first tried it out. At this point, it's more the biomechanics that make it slip out, but it's definitely worse if I'm on a slippery floor, despite the rubber tread on the bottom.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Thank you! The bed frame was a lucky thrift store find. As for the leg, I just sawed off a piece of the bedframe... just kidding! My prosthetian made it for me. He added a piece of wood to an old test socket.

author: @JamesOBrienAU
text: Thankyou forCthis video. I am planning a trip to Sweden (from Australia) in February, including a trip above the Arctic Circle. I have been to Sweden previously, prior to amputation. I am a lower right limb amputee and always wear shorts, it's just easier to explain things etc, so I was interested you had long pants and an exposed prosthetic. Great stuff. Any tips re that?

author: @justindoyle3072
text: Since you lost your leg how does it feel to get back into karate and sports you used to play

author: @marisamonson9647
text: It's amazing what you appreciate when you lose something. I'm even excited after a winter of being pregnant to be able to come back next year to shovel my walks again.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: And tell Little Miss I love the heart she sent. Here's one for her: 💖

author: @CinkSVideo
text: The inspo spots we all see like to show the marathon and the mountains climb, but it really is the mundane that feels the most triumphant in recovery. Standing on two feet to make breakfast is divine. Soon I will be able to walk my dogs. These are big wins in my book.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Your dogs will be thrilled! It really is the simple things that make the biggest difference.

author: @sharonrocke1929
text: Beautiful memories! When the little man said, "I got up!", that really resonated with me. Isn't that what we have to do? - get up and go on with living.  I  went to the beach this summer, the first time since my above-the-knee amputation 3 years ago.  I took one step in the dense sand with my prosthesis and face-planted! 😂🤣😂 Everyone, (including a couple of lifeguards) was so alarmed but I was fine and just couldn't stop laughing! I was so happy to be doing something that I hadn't been able to do for so long. You are so right- if we wait for things to be perfect in order to find happiness, we will die slow, miserable deaths.  Thank you for what you do.  May the Christ of Christmas fill your heart with His joy, peace and hope.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Thank you for your heart-warming story! That sounds like something I would do- fall in the sand then have a good laugh about it. Life is a comedy at times! You're so right- we've just got to get up and keep living. Merry Christmas!

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
This is so sweet! The love that you feel for your son (and your whole family) comes oozing through this video.

As for the uphill battles that you mention, I was telling my adorable wife yesterday that with all of the challenges that we faced together in 2024 with my amputation, my goal is now to live as long as I possibly can. We worked hard to get back on track, so we want to stay here. 🙂

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I love that goal, and that you have your sweet wife by your side. I think such an intense 2024 will make future years even sweeter. Merry Christmas!

author: @monicaoconnor2357
text: Thank you once again for putting things in perspective. I look forward to seeing your videos every week!

author: @paul-gv6hl
text: I can appreciate your situation as a pirate.  I find getting around a bit awkward since I operate more in the Long John Silver Mode (no left leg and one crutch).

author: @TDCflyer
text:
I, too, am a leg amputee as well as a youtuber.
Worst thing to happen: When you're back home after a long day of filming just to discover you're *missing footage.*

author: @marisamonson9647
text: I have been wondering if you're just immune to feeling awkward. It turns out you're just brave and persistent. ;) Thanks for pushing through the awkwardness to make us these awesome videos!

author: @justindoyle3072
text:
Love the fact you can still
Do
Martial arts I want to take it but still a bit timid to try

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I know what you mean. It's hard testing the waters to see what still works. I found that having the right group of people makes all the difference.

author: @Carrieliney
text: I was weird in public on purpose/for fun a lot as a teenager, and I think it really helped when I started using a wheelchair. A lot of people describe feeling incredibly self-conscious in their chairs, which is not something I've really experienced. So I think there's something to be said for being weird in public.

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text: I love it that you are apparently able to see the upside of any situation 🙂

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's cool! Just goes to show that goofing around can be a real life skill!

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text: You need to take up method acting. That's where you basically **become** the character that you're playing. So no more Stephanina the YouTuber who is embarrassed when she films a pirate segment at the local gym. Instead, you are Pegleg the Swashbuckler, seeking to steal treasure from the unsuspecting locals at a nearby sports center! It is my understanding that what made William Shatner so believable as Captain Kirk in the Star Trek series is that when he was onscreen then he literally became Captain Kirk in his own mind. So this is your opportunity to show off your acting skills. Stop worrying about what others think of you and instead go show all those weak-minded and poorly dressed people in shorts and sweat pants that they are no match for the feared one-legger with a camera! 🤣

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Argh! Ye be right, matey! If they give me any lip, they can walk the plank! 🏴‍☠️

author: @kaitlyn__L
text:
I’ve had that first thing happen with my wheelchair a few times too. It trips up on something, but I somehow catch myself, briefly stand, and sit back down in the chair. Only once or twice on level ground have I fallen on my front.

And, yeah, uphill and downhill is relatable too 😅 I had some near misses at 20mph downhill and make sure to stay at or under 10mph now. I do a kind of ABS-inspired thing on the pushrims (not recommended without leather gloves!) so I don’t dislocate my wrists trying to slow down.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I try to follow a speed limit too...sometimes gravity gets the better of me, though. I'm glad that at least you managed to avoid a big crash.

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
Can you imagine the stories that squirrel 🐿️ is telling?! 

“I was minding my own business and then out of the blue some cyborg tried to kill me. I gave her a piece of my mind $@@?!#*%! And she scurried off in complete terror. 

She seemed strangely familiar though…like a pirate I once saw. Naaa. Couldn’t be. Cyborgs are dangerous.” 

Btw…I’ve totally ditched my crutches at the 2.5 week mark of socket life. Somebody’s walking.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text:
🤣🤣🤣
Love the squirrel POV!
So glad you're up and walking without crutches! Way to go!

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text: Congrats on losing the crutches! I went through this myself a month ago, though my wife was quite nervous watching me walk around the house without anything other than my prosthesis holding me up.

author: @CinkSVideo
text: @toddbu-WK7Lmine too. She was quite afraid at first worried I would fall. That settled pretty quickly as she realized I wasn’t being stupid and had good balance.

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text: All I could think of while you talked about your leg staying on when the squirrel cut you off is that this is a disadvantage of the pin lock system. Sometimes having a break away leg is a good thing

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I've thought about that, even before the crazy squirrel. Have you ever used a break-away leg? What was it like?

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
@StephaninasAmputeeAdventures The only way that I'd ever have a need for a breakaway leg is for those times that I might accidentally fall off the couch during my nap. 🙂

I have an appointment with my prosthetist in a few weeks to try out a new computerized foot for a few hours. If I can remember, I'll see if he knows anything

author: @marisamonson9647

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures

I'm a right BKA and have always enjoyed going out with the grandchildren on Halloween, I have an authentic Jack Sparrow costume and this last year I made a peg leg for my prosthetic side, and you are right, it's really hard to walk on that peg! I found, by trial and error, that one needs to keep one's  knee straight, in other words try to walk as if you had a completely stiff knee- it give more stability, the gait sucks but it is more stable.  Your costume is pretty cool too!

author: @andrew_radio

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures

author: @parrant8134

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text:
That's how I felt! It's so hard not being able to be active. Having the right leg makes such a difference. Here's a link to the application I used:
https://challengedathletesfoundation.my.site.com/Grants/s/

author: @michellduer3604
text: I've just discovered your videos by chance only to watch the goodbye video 😒.  I hope to see more of your insights in the future and perhaps on Facebook/Meta/whatever name it chooses to have next month, lol.  Those of us in the limb loss/difference community always enjoy seeing perspectives that either mirror the thoughts we have (and think we are the ONLY one feeling like that) or to gain perhaps a new perspective on things.  Keep being you and enjoy your life journey!  Be Blessed!

author: @jeanplamondon7670
text: Stephanina! I don't know if you will see this but thank you for everything you shared on YouTube. 4 months ago my husband had to have an unexpected BKA and we were lost on what to expect, how to prepare and then how to cope. Your videos have been the single most helpful source of information for us to get through everything to date. Even today I am watching your tutorials on adding a zipper to pants :). But more than that, from the beginning, your story inspired us, showed us what is possible and helped us frame our mindset to stay positive through this challenge. We can't thank you enough for all you shared. 👏🦿❤

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
We love you, Stephanina! ❤ Your family comes first so please take care of them before you take care of us. 

I want to respectfully disagree with just one thing that you said. While I've found your "serious" videos to be both touching and inspiring, I feel like you're discounting the impact of your "silly" videos too much. I likely would not have found your channel if I hadn't specifically searched for peg leg videos. As a new amputee (I just had my one year ampuversary on Feb 6! 🙂), having you show your range of emotions from bad to good has helped me immensely. You show us every week that you are as normal as any two-legger, and that is very inspirational.

Anyway, I'm glad that you're still able to contribute to our community through shorts instead of long-form videos. My prayer is that God blesses you and that young family of yours.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text:
That means a lot! I enjoy a pinch of silliness, so it's good to hear that it did some good. Life is too short not to laugh. 
And thank you for your understanding! My family just needs more time (hence I've had a hard time messaging you back- I think that 3D printed aligner is brilliant!). The kids have been loving having more time just to do ordinary family things. I think it's good for our family.
Thank you, as always, for your kind words and support. And happy ampu-versary!

author: @tboneaja
text: Thank you for your knowledge, your beautiful and positive, I'm a LBKA since 2015 lost it in a motorcycle accident, I'm in a pin lock system now for about a month, I really like it, all my previous systems were suction but I started having issues with sores and volume changes I'm 64, it's a hard thing to go thru when you've had your leg for 54 years and all of a sudden it's gone, thank you again !

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text: Based on your recommendation as well as watching other YouTubers, I just received my AllPro a couple of days ago and I ABSOLUTELY LOVE IT!!! Like you, I upgraded from a hydraulic foot (K2) and the first thing I noticed was a new spring in my step - quite literally.. I also love the fact that I now have dorsiflexion that my old foot didn't offer. So now walking feels very natural. It takes a bit to get used to the foot because your leg muscles (especially your quads) have to help with balance that used to be performed by your ankle, but I already notice a big improvement. So, from the bottom of my heart, a big THANK YOU and virtual hug from someone whose life just got that much better with this foot. I have a lot to be thankful for this Christmas season. God has blessed me with the gift of salvation through to work of His Son, Christ Jesus, and now I have a shiny new stocking stuffer in the way of a foot that helps me feel just a bit less of a robot and more human. I have been blessed beyond belief!

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That makes my heart so happy! I'm so glad that you were able to get the AllPro. It made a big difference for me, too. And you're 200% right- we're so blessed to celebrate the birth of Christ and remember just how blessed we truly are.

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
Another factor comes into play. There are some prosthetic feet that are really difficult for people with smaller feet and others that tend to work for most everyone. I like my foot. It’s has a big range of motion so I’m totally fine on uneven ground. It also has a soft heel strike that feels very natural. 

Also, one challenge is that some of the prosthetic manufacturers are buying prosthetic clinics and then restrict the availability to their brands, which is never going to be good for everyone.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I hadn't thought about the foot size issue from that perspective. Food for thought.👍🏼 That's unsettling about the manufacturers buying up the clinics. I like a little more diversity in the market.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's so hard! What country are you in? I wonder if there are any grants/programs that can help. Prosthetic sockets from one amputee don't generally fit other amputees. The fit is so customized, and it's painful to walk on if the shape is even a little off.

author: @vinnnceeee

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
As always, an amazing video! Being a relatively new amputee (< 1 year), I still learn a lot from your videos.

I tried an Ossur computerized foot a couple of weeks ago and I liked it, except for having to charge it. This week I am trying another computerized foot. The problem with those feet is that they are heavy like you said (about 7-8 pounds). You also need to charge them. But they do make going on a long hike on uneven ground much easier. My current hydraulic foot lacks dorsiflexion, which is what I find makes walking backward harder. Oh, and that computerized foot I tried had what is called "relaxed mode" where it automatically relaxes the toes so you can easily pull your foot back underneath you when sitting. This makes standing from a seated position much, much easier. However, in your video you described the issue when you take a step forward and your toe is pointing up and you almost fall over on your face. After taking one step in the computerized foot then it automatically points the toe back to level, so I found myself standing and doing the "hokey pokey"... I put my right foot in and I shake it all about. 🙂

Random question: Do you often check Patreon for messages? I love your channel so much that I recently became a supporter so I left you a message of encouragement there. Being as busy as you are, I figured that you probably don't have much time to check yet another email inbox

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Whoa! That computerized foot sounds amazing! I hope I get to try one someday. That boggles my mind what technology can do. We've come a long way from wooden legs strapped on with leather. I got your kind message on Patreon, thank you for your support! Did you see my reply?  Sorry I'm sometimes slow- I had sick kids and thigs were extra crazy.

author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
@StephaninasAmputeeAdventures Sorry that I haven't responded to your Patreon message yet, but I plan to. Like you, things have been really busy around the house.

I had to give up the new foot earlier today because the one-week trial was over. I'm already missing it. 🙂 My current hydraulic foot has no dorsiflexion, making climbing stairs and going on long walks difficult. The computerized foot has a much greater range of motion. That extra range makes walking much more natural, though it takes a bit of getting used to because it takes more effort to keep your balance until you learn. But when walking out of the prosthetist's office today (the word prosthetist is a noun 😉) , I noticed the difference in the first 20 feet.

The next steps for me (pun intended) are a visit to my PCP and then a LOT of paperwork for insurance. Lord willing, if all goes as planned then I *might* have a new limb by the end of the year. There are no guarantees that insurance will sign off on this, but both my prosthetist and the rep from the foot company said that more and more insurance companies are willing to consider the extra expense because the computerized foot can help reduce future injuries. So we'll just have to wait for now.

I hope to get back to you this weekend on your other message. Thanks again so much for all your amazing videos. I'm really hoping to get me some of those zipper-leg p-p-p-p-pants
