author: @toddbu-WK7L
text:
Hey Stephanina, from one professed dork to another, please let me say how much of an impact you have already made on my life as an RBKA. As soon as I knew back in February of this year that I'd be losing my foot (due in part to my own stupidity 😞), I started searching for answers to my questions on amputation. Not wanting to let a good opportunity for humor to pass me by, I asked Mr. Google about dressing up as a pirate and your videos popped up to the top of my list. After enjoying the first video, I quickly watched many more from your channel and found lots of great info. For someone with little spare time, you have done amazing work!

Before I go on, I'd like to say that you should never make a video because you feel that you have to. If you're not feeling up to it or are too busy or whatever then feel free to blow it off. Even if you decided that you no longer had time to make videos then you've already made a huge contribution to the amputee community and we thank you for that. You should never feel pressure to make videos. Ok, one exception... if you have any more squirrel encounters then you gotta tell us. :🙂 But seriously, you have yourself and your family to be responsible for. You don't need any extra pressure. Make videos because you want to, not because you feel that you have to.

Let me finish by saying that we not only love your channel, but we love **YOU**. In a world where so much is fake, you are the real deal. You laugh with us and cry with us, and you are often so silly. It seems that you have little to hide. I look forward to every new video. I say this not to pressure you, but to encourage you. If making videos is what you want to do, we'll be here to watch. If you don't have time, we'll understand. Either way, it won't change how we feel about you. You are an amazing young woman, Stephanina, and your family is very blessed to have you as a wife and mother.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Thank you for your kindness! That was so touching. I will definitely let you know about future battles with squirrels! The past couple weeks were extra busy, and I think it was just wearing on me. I felt better after I stopped and reflected on why I do what I do. Sometimes we need that, don't we? Today I'm feeling good, and the kids had a great Halloween. Which just proves what I always say- even on hard days, good days are sure to follow. Thank you for picking me up when I was down!

author: @the_d12rose
text:
Thanks for sharing your story, I'm happy you've found the core of your dream.  Your original instructor really let you down - I firmly believe making space for everyone (all abilities, genders, identities, etc) is the highest duty of an instructor.

I'm in the midst of my own volatile journey - being trans & queer in martial arts has been rough, not many welcoming gyms around me.  Consequently, I've found myself entering the role of instructor *way* earlier than I anticipated in order to create a space for my community.  It's been hard honestly, but very worth it.  The dream evolves. 💖

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That sounds challenging but simultaneously inspiring how you've persevered despite those challenges. Life is rarely fair, but at the end of the day, there are still things we can do to follow our dreams.

author: @jbbevan
text: I knew you were in martial arts (before) but had no idea (or wasn't paying attention, adequately) that it was so dear to you nor that you had achieved black belt status (which they can't take away from you).  So thanks for sharing and becoming vulnerable in this story.  The various elements of your life don't logically hang together...like pieces of a puzzle one would not expect to fit together...but they certainly add up to a very unique individual.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Your observation made me chuckle! For years, I've been heartily amused whenever people in our congregation realize that the organist punches people in her spare time. (Which makes me sound awfully violent!) Martials was a major part of my life before, but there were, and always will be, things that are more important.

author: @paulbudak6316
text: Don't  let someone's freak accident stop you.  You are differently abled, is all.  Fencing looks fun.  I do Planet Fitness again.  Can't do tread mill but I am able to wiegt lift.  Having a physical set back SUCKS!  Just do what ya can.

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
I’m glad you found fencing, but shame on your teachers for pushing you out of your martial arts practice. They had no right to take that from you. Anyone watching paraolympic athletes knows that there is a multitude of ways to compete. I’m sorry you’ve lost that…for now. 

We all have to grieve the life path we were on. You often hear the phrase “I want to get my life back,” but the truth is that we really need to grieve that past and find the new life we have ahead, which could look quite different. It can still be very good, but is likely different than what we had. That’s okay.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: You worded that so well- like it or not, we can't always get back the life we had before. I'm grateful that I found something that scratches the same itch, so to speak, but it's one of those things where I think I'll always have some grief about what I left behind. I'm just glad that I have something that makes me so happy.

author: @CinkSVideo
text: @ Remember that we don’t have to leave things behind forever. There may be a time when you can and will return to this practice that feeds your spirit.

author: @laurieboldis1537
text: Wise words , as always. It does my heart good to see you finding some light and happiness❤️

author: @justindoyle3072
text: I have had the same issue since loosing my leg and wanted to get into martial arts I have ran into schools that don’t want to teach me I hate the attitude of some schools still looking have not found one yet

author: @marisamonson9647
text: I'm so glad you found something new that challenges you. You truly are a great athlete, and you deserve an exciting challenge. Not to mention, it adds to your experiences for writing. 😉

author: @vinnnceeee
text: thank you for this video. you always inspire us, i know it's hard as an amputee myself i cant do the things that i love anymore due to mobility, but i keep on fighting.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: You're amazing! Just the simple act of going on is truly a testament to the strength of the human spirit. Keep going!

author: @johnfallon7206
text: Well done Nina,i wish you all the best,i had spinal fusion surgery on 4 levels and a decompression 2years ago which left my left leg with no power ,i had to get my leg amputated 2 months ago and now been told that i am not a candidate for a prosthetic because i have spinal cord injury so it looks like i will have to continue to use a wheelchair, i try and stay positive but as you know it is hard, anyway i will keep praying for all who are in pain and in this position, best of luck to ye all

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's rough! You've been through so much. For about a year, I didn't know if I would ever be able to walk on a prosthesis, because of issues in the other leg. Some days I was resigned and relatively okay with it. Other days it was really hard to accept. I'm sorry you're having to cope with that news. Hang in there. I'll keep you in my prayers!

author: @jbbevan
text: Always a great balance between information (usually from a different perspective than other sources) and humor.  Nina, I am glad you are in a better place for this ampuversary than in either of the previous ones...at this rate the new normal may become tolerable by #5.  Continued good luck with that.

author: @grievusdahagangus4209
text: Really difficult question for you: Did your marriage/relationship survive?  Can you offer suggestions toward family/friends to make your relationships easier?

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Great question, though definitely a hard one. Amputation definitely was hard in my marriage. Every time my husband saw me, it reminded him of what he was stressed/anxious about, so he had a hard time being around me in the weeks leading up to amputation, which was hard on me. After amputation, when he saw that I was still me, he felt much better. Some other relationships (with friends and extended family) definitely struggled. I've heard other amputees say the same thing. I think that the passage of time combined with not giving up is what worked for me. Eventually the stressful period gave way to a "new normal." It was just rough waiting it out. Even though I wanted people to be extra patient and comforting to me, I ended up trying to provide extra comfort to them in order to get everyone through it. Maybe that isn't how it "should" be, but that was the reality I had to work with, so I did what I had to. I'm sorry if you're going through that, too.

author: @kaitlyn__L
text:
Oh, that leg looks great! I assume the arc is made of a composite material? (Oh yeah, I can see it flexing more in the clips midway through.) 

I'm glad your pain is consistent nowadays.

My American pals personally used the "pain and torture" acronym expansion rather than the "patient torture" one, haha. Alas, here we just call it "physio" rather than "PT", so disabled people here don't get to make jokes like that. 

Oh, that looks like quite a nice recumbent bicycle!

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I'm not sure what the exact material is, but it's cool because it can compress and have a little spring to it. I liked my first prosthetic foot, but I like this new springy one even more.

author: @darrinbdc6837
text: its difficult, but you gradually work through it, my biggest issue is getting to suppport myslef, I applied for disability, but its a long term game , in the meantime what can i do to earn a living,  i was in heating and air conditioning and no way I can do that now

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: That's a hard one. I tried to apply for disability and ran into some problems (specifically, I hadn't worked enough over the past ten years, because I was home taking care of my special needs kids). I hope that it works out for you. It's so much harder when your occupation doesn't work post-amputation.

author: @CinkSVideo
text:
So far, I’ve avoided any prolonged depression. I definitely hit some lows a couple  weeks after getting home (6wks post op) where I was just crazy tired from the added responsibilities and activities. 

Just started walking this week.  My goal is to do a little more each day. I break up walking into two sessions each day. 15 minutes then 18 minutes then 24 minutes total for the day…we’ll see what today holds. I have zero ability at this stage to determine if I need to add a sock. It’s really wonderful to stand as I make breakfast even if a bit uncomfortable at first. 

Holy moly the muscles up my back and core are sore and tired from no real use the last of months.

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: Hooray for walking! That's wonderful! I felt so tall when I started walking again, like a kid on dad's shoulders. Everything looks different when you double in height!

author: @rachaelteacher
text:
Dear Colleen, Thank you very much for making this video. I am a recent amputee looking for answers about prosthetic arms for people with trans-humural amputations. I hope your implant helped with your phantom pain. I am blessed that my phantom sensations have subsided significantly. If you are still struggling with this, I'd be happy to share my experience with you. If you do get a prosthetic that is useful, please let me know.
Wishing you all the best,
Rachael Lilienthal, Orlando FL

author: @shabanashaik2784
text: How to use it while sitting? How to avoid hurting buttock while sitting wearing KaFO

author: @RealLifeProsthetics
text: Great question, we’ll definitely add this to our list of videos to address. In the meantime, sitting comfortably while wearing a KAFO generally depends on proper brace alignment, posture, chair height, and cushioning to help reduce pressure and protect the skin. Because comfort can vary from person to person, your orthotist is the best resource for guidance specific to your brace and needs.

author: @RealLifeProsthetics
text: Lots of variables; like what you would need and your insurance coverage.  Call us for a consultation.

author: @newatheistplusxyz
text: who are you why are you sending your videos clips why are you waisting your time or waisting me for nothing

author: @amputeecharm
text: I think it's meaningful, and I really like it, but it doesn't exist in reality. And many people like it just like me. The main reason is that it doesn't exist in reality, or is very rare; even if there are a few instances, most people wouldn't even notice them.

author: @amputeecharm
text:
In real life, you rarely come across this, which is one reason. Another reason is that my definition of beauty is a bit different from most people’s. Of course, it may also satisfy some people’s sense of curiosity, and it can have a positive, motivational effect on life.

Overall, that’s the meaning behind why I post these. Most importantly, I like it—I even enjoy the process of making the videos. I’ve spent quite a lot on memberships for this hobby. Every video is made with care, and I always delete the ones that don’t work out.

So you may not like it, but that won’t have any negative impact on either of us. Thank you for your reply.

author: @bodieb.1239
text: A few years ago I had seen a young woman in a wheelchair at a store I was shopping in. She was covered up and it was obvious was unable to communicate but I to this day remember her beauty and I truly believe with all my heart she was a visiting angle. To this day I wish I had stopped and just looked and smiles at her but I felt awkward because of her care givers. I will never forget the beauty in her face and the glow that I saw. Maybe it's the adversity that created such a genuine beauty and not a package. All I know she would make anyone she came in contact with a better person and I missed my chance for that fleeting moment was never to repeat itself.  All of you ladies are the greatest.

author: @FaeriePrincex
text:
Im not an amputee but I struggle with stairs. My first apartment had been built in the 1910s-1930s, and the stairs had been painted over and over, making them very smooth and slippery, and then the bottom was tile, with a single drain that didn't drain directly into a drain, but ended a little over a drain, so if anyone had a shower or bath, and used soap, oils ect, it could easily overflow and leave the floor extremely slippery. 
I absolutely adored the place and would give anything to live there again, but im also a wheelchair user, and it just isn't accessable

Soo happy for you and your family living your best lives abroad!
All the best always!!!!!

I’m going to try and be a living donar match for him, he has had my heart for 25 years, why not give him my kidney and part of my liver also? He’s my bff.. I want to grow old with him. Diabetes is a horrible disease, I pray someday we have cures for it and so many others. Sending love & hugs! Keep sharing your journey, you are an inspiration to so many and we love you and your family! ❤

author: @oppositepuppy
text: My husband's prostethic is made from titanium. You just need rust proof screws but also it's unlikely for the bolts to fail that quickly even in a weaker state yeah ok don't leave them that way however they shouldn't give out on you any time soon. Even rusted bolts take a great deal to break you'd literally have to be kicking a wall with some kind of force daily for them to just snap off. Rust eats metal and makes it weaker correct however has anyone tried to get a rusted bolt out of anything? Most of the time you gotta smack it with a hammer several times before it even comes close to coming loose nevermind breaking. Would always recommend changing yes but worrying not so much unless it takes 3-5 months  or constant submersion in water then I'd worry.

author: @howareyou-d7y

author: @MicroMoves_Matter
text: I hope a prosthetist or someone with an amputation knows the answer! It’s comforting to hear someone name how complicated bodies can be. There’s relief in knowing we’re not failing—we’re adapting.❤

author: @creativestudios3533
text: Well, here's a thing. Sometimes, metals don't like each other. You have to find out what type of metal is contacting with The Other metal. Yeah, For example, galvanized does not like copper something like that. They eat each other, so talk to your processes and find out what screws they're using, try stainless steel screws, you go to home depot, take off the original screw. Just one of them and size it on the sizing mechanism, they have there, it could be metric. It could be standard, you have to find out the threading are different too sometimes.  Just don't lose that screw. That's the only way to do it, or you could talk to your prosthesis.

author: @jbbevan
text: Keira, you are a very interesting young lady.  I'm an engineer and interested in prosthetics (though retired...and old enough to be your grandfather).   But if I could back up 20 years I think I would like to work for Otto Bock (or similar).  I do speak fluent German, so that might be a help..if I were 20 or 30 years younger.  Secondarily, I have lost two wives to relatively sudden deaths (18 hours each) and am frequently asked to comment on living with adversity.  So I "study" people like you to get some other stories so I don't have to talk about myself so much.  BUT your case is different, isn't it?  I think you are the first amputee I have come across that actually celebrates her amputation and feels that her life is better off for "having your arm off."  That makes your story interesting...for sure.  In 2013 I drove from Swansea to Preston.  I'm LDS so I wanted to visit the longest continually functioning branch of our church in the world...Preston, England.  My own ancestors are from Wales.  Anyhow, you get the picture.  You are very pleasant to listen to and you don't get even near being maudlin about your situation.  Thanks for sharing all.  Best wishes to both you and Stumpie (which sounds like the name of an 8th dwarf).  PS: I know these videos are all about 4 years old, so I'm late to the game.

author: @IDYLBERRY
text: Hi Mrs. Opiner. Sorry to hear you had the flu twice. Glad you recovered. Was thinking of you today wondering why no posts for a while. I did hear of mirror therapy when the phantom pain hits. Be careful what you do with that foot shell, someone might turn you in for abuse. When my wife's little thigh starts to spasm she has me hold the end firmly. She says it helps but if the spasms continue she takes a couple Alleve's and that usually stops the spasms. I know it's a different pain than yours as her leg was amputated 5 inches below the hip 58 years ago. Good to see you again, stay well.

author: @SheOpines
text: Are the pains less frequent now that so much time has past? Don't you also think that calling it "Phantom" gives the impression it's not "Real"?

author: @IDYLBERRY
text:
My wife was 13 when she lost her leg to cancer.
“Phantom” is like a ghost or sprit, we would relate to being dead.
Her phantom limb sensation lasted for 4-5 years then all was fine for 20 or so years. 
She wore a leg and started experiencing spasms in her middle 30’s with and without the leg on. She said sometimes it lasted for 3 days and she couldn’t do anything to make the pain stop. Couldn’t go to work and just wiped her out. 
In her middle 40’s she stopped wearing the leg and went to crutches. It didn’t stop the spasms which she says is like a "Charley Horse". Only the front thigh muscle or tendon remains, the gluts have atrophied away. The spasms result in the thigh lifting.
The thigh is like a weather vane, sensitive barometric changes. 
But the remaining nerve ending still feel what was there. You are probably hearing this from some of your new acquaintances. 
“Phantoms” One of my girlfriends had this rocking chair that would “grab” people that sat in it. Sometimes while sitting in it we couldn’t move or talk, we were just stuck in it. She gave it to one of our friends. The first friend told us that he could hear the chair rocking and he gave it to another friend. The second friend said he seen it rocking on its own. Come to find out someone had died in the chair.

author: @SheOpines
text:
Woah! That type of pain lasting for 3 days! Horrible. I feel so sad for her
. Is she using a prosthetic leg now? How do you do as a Carer? 
My husband has a big job. Luckily I'm an independent cuss! LOL

author: @IDYLBERRY
text:
A thigh spasm woke her this morning but she let me sleep as I put in a good day yesterday with yard work. 
I’m 65, she is 71 and we have been together for 21 years. 

She quit using the prosthetic leg about 35 years ago due to the hassle of having to remove it to use the toilet. Very inconvenient while at work and was a contributor to pain. It also slowed her mobility.

As her caregiver I do everything that requires the heavy lifting such as vacuuming, changing bed sheets, laundry, 99% of driving and the big cleaning.. 
She does things such as bed making and some of the lighter cleaning, food prep but doesn’t cook. We bought a stool on wheels she uses while in the kitchen.
When we have stayed in hotels without a shower seat I need to join in to keep her from falling. For driving she has an attachment that transfers the gas pedal to the left side.
We have taken many cross country motorcycle trips. We also ride a tandem bicycle.

She will be on her own for about 6 weeks this year while I travel. I’m concerned about her falling and not being able to get up again. 

I hope she will be able to give me care when I have a double hip replacement later this year.

How is your husband coping with your new condition?
Hope you’re having a good weekend.

author: @SheOpines
text:
IDYLBERRY : it seems we are always having to compromise between comfort and mobility. It is such a bummer. I would imagine that your wife didn't awaken you about her thigh cramp because she would rather cope. No reason for you both to suffer.
I also would be concerned about a double hip replacement. I can't imagine how you can manage without some help. Either of you could fall or other bad situation. Things can go from bad to worse. Needing assistance might be one of the hardest aspects of being an amputee.

author: @IDYLBERRY
text:
Hi,  She may have thought the spasm would stop on its own. If she asks me to hold it I do but I can only lay on my left side for a few minutes. I gave her 1 Advil yesterday afternoon but the thigh is still spasming today and she's cranky. She tries to keep herself occupied as to not dwell on it. Nothing I can do.

I want to have both hips replaced in 1 operation as not to be anaesthetized twice or go through therapy a second time. The doctor said this is becoming more common.
If I move my leg wrong while sleeping the pain wakes me up.

Were you subjected to multiple surgeries for your leg?

It’s a beautiful day here and I need to ride the motorcycle.
Enjoy your day!

author: @SheOpines
text:
The first time I heard of a double hip replacement was Barry Manilow. That was years ago. 
As for your motorcycle passion? Ekkkkkk!

author: @IDYLBERRY
text: I hope you are doing well today. I talked with the surgeon 2 weeks ago and he is only willing to do the left hip at this time. So I'll take what I can get. Do you fear riding a motorcycle? I've ridden well over 100k miles and yes I've went down once but picked it up and rode on. We have a Goldwing so it's comfortable and quite. I've planned a solo ride for Sept. through Montana, Wyoming, S. Dakota, Idaho, Washington, Oregon, and back to CA. For years I surfed in the red triangle around S.F. bay and never worried about sharks. From the novel Dune, "fear is the little killer that keeps on killing." Always good to hear back from you!

author: @IDYLBERRY
text:
I thought you got lost, good to see you back.

What ever happened to the self winding legs?

author: @SheOpines
text:
I didn't. I was actually in the hospital when I barely bumped my toe. They sent me home, saw my regular Doc. 2nd Er trip, they sent me home because nothing was broken. They didn't check for blood circulation. Then saw my regular a Doc again.
ER for third time. Finally they realized I had no blood return and the foot was (unknown to me) black on the bottom. So 3 visits ER two to the Doc. What I didn't know was that I had a flu that turned to pneumonia then septic. So 1 week in ICU (mostly unconscious). They saved my life because the septic was spreading, lung were bleeding and other organs struggling. Had brain swelling due to high platelets and the anesthesia. I am very very lucky. I had no idea this could happen. Bumping the foot didn't seem like a big deal. My pain threshold is very high. 
Thanks for asking Morris!

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text:
I still have bad phantom pain BUT learned something super helpful. One of my specialists said that since amputees use wheelchairs after amputation, and the body isn't equally supported due to the short leg, it's common to develop back problems which can manifest as phantom limb pain. I got a back MRI and sure enough, the nerves running down to my amputated leg are pinched. Now I can start doing something about the pain. If you're having bad phantom limb pain, you could ask your doctor about a back MRI and have a specialist take a look at it.
Good luck!

author: @King_Fred_II
text:
How do you react if people stare?
When I was a child I always stared at everyone and everything that was extraordinary, and my mother always told me to stop staring when disabled people were the subject. The only thing she caused was: I found it very difficult to speak to disabled people... until I met a woman online and I fell in love with her without ever having seen her... and guess what: she is disabled (muscular disease)! Things have changed since then; now it's much easier for me to talk to disabled people. And that's my second question: what's your advice to people who find it difficult to talk to disabled people?

author: @adrienlagrange9286
text: Hi! I'm very late regarding the last comments of the video, but I just started studying biomecanics and prosthetic. I noticed that you often wonder why the doctor decided to keep as much as possible, cutting in the middle of the knee instead of going higher in the thigh. I've been told that the most resistant to load stumps are, in order: lower leg, knee, thigh. That means that if you were cut higher, you would probably feel more painful putting your weight on your prosthetic leg. Let me know what you think about this!

author: @maddystout4817
text: I'm an amputee as well. I was born with out my tibia in my right leg an radius in my right arm. I have never met or talked to another amputee who is close to my age. The only people I've met were all 30 years older then me and dealing with VERY different issues. I would LOVE to get in touch with you and just talk, because most of my friends and parents have no idea how hard it can be and I'd love to talk to some one who does! If you would like to get in touch email me at Maddylee06@gmail.com

author: @Arthur172B
text: Hi Aimee! What a video! You have put more (and more interesting) info into it than any other I have seen. The fear of amputees, I had also, but because of an unusual situation (family history) combined with a 1952 chance encounter with a World War II magazine which I saw in the 8th grade. It showed a DAK amputee (executed prisoner) in a Japanese prison, I almost got sick before escaping the library. This was soon followed TV clip of a living lady DHD, which was very shocking, changing my life.

Depends. I rarely go any measurable distance without it. 
If I'm in bed without it on and need something at the other end of my room, I'll hop or crawl. 
I crawl around my house often.

author: @Arthur172B
text: When I eventually practiced enough, on the armpit crutches, I was able to feel so light that it was like floating, the crutches even felt unnecessary, a totally amazing feeling. It' s all in setting the exact length. I stand totally upright (NO leaning forward) and set the tips almost a foot in front of, and to the side of your toes. I am 5 ft 10 in, so adjust accordingly. Learn to reduce the up-and-down motion to almost zero, and the effort is reduced a lot. That is the secret to saving energy

author: @Arthur172B
text:
If you can put up with the constant irritation of the tops rubbing on your ribs, OK, but just keep the tops well BELOW your armpits, so NO pressure occurs. All the body weight stays on the hands and arms just like with elbow style ones. 

My wife needed crutches for an Achille's Tendon problem,  the PT immediately remarked that in 20 years she had NEVER had a patient arrive with them. Also, she mentioned a tragic case where a man used axillary crutches improperly and eventually lost his ARMS.

author: @Arthur172B
text:
I have seen numerous videos of PERFECT, graceful crutching with forearm crutches, and the control over where to place the tips is much better, and you have better mobility overall. I had to find a lot of these videos, to learn how to use crutches to "train" my wife, who hates the old style, due to a bad past experience. 

When I see someone struggling along with a pile of towels on top of their crutches, I know the pain will NOT go away with this trick, the problem is in where the pressure is

author: @AmputeeLife

author: @tritrack
text: @AmputeeLife Won't necessarily be easier to use or give you greater freedom.  With underarm crutches you can have the free use of both arms & hands when standing still, and you will develop the ability to carry things, objects, etc either single-handedly or both hands by tucking the crutches under your arms & swinging them forward as you move your body forward. Can't do that with forearms. Forearms are dependant on your hands/arms always being in control of them..

author: @Regislive
text: Just wondering why you don't use forearm crutches they are so much more easier to use and be able to do things with....I would think they would give you greater freedom.

author: @sepaku1968
text: You will become a pro with crutches also. I navigate in the dark, carry things in both hands without gripping the crutches with my hands and walking. Just take your time. Being a new amputee is a challenge, but in time everything will be second nature.

The theory states that behind the anonymity of the Internet, people become total jackasses.  They feel that, because they're "anonymous", anything they say can't be traced back to them, and feel that the targets of their hateful comments won't really be affected.  (Of course, some do want to cause others pain, but that's another story...)

I had to fight arrogant taunting with a moment of...violence. I am a peaceful, gentle person but was pushed too far. For us guys anyway, it isn't so easy.

author: @buddyblair8878
text: I am a recent BK amputee on 1/11/11.My choice was easy it was easy for ME.It was walk with extreme pain the rest of my life or have my foot removed(due to lack of blood flow) I had cancer as a child and the Radiation compromised my leg artery,and it finally failed on me and couldn't walk anymore,so for me it was easy keep it and be in a wheel chair or loose it and get back to life,for me it was an easy choice.you can email me at blair626@hotmail.com if you want more answers or questions.:)

author: @area51invader
text: It amazes me how many times a day STUPID people will walk up to me and ask me how I lost my leg, strangers, walmart check stand personal, waitress's, etc etc etc. I can't go anywhere without being constantly asked " how did you lose your leg" If I was out in public all day, I swear a good hour or more would be spent answering this rude question So most of the time I just stay home just to avoid that constant F*&%#@D UP question.

author: @area51invader
text: I lost my right leg above the knee when I was going on 11yo. Over 4 decades has gone by and I have very severe phantom pain at times and have very mild pain most of the time. I don't think you really get used to dealing with it... I wore a prosthesis thru out school and it was very uncomfortable to walk with. A couple years after high school hell, I moved to Colorado and finally stopped wearing the "ball & chain" after a couple years.

author: @ampfan
text: The 'fake amputee" is also a problem for devotees. I have been fooled too many times by amputee fakers who waste my time builiding a friendship with them, only for me to catch them with an inconsistancy in their story. It has become so bad, that when I find an amputee who is opening accepting of my devness, I have to ask them for a 'proof' photo. e.g. something contrived (finger on nose) to save my emotional investment in their fakery. I take my friendships seriously, and getting burned by fakers is a problem on both sides. (One amp friend even had a 'fake dev' approach her. He figured that being disabled, she'd be desperate for sexual communications).

author: @zonalklism
text: I have a question for AmputeeLife, AmputeeOT, and any other amputees who read this. Am I the only one who uses my amputation to mess with people, especially devotees? The amputees I've met irl have always taken it super seriously, where as I don't think I've ever *not* laughed or joked about it. For example, I work as a paintball ref at the local field, and when people won't follow the rules, or just keep staring, I tell them I lost my leg from a paintball shot too close. The large majority of the time they believe me, and many laughs are had. Even when the doc said amputation was a strong possibility I said, "Well, I had been trying to lose some weight."

author: @rocko10000
text: Well, if all man would say, what they think about woman, no woman would ever talk to man. Devotees still didn´t learn what is ok or what ist not ok. They grow up in a situation without a community. Gays do have community, the disabled do have a community, woman do have social discurses .... bababa ... but a devotee grows up without nothing. He feels his beeing different, but hasn´t a social network to learn to handle his feelings and intercactions. He has no network (familiy, friends or other devotees) to learn the dos and donts. He has to think through all this by himself. It´,s normal that the most of us get weird an strange in their behavior. Beeing young and exploring your own sexuality isnt the best time in a mans life. And the special body girls only read the doing-wrong. Later - when you realize you should be repectful to girl or woman, than you dont write unpolite trash. But nobody realises this improvement. The girls just see the stupid actions - but not the not-doing-it. This social group of devotees is very "young"  somehow. author: @AshleeAlyce89
text:
My foster brother was in a really horrific accident a few weeks ago and went from being a healthy, bright, energetic and hard working 22yo to being a double amputee with an aquired brain injury after letting his emotions, a fight wih his biological father, get the better of him. He got incredibly upset and chose to very stupidly jump on his ninja(road bike for those who arent familiar) without protective gear or a helmet and then reached speeds of over 250km/h, weaving through traffic like a maniac(there were A LOT of people who managed to capture videos of him because they couldnt believe how recklessly he was riding). He eventually clipped a truck, which caused the horrific accident. The crash was also caught on camera by multiple people and shared all over social media and we couldnt avoid seeing it multiple times a day along with the usual hateful comments, which caused my already traumatized family lots more trauma. 
Yes, we know that he was putting himself and others in danger and we are so incredibly grateful that he only injured himself physically. I also acknowledge the witnesses, first responders and hospital staff might not be physically harmed, but to see someone so messed up would be insanely traumatic also and we are so sorry that they had to go through that because of my brothers actions. But, i think he has been punished enough for a thousand lives. He is still in ICU, but he is healing by the second and we are so grateful that by some miracle he survived. The grief he expressed when he woke and found out he had lost his right arm above the elbow and his left leg above the knee was soul crushing and i havent known how to help comfort him, until now!! 
I cannot wait to show him your video so he can see that he can and will get through this. Thankyou so so much for sharing your story and i hope that you continue to heal and have great health going forward. Noone deserves to go through what you and my brother are right now and im so sorry that you are, but you are such an incredibly inspirational and strong person for it and i hope my brother chooses that path also. 
Sorry for the monster comment, but thankyou for taking the time to read my novel 😅🧡

author: @itspeglegmeg
text:
What a story! I am so sorry to hear what you and your family are going through at the moment, I can’t even begin to image the incredible hurt you all must feel. 

I know your brother will get through this, it’s really hard, but he will. 

I just also did a video on my TikTok called “Dear future amputee” which might be helpful to show him as well. It’s
Something I wish I had when I was in the thick of it. 

Wishing you and your family all the best! X

author: @OneLegisEnough-BAW
text: Hi Jodie, it takes time. It takes time to heal, to feel good with something on you and to trust it. Take your time and be consistent with using your leg. Everyone’s journey is different so don’t compare yourself to anyone you see. Set your goals and keep your head down until you achieve them. You’ll get there!

author: @nickynonque5482
text:
Sorry in advance - got the writing version of verbal diarrhea (writing diarrhea?? 😂😂🤭🤭🤣🤣), so it’s a bit of a long msg…. 🫤🤭…. 
Hi there 😊🫶🤩. Oh blimey! 😬 you poor thing 😢 so sorry you’re in so much pain 😢 it’s 4 weeks since my amputation and so I think I’ll be being measured etc for my 1st test prosthetic in about 5 weeks as I’ve been told that it’ll be around the 2 mth mark.  I  don’t remember the last time I was pain free, and I didn’t réalise to what extent, having the test sockets, was so painful. It’s my motto to keep smiling, and my docs, physio etc are all amazed at my change in personality for pre and post op ME 😅 I think I just decided that this is it now - either I go for it and have the chance to finally regain my life, or I sit feeling sorry for myself and turning into a miserable person with no life!!!…. And then I saw this video…. I only found you about an hour or so ago, and I will now be one of your no.1 followers!! I am, however, a below the knee amputee, left leg…. I really hope that the pain of the test prosthetics won’t set my mental attitude back… your above the knee prosthetic even looks and sounds painful and I’m so sorry that you have to endure that pain each time…. Have you had setbacks with your mental strength at all? You seem like an amazing and fabulously outgoing person, but I know that inside, you may well be saying to yourself ´aghhh, I can’t stand much more of this’…. How do u get through moments like that? Do I just keep pushing myself and obviously, as long as it’s not totally and utterly unbearable pain, just keep on going, to get the best indications of where it needs tweaking, so that the future will be a brighter place?
My bandages come off on Monday and my leg will be bare, which is slightly worrying me, but like you, I can’t wait to get back to doing what I loved and what I took for granted until 2.5 years ago when I fractured my ankle, and unfortunately contracted the bas***d MRSA (called staphylocoque doré here in france…. 
I live here, but am English)…during the operation…. the one thing top of my list is swimming!!!!!!! My God…. I have another 2 weeks to see if the MRSA has gone back to sleep (it ´woke’ back up in January and in just a few months, destroyed my muscle, nerve and skin transplant that had originally saved my ankle at the beginning, until amputation was absolutely the only option left….) I have 2 weeks left of bigtime mega antibiotics, then blood test and 🤞🤞🤞🤞 it’ll be under control and I can go and swim, swim, swim and will have to be dragged kicking and screaming, out of the pool!! 😂😂😂😂😂😂😂 (sorry, I just thought - I’m a mega breastroke swimmer - how the hell will that work??? Will I go round and round in circles??!! 🤭🤭🤭🤭🤭…. Think some more YouTubing is in order 🤭🤭😅😅) 
I’m SO SO SO sorry - I think I’ve probably written an entire novel to you here and it’s 2am here, and I’ve probably bored you back to sleep by now 😂😂 Don’t blame you!!  Sorry, I should’ve looked to see if you have posted your email address instead of writing all this here…. I don’t know anyone, who’s English speaking, and is an amputee, so I just wanted to, I don’t know, chat to someone in English for a change, and see what your perspective is. 
Thank you heaps if you’re still hanging on in there and reading away!!! ❤️🫶❤️….. take care and I hope that your pain has decreased, as I think today would be the next fitting stage, which is (hopefully) more comfortable for you 🤞🤞 Will keep tuning in to see how you’re doing… and just remember, one leg is enough 😁😁👍👍❤️❤️💪💪 have a fab day, Nicky xx

author: @RuneNorwegianFlashWillem
text: Im LBKA since 1986. I have tried several feets and have always ended up with 1C61 Triton VS from Otto Bock. This foot has a vertical shock system in the ankle that make it adorable for walking in all terrains. It is good for jogging too.  I work in kind of job that I have to walk and stand alot on my legs for hours at the time. The vertical shock system make it softer to use your amputated leg. I highly recommend this foot for the active person. This foot is also make it easier to fit a leg cover for your prostises. Leg covers from Alleles designs in Canada.

author: @thehandicaphunter
text:
I just use a jell liner.  I tried suction and didn’t like it.  I am able to flex the remaining muscle on my residual limb.  I flex the muscle inside the socket to keep it on and increase control.  I used Alpine suspension liners back when I made these videos but now I only use the suspension liners for mountain biking and going in the ocean.  Other than that I don’t use suspension liners.  

I’ve tried lots of different brands but the alpines are the best.

author: @bocelle8341
text: I noticed that when I got my All Pro, my quads were sore.  That is good news because it means I am using more muscles.  It is also a lot lighter than my old foot and can tell it when playig

author: @donreinke5863
text: No rubber foot. I would bond a hiking boot sole to the bottom of the blade. No shoe necessary either.

author: @tenglin3584
text: do you know how is this compare to the Ossur Pro-Flex XC Torsion? I'm looking at both feet and so hard to make a decision...

author: @thehandicaphunter
text:
Tricare does.  Since this video I also have a sprinting leg for the CFT, a foot that’s for cowboy boots, and a versa foot for mountain biking.  I’m still active duty so I’m pretty spoiled when it comes to getting what I need.  

Even with all these legs, I still think the AllPro is the one stop shop if you can only get one foot.  

I used to get sockets for each foot but overtime that became too cumbersome.  Now I just have one socket and I screw a different pylon and foot on when I need it.  It’s much easier that way.  The only consideration is, you have to learn to set your own alignment.

author: @davidchalupa3791
text:
So I am 65 (almost) and healthy with the exception of both ankles.  I am told after x-rays and C.T. scans that both ankles are out of alignment, the tallus is tilted outwards in the the socket and the inner point rubs on the middle area of the tibia, no space between on the left ankle and very little on the right ankle.  They tell me I am not eligible for replacement ankles and only option is to fuse them.  I will not do that yet.  I play pickleball, and compete in Senior track and field but it has gotten too painful to do short runs for the javelin and to 
spin while throwing the discus, not to mention running the 50M.  I alos enjoy hiking, biking and other outdoor activities but the ankles are limiting me.  Do you recomment amputation and movimg in this direction or should I continue to tough it out?  Dave in Nebraska USA.

author: @thehandicaphunter
text: I doubt you’ll find a doctor willing to even do it.  It’s too much of a medical mal practice liability for them.  I’m glad to be an amputee and it’s Mitch better than I was for the three years earlier,  but I also know that I’m the outlier in most amputation situations.

text:
With positive attitude and good physical therapist they can be walking within months.  There’s so many variables that will determine the recovery.  Best thing is to stay positive.  

Ossur is the best for an average person who has limited activity.  Otherwise I’d look at something more like the fillauer.

author: @AmishFury
text: Been in a ProFlex XC for about 4 years. Never been able to jog well and some gym activities are difficult, as you point out.  What size AllPro is the one you use? I'm guessing 10" maybe?  I saw another video where he commented it doesn't work well with long pants, though he had a 12".  My understanding is the 8" is the go to, but 10" if more active. I'm a tall guy so either would fit under my leg, but curious which you use. I have a script to replace my leg so I'm on deck for a new foot. I'll keep the XC since I really do like it and it's still in good shape. Much appreciated.

author: @hook-her904charters3
text: Do you have the XC or the XC torsion? How is yours when outdoors fishing and hunting?

author: @AmishFury
text: ​@hook-her904charters3My post is 4+ years old. 😀 There wasn't a torsion version at that time. But I did have an Ottobock torsion pylon instead, which was fantastic. They are more adjustable and my leg guy prefers the separate unit. It also helps that it's right under my socket as well. I can't imagine not having it. Since this post I did go with the fillauer all pro 8", and now on my second. I will say after switching from the Proflex to Allpro it takes more energy to walk. We went on a trip right after I initially got it and walked something like 28 miles in 4 days. I was so tired and leg was sore from the extra effort.  I will say for every day walking around and a little terrain, the Proflex is just a great foot. But if you do more athletics and rough woods terrain, the all pro is far superior. However, you should be in moderate physical shape. (Just my caution). Also, the fillauer foot shells are mediocre compared to the Occur, which looked almost new even after 4 years.

author: @thomasarchuletajr600
text: It’s been a year April and I’m still struggling is it normal for your doc to take your old hardware off your old socket and put it on your new socket he’s been telling me I’ll have a backup never happens told me I’d have a hydraulic ankle something to walk up hill so I can go outside I live on a hill. I can’t even walk up the drive way but I’m still waiting.  Anyway I. Just thought it was weird that he took my old hardware off my old socket. and put it on my new one wants sell one of yours

author: @thehandicaphunter
text: That’s normal pending adjustments to heigh or other types of feet.  Everyone progresses differently and no two amputations are the same.  However,  I will tell you that you should never accept a passive response.  Ask the Medical provider what conditions need to be set in order for them to provide you with the device that you specifically want. Then go out and set those conditions. Make those conditions a goal and achieve them. That way the doctor has no choice but to give you what he promised.

author: @elmaya12
text: Hi friend! I have the Ossur Proflex XC but I want to buy a new foot, but what do you recommend, fillauer all pro or Ossur flex run, I practice athletics, specilly long jump but I want a foot for long distance trainning. Thank you in advance.

author: @thehandicaphunter
text:
I use the flex run for long distance running.  I can run in the fillauer but it’s not efficient or fast.  The down side is that the flex run isn’t stable enough for agility.  

Fillauer = agility and jogging
Flex run = fast and easy running but only in a straight line.  

I hope that helps.

author: @elmaya12
text: ​@thehandicaphunter thank you so much, I have the cheetah xtreme for trainning but I want another foot for warm up, so in I think is better the Fillauer even for gym, right? Do you still use your proflex XC, or you are using the fillauer insted of? Thank you

author: @thehandicaphunter
text: Ulises Palacios I wear the fillauer when I’m in the gym, hiking, or at work (I am in the military so I need balance and agility day to day).  I wear the pro flex XC at home when I’m not doing any activity.

author: @Ucceah
text: out of curiosity: couldnt you use a smooth-running swivel instead of weights, and keep the line horizontal as it twsts up? woult make things a lot easier for folks who dont have a slace with >10' ceiling height.

author: @thehandicaphunter
text: That would work as long as you control it so the swivel only engages after you’ve wound up each side with the drill.  Also you’d need to maintain pressure on the line.  I’ve seen guys use a bunjy cord and swivel when they use the machines that spin both lines at the same time.

author: @thehandicaphunter
text: Mark Krieg , It’s 120 denier embroidery thread.  You can pick up three spoils of 500 meters for less than $20 on Amazon.  There’s no need to buy expensive spoons of tying thread from Uni or other companies.  Just make sure you get embroidery thread because it’s poly based and will have less of a chance of mold.

author: @Hawaiibohntr

author: @thehandicaphunter
text: Mark Krieg after I thread the line around the posts I slide the ring on and move it to the bottom.  Then I start to spin the line and furl each side. If that’s not clear let me know.

author: @GloriaMoreiraMusicMantras
text: Unfortunately and $6000 later, I have not had good results at all with the Bioness L300 system.  The customer support has been dismal and, I hate to post these negative reviews but, if it saves just one person from having to go through what I have been through with Bioness, it is worth it.  Once they sold me the product, they would have me go 20 miles away from where I live for adjustments.  I have disabilities and Multiple Sclerosis and this was not an easy task for me.  I finally gave up going and they refused to meet me anywhere closer for any further adjustments.  Please don't make the mistake I made, research the reviews THOROUGHLY, before buying this system.  The time frame for which to return it expired due to all the back and forth with the adjustments and they will not refund a penny.  I am still paying $355 a month for a product that did not work for me and the company is heartless - they do not care - they take your money and turn their back on you.  I am disabled with very low income and this has been a HUGE sacrifice for something I am not getting use out of.

author: @lorraine03041968
text: Below knee amputee. Touch 50 and back in the gym doing weights after a year of lapse. Thanks for the video. I love the leg press and hack squat machine as I can't go all the way down with the prosthetic I use and my form is bad so I usually hurt my back but now with the machine I get what I need out of it. I get more than my fair share of looks here in Malaysia as its uncommon mostly for a woman to be where the 'guys' hangout/train (thanks to my dad's training from early years) & more so with my prosthetic limb.  Keep the videos coming.

author: @konartis8108
text: I'm an amputee on the same side, had some trouble with good knee. phsio told me i need to train both legs separately as I just let my left leg take over with these kind of exercises. has this helped build your right quad as much as the left?

author: @ruparbo
text: Hello I have the same question. I'm soon joining the gym. My left quad are kind of bigger than my right quad which is my prosthetic leg. Can you tell me how can I train my right quad as well. Do I train them separately and how. Any suggestions. Plz reply. I really need and kind of depressed about it.

author: @hoss9615
text: sorry to hear that.  I'll pray for you.  if you ever need someone to vent on feel free to message me and ill give you a call.

author: @hoss9615
text: Thanks for getting back to me.  If you don't mind me asking what happened to go from below the knee to above the knee?  Something that I should be aware of?

author: @scottbonebrake893
text: My pleasure.  I have Chondrosarcoma its a type of bone cancer.  The cancer had decided to return in my femur, and they had to remove my knee..

author: @scottbonebrake893
text: Thanks Tim!!  I am actually an above knee amputee now, since Nov. of last year.  I just had normal Ottobock pylon parts and attachments.  I was able to get a very rigid carbon fiber foot that could handle whatever weight I could throw at it.  I used to push 1000# leg press with no problems with the leg.  I would think you could do the same, and maybe just had a defective part.  Best of luck!!  Keep pushing the weight!!

author: @hoss9615
text: Great job.  I am a below the knee amputee one year now.  I miss lifting heavy.  My leg can take the weight the issue is the connection between the Carbon fiber and the foot to take the weight.  how are you getting around the that?  I was up to 12 plates on the hack when the ring that hold the foot in place developed a stress fracture. I only know this because while walking the foot broke off.  Wasn't a pleasant fall.  lol.  Are you using special component between your bucket and your foot?

author: @jamesmassey5391
text: Isnt the reason that you, myself , and others post our accomplishments on YouTube is so others will  "LOOK AT ME,LOOK AT WHAT I CAN DO"? Well , i have already done this, except the big difference between my accomplishments and yours are that they havent been done by another amputee....MAKING MINE THE GREATEST, and therefore warranting the " LOOK AT ME, IM REALLY DOING SOMETHING SPECIAL, AND THAT NO ONE ELSE HAS". I wasnt being a dick, just jawing you a little. But i can see that you were hurt to find out that your "accomplishment" wasnt worthy of any amputee records. TRUST ME, I DONT COMPETE WITH OTHER AMPUTEES IN THE GYM, AS ANYONE CAN SEE IN MY YOUTUBE VIDEOS, I AM WELL BEYOND THAT , AND I EVEN HUMBLE THOSE THAT ARENT AMPUTEES. NOW THAT'S AN ACCOMPLISMENT WORTHY OF SHOWING THE WORLD. LET ME KNOW WHEN YO0U HIT A LEG PRESS FOR 1678#. HAVE A GREAT DAY

author: @ikonane
text:
Great video! I am also a below knee amputee that works put at the gym and I have problems finding exercises that don't hurt my stump. Do you have any other exercises other than these that you do without pain? 
Thanks!

author: @scottbonebrake893
text: These were done 7 mos. post amputation.  It took me that long to be able to push through the weight without too much pain.  I still have some bad days, where I can't do this.  You just have to listen to your body.  I also do Romanian lunges, thigh abductor, and adductor to strengthen my limb..  Hope that helps some. Feel free to reach out if I can help more..

author: @massey088283
text: Not bad , bro. I hate to bust your bubble , but you gotta look at "James Massey How Great I am " on Youtube. Im also an amputee like yourself, however, im blowing out 1678# leg presses in one of my videos , 690# hack squats , 500# free squats ( not using the bitch ass smitrh machine, the real deal) , 535# Deadlifts , etc. my claims are all on video right here in YouTube. Im on ABILITY DYNAMICS 2015 marketing campaign. keep up the efforts , I'll keep raising the bar. lol

author: @caitlinmcguire903
text: Absolutely wonderful. ❤ Sorry for what you had to go through. I just had open heart surgery from an infection keeping my valve open, had my valve replaced and they put in a pace maker. Which I call my backup generator in case I go under 50bp it will kick on. But my heart has been doing its own thing. I am happy about that. I also had to get both knees irrigated due to the bacteria spreading there. I’m healing well. They say the infection is gone, but I’m at a facility for 5 weeks to finish off extra antibiotics. I’m grateful to be alive. If I didn’t check in the hospital when I did, I would have had a few weeks left to live. It’s wonderful to hear another story and a survivor. May god bless you and keep you and your family healthy. We are all stronger than we know ❤❤❤❤

author: @StepsReimagined
text: Caitlin, we are so moved by your comment! It makes sharing our story via Steps Reimagined all the more worth it. Thank you!! I know all too well the battle of overcoming serious infections. I've been prescribed antibiotics for life. My wife and I are adding you to our prayers for a full recovery! Thanks again for the encouragement!

author: @kenedacarni7388
text:
Étant amputé en fémorale depuis septembre 2024 et après un an passé en tibiale avant de passer en fémorale, je ne peux que comprendre, et franchement j'ai envie de dire la chance de pouvoir faire du snow 😊 j'en rêve de pouvoir refaire de la glisse, malheureusement les prothèses fémorale qui peuvent me le permettre son hors de ma possibilité financière et non prises en charge 😢 
J'espère que d'ici l'hiver prochain je serai sortie de rééducation et pourrais participer à des sorties avec l'adepa pour pouvoir tester des prothèses adaptées à la glisse qui me sont inaccessibles autrement 😊
En tt cas merci de partager et de montrer qu'il ne faut jamais lâcher 🤘😌

author: @kenedacarni7388
text:
Bonsoir Sophie 😊 je découvre ta chaîne, je t'ai découvert sur le site proteor avec la synsys, c'est ta prothèse là ? Ou une autre ? 
Je viens de passer 1mois et demi avec le c-leg 4 et depuis hier je suis avec l'Orion3 de blatchford et un pied avec cheville hydraulique de chez blatchford également, j'ai appelé proteor pour tester la synsys également, je serai très intéressé d'avoir ton retour sur les prothèses que tu as pû tester ainsi que la synsys. Enfin sans vouloir déranger bien sûr 😅

author: @sophieloubet
text: @kenedacarni7388 salut ! moi j’etais sur C-Leg avant. Le Synsys ça m’a changé la vie. T’as l’impression de retrouver ta jambe, genou-cheville couplé qui fonctionne avec la même angulation que ta jambe valide, c’est vraiment un autre monde cette prothèse. Tout est plus facile, montées/descentes, plus stable, plus d’équilibre partout oú tu es, ça augmente la confiance en toi et tu verras que le faite d’avoir les deux jambes physiologiquement plus proches la charge mentale est bien diminuée. Donc moins fatiguée. Et hauteurs de talons jusqu’à 7-8 cm 😜

author: @kenedacarni7388
text: @sophieloubet   j'avoue qu'elle a l'air vraiment bien cette association genou cheville pied, là je suis en test du orion 3 + pied avec cheville hydraulique, déjà rien que là ça change vraiment tt en termes de confort et souplesse, mais vraiment envie de tester la synsys. Surtt que j'approche de la sortie du centre donc je veux tester pour pouvoir faire mon choix pour pas me retrouver bloqué ensuite avec une prothèse qui ne me donne pas un maximum de possibilités pour mon quotidien... Merci pour ton retour ☺️

Thank you for the comment!

text: Yes, It is a process to get the length on the comment before yours I left why it may appear that way. I agree though with you too. Its a work in progress lol a lot of trial and error. Also sometimes they adjust the height for different reasons to help compensate for things. I'm working on it though hopefully I cant get it more narrowed down. Thank you for your support on my journey!

author: @IDYLBERRY
text: Sorry to hear you got a bug, but you're still carrying on. Have you tried drinking apple cider vinegar? It breaks down the mucus and helps clean your gut. Sugar and dairy make mucus.  Easier to stay on top of the cleaning than to let it slide. The steam machine really does a good job. Thanks for the Mrs. Myer's tip I'll be on the lookout for it. What a nice neighbor!!! I hope you feel better soon.

author: @CountingStepswithCourtney
text: Yes, we must carry on lol, no i can't get past the smell!! Do you drink it? I know the steam machine is gold lol best purchase in a while. As we get older the things we get excited for makes me laugh. I'm excited over a steam cleaner! Oh my god, yes you must get the Mrs. Myers you won't be sorry. You will have to comment back when you get some which one did you buy!? The neighbor yes she is awesome! Talk to you soon. Hope you are doing well!

author: @IDYLBERRY
text:
@CountingStepswithCourtney Before bed if congested I have a decent swallow from a bottle of Bragg's apple cider vinegar its organic and unfiltered. Shake before using. Regular apple cider works also. Vinegar is loaded with "B" vitamins and good for gut health. Germs/viruses don't like vinegar. 
If you are going to try it; 2-3 tablespoons should be enough.
If you find it too strong for your throat dilute with water. Try and drink it from a bottle so you don't smell it, don't let it linger in your mouth, chase it with a bit of water. If you have ulcers don't drink it. Don't drink it before having coffee as you're get a cleaning, stay close to the bathroom.
I think it's a better way to go than NyQuil.

I see Mrs. Myer's is at Walmart in a few varieties. I added the vinegar base to my cart as a reminder. Will give you feedback about it.
I've been using Super Clean diluted 5 parts H2O to 1 part SC as it cuts automotive oil/dirt, but it doesn't work well of shower tiles. About $10 a gallon. My get up and go got up a went and I have to chase it down. Good hearing back from you.

author: @loriscampagnolo1770

author: @CountingStepswithCourtney
text: Hi, thank you! This is the best we could get it to pretty much. I do not have enough residual limb left for the liner. I have tried the liner and we have tweaked it time after time, unfortunately I think this is my best option currently.

author: @IDYLBERRY
text:
like cars, having machines interact with computers is asking for problems you can't resolve. 
Buying a simple machine looks like a good choice. 
Many years ago, before chips, my girlfriend would fix the washer or dryer. 
Once a sock became lodged in the water pump, she fixed it within an hour.
Don't start making bread, you might be called a gingerbread woman. If you get bored, you can go with the Peg Bundy hair style.
Your movement is good, have you tried steps? 
Yesterday I rode the train to Marin County Civic Center and cycled back 18 miles. Sat. is tree trimming.
Thanks for continuing to share yourself. Enjoy your weekend!

author: @юлия-ю5р
text: ⁠@ИгорьДонской-е7к травматическая ампутация в начале думали до колена но потом решили так отрезать,  и в 19 я стал инвалидом протезом не могу пользоваться культя болит , по этому хожу на костылях, и при прохождении комиссии я ещё доказываю что я инвалид , а наличие культи одной ноги и костылей им не достаточно 🩼.

author: @ИгорьДонской-е7к
text:
Сергей,в такой ситуации только костыли подмышки. Чтоб у вас хоть руки свободные были. Чтобы хоть что то подпирало плечи. Чтобы вы хоть могли нормально ночью встать,открыть дверь,сходить в туалет,прозаически налить себе стакан воды. Да просто зажечь сигарету,если это нужно вам,хотя в вашем положении курить не стоит. Вам же при такой высокой ампутации нужна хоть какая то свобода маневра. Насколько я понял,ногу вам оттяпали реально высоко. А протезировать такое,я так понял,сложно,если вообще возможно. Вы еще всего то 19летний парень. Только жить начинаете...
Жму вашу руку.​@юлия-ю5р

author: @юлия-ю5р
text: @ИгорьДонской-е7к я не когда не думал что останусь с одной ногой , тяжело жить без ноги  а на протезе пробывал ходить но под ним опоры не чувствую один даже раз упал сегодня фантомы были то пятка чешиться то под коленом щекотно.

author: @юлия-ю5р
text: @ИгорьДонской-е7к да отрезали очень высоко мне на комиссии сказали если у тебя было коленно тогда можно было сделать протез 🦿 а так сказали, что костыли 🩼 мне ногу заменили и они лучшие друзья

author: @ИгорьДонской-е7к
text: Понимаете,для такой девочки вопрос не в том,чтобы использовать то,что есть,и не жалеть себя. Тут важно,чтобы плечи при ходьбе что то подпирало,при такой то ампутации. А то ведь можно грохнуться,и переломать себе все на свете.

author: @egalobeinbein
text: I am used to walking with a prosthetic leg in hot weather. In my country, it can sometimes reach 40 degrees Celsius. I sweat a lot inside the silikon, and sometimes I have to take it off to dry it before putting it back on. I might go to the restroom to dry off the sweat if I can’t take it off in public.✌🏻

author: @AnnikatheAmputee
text: I used to actually have a friend in the hospital with me that was jealous how quickly my stump shrunk. And even now, I see some other people with slimmer stumps and I get that feeling of being jealous. It is weird to be jealous of something like that but I think its also understandable!

author: @feluzcampos142

author: @Caprossi
text: This is timely for me as I am having fit and alignment problems.  I’ve had prostehetic BK legs for nearly 10 months but also still have some wounds so cannot wear a leg all the time. My leg is still  changing a lot.  My prosthetist has been letting me try different liners and systems for the past few weeks.  Your comments are similar to my experiences with each. My reaction to the suction system with the outer sleeve was “I hate this!”  Your videos are always helpful. Thank you. ❤

author: @AnnikatheAmputee
text: I'm glad you find my videos helpful, but more importantly, I'm glad your prosthetist is working with you. It is a very long and stressful journey sometimes and it's good to have a good medical team who wants to work with you and not against you.

author: @TimAllen1965

author: @AnnikatheAmputee
text: Ha yeah it is a crazy tan line! Since my limb almost never sees the sun, I have to be really careful on the rare occasions it does because it burns like nothing I have ever seen before

author: @TimAllen1965
text: @AnnikatheAmputee  oh yeah, I bet it does sunburn like crazy. Keep up the great content.

author: @doriskerubo7023

author: @AnnikatheAmputee

author: @doriskerubo7023

author: @Timothy-NH

author: @brianbarnett100

author: @AnnikatheAmputee
text: I have never used a moisturizer on my stump, so unfortunately I do not! I have super sensitive skin though so I probably would use something for baby care. For instance, I wash my liner with baby shampoo because my skin is sensitive. Hope that helps!

author: @torejorgensen5344
text: I think you need to give her a bit more to work with. What is the difference? I looked at two sites making prosthetics for UK (ossur and proactiveprosthetics) which was the two first when I googled "prosthetics uk", and all the suspension methods (or something very similar) mentioned in this video plus one somewhat similar to Martin Bionics seems to be available. I'm not a prosthetics user, so there might be something obvious I've overlooked, but at least I didn't catch on to what the difference is.

author: @AnnikatheAmputee
text: I don't know much about other countries and their prostheses. I did meet a couple of service members with amputations from the UK and their prostheses were similar to mine.

author: @TomTomLLong
text: Nice explanation of each system! I use a sleeve/vacuum system and agree it is bulky and does get hot. So cool you have options!  Better every day!!👍

author: @AnnikatheAmputee

author: @GingerKiwiOnWheels
text:
Awesome one-handed wheelie! I'm still working on holding one with both hands and have done it once, though I do multiple "getting though all the construction in Toronto" wheelies every day. I hear you about cats and wheelchairs! I have a Varilte cushion (air + foam) and I hang it up every night to keep my cats from putting a claw through it. I had a soft back until about a month ago and made a chair bag that covered it to keep the claws away.

I got my first custom wheelchair last July -TiLite  titanium ZRA with purple anodized caster fork. I upgraded the casters from 1x5" to 1.5x5" with purple anodized hub. Since last fall I've had purple wheel lights - which are led dog collar plastic tube lights. They're rechargeable and water proof.

I just stumbled across your channel and subbed. 😃

author: @torejorgensen5344
text: Annika, I just realised a video I would love to see. Zack (JerryRigEverything) is moving their custom wheelchair factory "NotAWheelchair" to a new and bigger building, and they are getting a lot of new cool machines. The NotAWheelchair website is focused on their off-road solution, but they are also moving into regular custom wheelchairs and focus on making them more affordable (I know cost isn't much of a factor for you because of your military service, but it is of course important for many people). I would love a collab where Zack shows you around in their factory and chatting with you. Even though his channel is much bigger, I think it will be ok to reach out to him. He is a very nice guy, and his wife is a full time wheelchair user. They live in American Fork in Utah. I am not sure exactly where you live now, but a google search came up with Midvale in Utah? If that is correct, you are almost neighbours 😀. Anyway, I think you would enjoy meeting both Zack and his wife Cambry. and see the factory, and I for one would love to see the video.

I know you were a marine so perhaps you have muscle tone that others don't have.  But I do wonder how long you will be able to keep this method up.   But for now, good going !!

author: @Bradfordj
text: So if I go to Disney should I leave my leg off and go in my wheelchair?  Or do you still get disabled "conveniences" while wearing the prosthesis?

author: @vinc885
text:
Disney has apparently altered their DAS pass policy and many people with physical and other disabilities can't get it anymore. They have reframed it so that it is only for people with autism (developmental disabilities), which makes absolutely no sense. They definetly might need it, but many other disabilities also make waiting in line hard, especially if it's painful or they are unsteady.
They advice people to ask for help at each attraction, so you can briefly step out of the cue, but you have to find a cast member each time. It's ridiculous.
You can still try to get accommodations in advance, you have to ask early and if you are denied, you will likely not get a refund.
This is definetly not an elegant way to prevent people from abusing accommodations, if that's what they were trying to do.
Maybe you could take both, that way you don't always have to stand, but can if you need to.
Sarah Todd Hammer made a video on it.

author: @PeglegBikerPete
text: I'm almost 2 years in and I've just been cast for my third socket, changing from foam/gel & socks to a gel & pinlock for the first time

author: @kevinfox500
text:
Thirteen months post op on my left, BK. Still on socket one, and about to replace the right socket, AK four years ago. Part of it fit. Part of it being pin lock. Like ot pn the left. Despise it on the right. Have to stand to get into the socket, And I have only five muscles to pull it off with, and a fixed ankle.

But being able to walk in the park with the woman that gave me forever is worth it. To see my daughter, dad, son in law, and others who haven't seen me standing in over a year is worth the fight. Learned to walk within five months. After three years of trying to learn, and my half a foot getting injured, sidelining me a dew months, four times, and two infections. On three years. Final one Last may.

Didn't want a matched set. Halfway to Anakin just doesn't work for me. Had it taken, Cleveland Clinic. Top vascular surgeon in the country was at that location. Antibiotic drips while there, as it was a bone infection.

Same thing that cost me the right one.

Bonus was I got to see the kids as we left. Three days after losing the foot, our granddaughter arrived. Day before mother's day. Fitting as our first day as a family was 26 December, 2002. Sent her the following for her first mother's day.

Remember why I don't ask for anything for Christmas? Congratulations on ruining any chance I'll ever have of giving your mom a better mother's day present.

Happy Mother's Day.

We both left that Monday, same time.

And ky wife already has plans for the current socket. After the left one's paid off, about a year, as it has the heavy plastic liner, she wants to turn it into a table lamp. Working on an airbrush design for the Not Even Close To SkineTone colored sections. Working on an idea for the left one, in tribute to a lost surrogate daughter, taken far too soon.

author: @tyreesetjjoyner1995

It’s great to

author: @marty8639

I have had the current socket four and a half months and wear 6 ply of socks, never going past 10 or eleven before going to the next one. The liner I use and the previous 2 test sockets were 3D printed! Although I did manage to break the last test socket at the base, even though it was reinforced with fiberglass tape!

author: @toddbu-WK7L

I've been an RBKA for just over a year and had two feet in that time. For both feet, I got a carbon fiber socket just a week or two after my fitting with the check socket. The socket for the first foot had to be made twice because my prosthetist didn't leave quite enough room for the end of my tibia. Otherwise, all has gone well.

If you are looking for video ideas, how about one on balance please. I've watched most of your videos and you still seem to have great balance. My balance is awful. Do you do any exercises for balance? Is your balance getting any worse?

author: @MickAv8r
text:
I’m kinda the same way, washed my liner at the end of each day with a mild soap (lately it’s Ivory dish soap).  Now a days it’s less often but instead each day before I put it on I will take my spray bottle of 70% isopropyl alcohol and spray it down and then wipe it with a paper towel.  Then put it on.   I also use the Ossur Iceross and the general cleaning instructions actually say once or twice a week spray with alcohol.  I just do it everyday and it’s had no adverse affect on the liner but I don’t use stronger than 70% and I immediately dry the liner off as well.  Amps check with your prosthetist or liner manufacturer because I don’t think every liner will tolerate that.

I too am generally against the third party “liner cleaners” as they tend to be rather pricey solutions and being an amputee is expensive enough.  In fact it kinda looks like the most expensive product out there may be out of business, their online store is closed and social media is stagnant (thankfully)

By the way, quick tip for your audio... If you want to cut down some of the room echo while you're recording, you can hang a blanket or something directly behind your camera/microphone. Voices often bounce off hard surfaces so adding that buffer to dampen the echo can help give you a cleaner sound without having to invest in extra equipment or audio processing afterwards.

author: @1320Anne
text:
Great to see you back Annika! I'm an Above Knee Amputee and wear jeans most days. As an AKA, I have to do it the other way - instead of putting the jean leg down over the socket, I feed the foot down through the pant leg from the top. Same issue you mentioned - the bottom of the jean leg is just too narrow to fit over the top of the socket! Because the socket goes so high, I actually put my other foot into the pants first, then my prosthetis, then pull the jeans up over all of it. I didn't know if jeans would be possible originally, but once I got beyond rehab and was headed back IRL, I was tired of shorts and sweats!  Good to see more options in adaptive clothing these days.

Annika - hope to see you again in a couple of weeks - always nice to see a new video from you.  You're in CA now, but from your time in CO - any winter tips" Last year I wasn't driving and didn't have to go out much - was using the wheelchair when I did. We had our first icy day this week and it's a whole new ball game. I'm still using a walker as I continuing progressing, but Yikes. (I'm in NH)

author: @StephaninasAmputeeAdventures
text: I really like wearing jeans, so I put zippers in the side seams. Annika's jeans had the zipper on the inseam, but I thought it looked cool to have a non-invisible zipper on the side, so I ran with it...minus literal running. I've tried zippers on both seams. For me, I find it more convenient on the outer side seam.

author: @AnnikatheAmputee

I hadnt even thought about AKs and jeans but this is good information! Adaptive clothing is definitely starting to become more and more prevalent luckily! 

I will try to give my best snow tips but since I live in Southern California now, I dont have to worry about snow as much. Ill be heading to CO in a couple weeks so ill make a video then!

author: @EnglishRalph
text: If you haven’t read “An Unquiet Mind” by Kay Redfield Jameson, I can recommend it. She’s a bi-polar (she prefers “manic/depressive) clinical psychologist and if I remember correctly, set up the 1st bi-polar clinic in the US.

author: @rapidfirerob4
text: Definitely not a sad video. You're dealing with your bipolar, which is wonderful. I'm so glad the med you're taking is working well for you. It's great you're taking them regularly. I worked at a mental health agency as a teacher and have a masters in social work. I was a counselor before a teacher. You're very brave to talk about this so openly. Good for you. You should be quite proud of yourself. Sharing your journey will help others certainly. Bipolar, depression are diseases like a physical disease. You're doing what you need to do to treat it. Excellent! My mom had depression. My niece is bipolar. Most families have one or more people that have some form of mental illness. I've been an amputee for a little over a year and thankfully am doing well. I'm playing music in my band and back on the tennis court. I feel lucky as I know some amputees really struggle with it. I felt depressed before I got my prosthetic and could get back to work and doing things I love. My prosthetist came to my gig this past Saturday. This is my band: https://idlewildwest.com/ I'm on bass. Freckle on!!

author: @companymen42

The icing on the cake , was when my wife decided to leave me. I can't even begin to tell you how hard it was. I'm glad I reached out for help with my therapist.

My savings is gone and I’ll be on the streets in 2 days. And my main though is always, I could handle this IF I wasn’t an amputee. I dunno, better days for everyone I hope

I always appreciate it when people are open, like you were, because it helps erode the stigma surrounding mental health. Glad to hear things are looking up. Hang in there!

author: @ironmanepf1
